stem: The most appropriate next step is: Decision-making Capacity • Understanding, Appreciation, Reasoning, Choice • Lack of capacity typically requires two physicans State to State Law differ by state on requirements to determine capacity • Tools include the ACE (Aid to Capacity Exam) Competency is determined by courts American Bar Association website • www.americanbar.org/groups/law_aging/resources/health_care_ decision_making.html#consumer AGS Choosing Wisely Campaign: Ten Things Physicians and Patients Should Question • #1 Don’t recommend percutaneous feeding tubes in patients with advanced dementia; instead, offer oral-assisted feeding AGS Choosing Wisely • #8 Avoid using prescription appetite stimulants or high-calorie supplements for treatment of anorexia or cachexia in older adults; instead, optimize social supports, discontinue medications that may interfere with eating, provide appealing food and feeding assistance, and clarify patient goals and expectations. Case Discussion An 87 yo woman has a progressive decline in her baseline mental status. She has anorexia, constipation, intermittent nausea, and a 10-lb weight loss. More recently, she became nonverbal and was unable to ambulate at all and was using a wheelchair. She is disoriented and intermittently incontinent of urine. Case Discussion •Affect is flat. •HEENT exam normal. •Neck supple, no goiter. •Mucous membranes are dry. •Coarse breath sounds in all lung fields. •Irregularly Irregular pulse~105 bpm •Abdomen soft and not tender, BS+
choice_B: Ask a psychiatrist to evaluate her
choice_C: Call the patient’s son to get his permission

stem: What is the most likely cause of this patient’s symptoms? Apathetic Thyrotoxicosis • Older adults may not have typical symptoms • Depressed activity • Lethargy • Wtloss • Constipation • Do have wt loss and increased risk for atrial fibrillation AGS Choosing Wisely • #7 Don’t recommend screening for breast, colorectal, prostate or lung cancer without considering life expectancy and the risks of testing, over-diagnosis and overtreatment. USPSTF Aspirin to prevent CV Disease •60 yo and older: D Colorectal Screening •76-85 yo: C Mammography(*) • 75 and older: I Screen or Not Screen • Eprognosis • http://eprognosis.ucsf.edu/ • Key Question: Is life expectancy 10 years or less? Preoperative Assessment of the Elderly • Cardiac stress testing or other noninvasive imaging is unnecessary before low-risk noncardiac surgery Classic board question relates to cataract surgery • If patient has no cardiac hx and good functional status, preoperative cardiac stress testing is unnecessary for any surgery ≥ 4 METS (heavy housework, climb a flight of stairs) Elder Abuse and Neglect USPSTF (being updated) • Insufficient evidence to recommend screening AMA and Joint Commission recommend screening for elder mistreatment Identifying At-Risk Elders • Most states have Adult Protective Services (APS) • Risk factors for patient: Poor health Lower socioeconomic status History of abuse Dementia • Risk factors for perpetrator: Caregiver stress Financial dependence History of family violence Substance abuse Identifying At-Risk Elders • Cognitively impaired at greatest risk • Look for: Sedation (overmedication) Pressure sores Dehydration (Na >147) Malnutrition Fractures/Unexplained injuries

Which medication is LEAST likely to be associated with erectile dysfunction? E. Nortriptyline Causes of ED (in order) • Vascular disease (30-50%) • Neurologic Disease (including urologic surgery, 17-37%) • Medication Induced • Psychogenic (e.g. widower’s syndrome) • Hypogonadism • Endocrine Adapted from Geriatric Review Syllabus, 10thedition)

An 82 yo woman admitted to your nursing home has a urine culture that grew >100,000 e. coli. You should prescribe antimicrobials if: AGS Choosing Wisely #5 Don’t use antimicrobials to treat bacteriuria in older adults unless specific urinary tract symptoms are present. Urge Incontinence (Overactive Bladder) • Most common type of urinary incontinence • Signs and symptoms: – Abrupt urgency – Frequency – Nocturia – Volume of leakage may be large or small Drug Treatment of Overactive Bladder • Antimuscarinics (oxybutynin IR and ER, tolterodine, darifenacin, trospium, fesoterodine, solifenacin) • ___=less risk of side effects • Beta 3 agonists (e.g. Mirabegron) • Pelvic Muscle Exercises and Bladder Training High Yield Boards Urinary Pearls • Weight loss effective for stress incontinence • Check PVR in men before starting meds for incontinence (prostate/BPH) • Prompted voiding most effective in severe cognitive impairment Answers 1. D 6. C 2. B 7. D 3. A 8. C 4. D 9. C 5. C
choice_A: She has dark and malodorous urine.
choice_C: She has frequency, urgency, dysuria, or suprapubic pain.

stem: Which of the following pharmacokinetic factors changes least with age?

An 80 yo hypertensive woman is switched from atenolol to propranolol to treat tremor. A week later she seems more bradycardic, confused, and despondent. The most likely cause is: Changes with Aging Absorption-minimal change due to just age • Could be affected by other meds Distrubution-increase fat/water ratio, decrease plasma protein • Fat soluble drugs with wider VofD; more free drug NOT bound to albumin Metabolism-decrease liver mass; Phase 1 especially affected • Phase 2 metabolism minimally affected Elimination-Mainly renal; serum Cr not a reliable estimator • Calculate CrCl

An 84 yo man has been less alert and his balance is deteriorating. He was taking 5 mg of diazepam twice a day and agrees to stop. One week later, he’s no better. Three weeks later he’s improved. The most likely explanation is: Metabolism • Active metabolites may account for toxicity • Phase 1 (oxidation, cytochrome P450) reduced with age; Example: diazepam • phase 2 (conjugation) usually unaffected Example: lorazepam
choice_D: Patient must have continued taking the diazepam

stem: Which pharmacokinetic factor changes the most with age? Who has lower creatinine clearance? • 50 yo woman weighing 150 lbs., serum creatinine 2.2 mg/dL OR • 80 yo woman weight 110 lbs., serum creatinine 1.4 mg/dL Cockroft-Gault Equation (Weight in kg) (140 – age) ________________________ x (0.85 if female) (72) (serum creatinine in mg/dL) 50 yo = 33 cc/min 80 yo = 23.6 cc/min Serum Creatinine Does Not Reflect Creatinine Clearance

An 80 yo on warfarin has an INR of 2.5. The best explanation for increased bleeding risk compared with a younger patient is: Pharmacodynamics • Pharmacokinetics: What the body does to the drug. Pharmacodynamics: What the drug does to the body.* • Elderly are at greater risk for bleeding because: • Friable stomach tissue • Increased fall risk • Increase cerebral bleeding risk AGS Choosing Wisely #9: Don’t prescribe a medicine without conducting a drug review Resources • Beers Criteria (updated 2019 by AGS) • START/STOPP Lists • Anticholinergic Burden Scale (and others) • Polypharmacy=5 or more medications Beers’ Pearls • First-generation antihistamines • Clonidine • Amiodarone (unless afib and CHF/LVH) • Digoxin > 0.125 mg daily • Benzodiazepines • Sulfonylureas • TCA’s • PPI’s
75 yo female is seeing you for urinary symptoms. CrCl is <30. Which antibiotic is contraindicated? Beers’ Pearls • Avoid H2 blockers if patient delirious • Avoid Nitrofurantoin if CrCl<30

A 92 yo male sees you for regular DM follow up. The patient’s labs show A1C of 6.9, hemoglobin of 10.2, and creatinine of 0.9. Otherwise, labs are unremarkable. Medications include metformin, ASA 81mg, glipizide, and empagliflozin. Regarding his A1C, you counsel that: AGS Choosing Wisely #3: Avoid using medications other than metformin to achieve hemoglobin A1c<7.5% in most older adults; moderate control is generally better. Time to Benefit of Tight Control • >8 yrs before microvascular complications • e.g. renal and opthamologic • **vs. 2-3 years for BP and lipids** • Poor life expectancycloser to A1C of 9% • Life expectancy >10 yrscloser to 7.5%
choice_A: Great job, continue the good work.
choice_B: Emphasize the importance of diet and exercise

stem: An 89yo with hx HTN, CAD, and R hip replacement sees you to get a prophylactic antibiotic prior to a dental extraction? Which is the best course of action? ADA • https://www.ada.org/resources/research/science-and-research- institute/oral-health-topics/antibiotic-prophylaxis • Patients with prosthetic joints do not require abx prophylaxis without other immunocompromising concerns. • Reasonable in prosthetic cardiac valvues, hx of infective endocarditis, and some congenital heart abnormalities. Drugs Impairing Cognition (just a few) • Anticholinergics (eg, diphenhydramine, trihexyphenidyl, oxybutynin) • Anticonvulsants (phenytoin, gabapentin, valproate) • Muscle relaxers (carisoprodol [eg, Soma], cyclobenzaprine) • Antiemetics (promethazine, metoclopramide) • Digoxin, clonidine, amantadine, amiodarone • Benzodiazepines, antipsychotics AGS Choosing Wisely #4 Don’t use benzodiazepines or other sedative-hypnotics in older adults as first choice for insomnia, agitation or delirium. •Increased falls, MVA’s, hip fractures JNC-8 Adults 60 and older • BP goal less than 150/90 SPRINT trial • More aggressive treatment of blood pressure (<120 systolic) associated with better cardiovascular outcomes. • AKI, syncope, hypotension, electrolyte abnormalities more common in intensive treatment • Most patients <75 years old; • Excluded diabetics, those with previous strokes, and institutionalized elderly (Note: BP measured after sitting in a quiet room) Bottom line Life expectancy is key Frailty Syndrome (3 or more of the following) • Weakness • Slowed walking speed • Low physical activity • Low energy/exhaustion • Wtloss. Physical activity/therapy is always the right answer A 76 yo pt dx with dementia sees you for ED f/u after being seen for compression fx. Since then she is more confused, has loose stools, and temp is 100.6. Your exam reveals hyperreflexia and mild tremor. Home meds include sertraline, trazodone, and atorvastatin. Which medication prescribed by the ED likely is exacerbating her symptoms?
Which medicine is exacerbating her symptoms?

Which of the following is proven to be protective against Dementia? Prevention of Dementia • Emerging evidence for: • Diet • Education • Physical activity • Decreased mid-life cardiovascular risk factors https://www.who.int/mental_health/neurology/dementia/guidelines_risk_reduction/en/ AGS Choosing Wisely #6 don’t prescribe cholinesterase inhibitors for dementia without periodic assessment for perceived cognitive benefits and adverse gastrointestinal effects. Cholinesterase inhibitor side effects • GI symptoms, bradycardia, insomnia, headache, dizziness Answers 1. A 8. B 2. C 9. B 3. B 10. C 4. D 5. D 6. B 7. D

A 38-year-old female patient presents with pain in her R calf of 3 days duration. She had a lap cholecystectomy 8 weeks ago and a cervical conization for carcinoma-in-situ 6 years ago with no recurrence. No one in her family ever had a DVT. She has 2+ bilateral pitting ankle edema. Your choice of an initial diagnostic study would be: Diagnosing DVT • D-dimer (ELISA) – Cut off <500 mcg/L • Consider age x 10 for >50 years old • Increase in false positive with age – 95% sensitive, 40-60% specific – 95% negative predictive value – Excellent test to rule out, not so good to rule in! Schouten HJ, et al. Diagnostic accuracy of conventional or age adjusted D-dimer cut-off values in older patients with suspected venous thromboembolism: systematic review and meta-analysis. BMJ2013;346:f2492 SORT B J Am CollCardiol. 2017;70(19):2411 Diagnosing DVT • Normal D-dimer and low probability Well’s Score= DVT unlikely • Moderate/ High prob Wells score: get U/S (D-dimer NOT helpful) • Venous compression ultrasounds have 94% PPV • If negative and high clinical suspicion, repeat US at 5-7 days • Best sensitivity and specificity in symptomatic pts with proximal thrombosis of lower extremities • Limitations: new vs old clot, isolated pelvic clot can be missed tumor/ abscess false + Am Fam Physician.2012Nov15;86(10):913-919 SORT C If VTE Is Diagnosed, Who Needs… • Screening for malignancy? • Screening for thrombophilia? Screening for Malignancy • Cancer risk = 1.3x expected • Complete H&P (with rectal and pelvic exams) • CBC, LFTs, CXR, stool guaiac • Aggressive cancer work-up not necessary or cost-effective – Cancer usually makes its presence known prior to VTE – Stage 4, myeloproliferative, lungs, ovary, brain Just do age-appropriate cancer screening- SOR A Am Fam Physician.2022;105(4):377-385 Screening for Thrombophilia Don’t routinely screen for thrombophilia- SOR A • Initial clot prior to age 50 • Family history of VTE • Recurrent clot • Unusual vascular beds (non-extremity site) • Warfarin-induced skin necrosis • Testing- ideally before starting coumadin or DOAC – Protein C, protein S, fibrinogen, antithrombin III, factor V Leiden, lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin antibody, prothrombin 20210 – Prot C/S and ATIII can be inaccurate with active clot Am Fam Physician.2022;105(4):377-385

A 63-year-old man presents with a history of several days of shortness of breath with exertion and pleuritic chest pain. He suffered a DVT 5 years ago. His pO = 85% on RA and his HR is 2 110. He recently returned from vacation in Japan. What is the best test for initial evaluation of this patient’s symptoms? Diagnosis of PE • Symptoms and physical findings are very nonspecific • Likewise, EKG and CXR findings not specific • Arterial blood gases/pulse oximetry lack sensitivity • Normal SpO2 does not rule out PE Modified Well’s Criteria for PE Clinical Feature Score Symptoms of DVT 3 Other Dxless likely 3 HR > 100 1.5 Immobilization or surgery in past 4 wks 1.5 Previous DVT or PE 1.5 Hemoptysis 1 Malignancy with txwithin 6 mosor palliative 1 Low Risk Intermediate Risk High Risk <2 2-6 >6 Diagnosis of PE • D-dimer (ELISA) – Normal D-dimer with low pretest probability (<2) rules out PE Diagnostic Imaging • Computed Tomography Angiography – High sensitivity and specificity (99% NPV) – Test of choice if: • Low pretest probability and positive D-Dimer • Moderate pretest probability • High pretest probability – Preferred over pulmonary angiogram due to angio risks: • 2% mortality • 5% morbidity Am Fam Physician.2012Nov15;86(10):913-919 SORT C Diagnostic Imaging: V/Q Scanning VQ Scan Result Pretest Probability Interpretation NORMAL ANY PE excluded LOW probability LOW PE excluded HIGH probability HIGH PE confirmed – All other combinations require angiography or other imaging – Imaging of choice in patients with contraindications to CT • Contrast allergy • Renal disease • Pregnancy (VQ test of choice if only full dose CT scan available)
choice_B: Compression US of the lower extremities

stem: A 48-year-old female presents with 3 days of left lower extremity swelling. Venous Dopplers reveal a proximal DVT. She is stable and has no other medical problems. What would be the best initial treatment for this patient? (Xarelto) as outpatient therapy Treatment of VTE ACCP Guidelines • DOAC preferred for VTE when GI cancer not present • Rivaroxaban (Xarelto) or Apixaban (Eliquis)- no bridge • Dabigatran (Pradaxa) or Edoxaban (Savaysa)- requires lovenox bridge • Increased time in the therapeutic window, less major bleeding, patient convenience • Dose adjust DOACs for renal disease • LMWH with concurrent warfarin is still acceptable, just not preferred Stevens, Scott, Woller, Scott, et al. Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE Disease: CHEST Guideline and Expert Panel Report. CHEST 2021; 160 (6): 545-608 Treatment of VTE- ACCP Guidelines • Reversal of DOACs • Antidotes now available for some • Charcoal • Hemodialysis • Cancer-associated thrombosis - DOACs recommended over LMWH except for GI luminal cancers, then choose LMWH - Warfarin 3rd line- more recurrences and bleeding Stevens, Scott, Woller, Scott, et al. Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE Disease: CHEST Guideline and Expert Panel Report. CHEST 2021; 160 (6): 545-608 Acute Isolated Distal DVT- can skip anticoagulation • If no severe sx and low risk for extension, then serial U/S x 2 wks rather than anticoagulation • Risk factors for extension - Strongly positive D-dimer - >5 cm thrombus - Thrombus close to proximal veins - No reversible provoking factors - Active cancer - History of VTE - Inpatient status Stevens, Scott, Woller, Scott, et al. Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE Disease: CHEST Guideline and Expert Panel Report. CHEST 2021; 160 (6): 545-608 SORT A Treatment of VTE ACCP Guidelines • Outpatient treatment with DOACs is safe and cost effective if: – Ambulatory and stable- no O2, IV ABX, or IV pain control – Low risk of bleeding – No renal insufficiency – Reliable patient and system to monitor complications • Criteria for inpatient treatment – Massive DVT – Symptomatic PE – High risk for bleeding – Comorbid conditions Stevens, Scott, Woller, Scott, et al. Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE Disease: CHEST Guideline and Expert Panel Report. CHEST 2021; 160 (6): 545-608 SORT A Duration of Treatment of VTE ACCP Guidelines • The first three months is high-risk period for extension/ embolization • Treatment duration 3 months for provoked DVT/PE transient risk factors: surgical or non-surgical, COVID -Hematology Choosing Wisely Campaign 3+months if unprovoked DVT/PE (chronic risk factors) -long term if low or moderate risk of bleeding (Grade 2B) -if stopping tx at 3mo, start ASA if no contraindications Indefinite if recurrent DVT/PE Stevens, Scott, Woller, Scott, et al. Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE Disease: CHEST Guideline and Expert Panel Report. CHEST 2021; 160 (6): 545-608 Wilbur, J, Shian, B. Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Embolism: Current Therapy. Am Fam Physician.2017;95(5):295-302 Catheter- Directed Thrombolysis of DVT • Adjunct to anti-coagulation to reduce clot burden faster and prevent valve damage • Less chronic venous insufficiency • Less post-thrombotic syndrome • Iliac and proximal femoral vein DVT have greatest benefit • Risk: bleeding including intracranial bleeding • RCTs needed- mostly observational and retrospective studies Fleck D, AlbadawiH, Shamoun F, KnuttinenG, Naidu S, OkluR. Catheter-directed thrombolysis of deep vein thrombosis. Cardiovasc DiagnTher. 2017 Dec;7(Suppl 3):S228-S237. Treatment of VTE ACCP Guidelines • Early ambulation is suggested instead of prolonged bedrest • Hematology Choosing Wisely Campaign • Compression stockings are no longer recommended • no evidence of benefit for recurrent DVT or thrombophlebitis Stevens, Scott, Woller, Scott, et al. Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE Disease: CHEST Guideline and Expert Panel Report. CHEST 2021; 160 (6): 545-608 Wilbur, J, Shian, B. Deep Venous Thrombosis and Pulmonary Embolism: Current Therapy. Am Fam Physician.2017;95(5):295-302 Treatment of PE ACCP Guidelines • Consider systemic thrombolysis if hypotensive (SBP <90) • Start anticoagulation before diagnosis is confirmed • Consider IVC filter if anticoagulants contraindicated • Remove after treatment window to decrease recurrent DVT • 80% can be successfully removed- if left in: increased DVT, less PE Treatment of PE ACCP Guidelines • Sub-segmental PE – can skip anticoagulation if: • No proximal pulmonary artery involvement • No proximal DVT in the legs • Low risk for recurrent VTE Clinical surveillance recommended over anticoagulation Stevens, Scott, Woller, Scott, et al. Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE Disease: CHEST Guideline and Expert Panel Report. CHEST 2021; 160 (6): 545-608 SORT A
choice_B: Low-molecular-weight heparin for 5 days and Rivaroxaban
choice_C: Hospitalization for unfractionated heparin and warfarin

stem: A 28-year-old pregnant female, G3P2, is diagnosed with a DVT in her right lower extremity. The treatment of choice for this patient would be: VTE in Pregnancy  VTE risk increases 5-fold in pregnancy  Risk 1/1,600 pregnancies  PE is a leading cause of preg-associated mortality/ morbidity  20-50% have an underlying thrombophilia  History of VTE: 3-4 x increased risk for recurrence during preg VTE in Pregnancy • Risk of VTE overall, antepartum = postpartum and occurs with equal frequency throughout all trimesters • PE is more common in the postpartum period, especially 1st wk • D-dimer less useful in pregnancy (almost always positive) • Lower dose chest CTPA safe in pregnancy – If not available, do CXR – If CXR is normal, do V/Q scan – If CXR is abnormal, do full dose CT scan Anticoagulation in Pregnancy • Heparin and LMWH do not cross placenta and are safe in pregnancy SORT B • Unfractionated Heparin (UH) – Dosage requirements increase due to increases in heparin binding proteins, plasma volume, renal clearance and degradation by the placenta • Low-Molecular-Weight Heparin (LMWH) – Less bleeding, osteoporosis and HIT in non-pregnant pts compared to UH – Longer half-life – longer dosage intervals, but increased risk if epidural/spinal – Epidural anesthesia 12 hours after last dose of LMWH SORT C ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 196. ObstetGynecol2018;132(1):p e1–e17. Anticoagulation in Pregnancy • Warfarin- not for use in pregnancy – Crosses the placenta – Associated with harmful fetal effects – Use postpartum or in some patients with mechanical heart valves • DOACS not adequately studied in pregnancy • All anticoagulants are safe for breastfeeding mothers • LMWH and coumadin with good data • DOACs- limited data ACOG Bulletin #196-ObstetGynecol2018;132(1):p e1–e17. SORT B Anticoagulation in Pregnancy • Convert from LMWH to UH for last month of pregnancy • Compression stockings until anticoagulation resumed postpartum • Restart anticoagulation after delivery – 4-6 hours post vaginal delivery – 6-12 hours post C-section • May be bridged to warfarin or DOACs postpartum • Avoid paradoxical thrombosis and skin necrosis from early anti- protein C effect of warfarin by bridging with LMWH or UFH for 5 days and INR 2-3 x 2 days ACOG Bulletin #196-ObstetGynecol2018;132(1):p e1–e17. SORT B. VTE and COVID in pregnancy • If hospitalized, VTE prophylaxis while hospitalized continue for 2 weeks after discharge • No data on those managed as outpatients • National Institutes of Health COVID-19 Treatment Guidelines • ACOG: COVID-19 FAQs for Obstetrician-Gynecologists, Obstetrics Contraception if history of VTE • Avoid Estrogen • Use of progesterone is controversial • NIH: ok with first DVT, but not if recurrent • Depo Provera may have the highest risk but not well studied

A 48-year-old female with rheumatoid arthritis presents to your office complaining of two months of fatigue, and more recently dyspnea on exertion. Her Hgb=9.2 with normocytic, normochromic indices. You order iron studies, which show low iron, low TIBC and high ferritin. The best treatment for this patient would be: 12 Kinetic Approach to Anemia • Two reasons for anemia: – Decreased RBC production (reticulocyte < 2) • Lack of substrate • Marrow disorders/suppression • Decreased trophic hormones • Chronic illness/inflammation – Increased RBC loss • Hemolysis – inherited or acquired (reticulocyte > 2) • Blood loss – occult or obvious Morphologic Approach to Anemia • RBC indices – reflect cell size – Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) – Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) – Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC) – Red cell Distribution Width (RDW) • Use the RBC indices to direct your work-up of anemia Morphologic Approach to Anemia • Macrocytic (MCV >100) – Reticulocytosis (high RDW) – B and folate deficiencies 12 • PPIs and metformin can cause B12 deficiency – Myelodysplasia Courtesy: Wikimedia Commons – Alcohol abuse, liver disease, hypothyroidism Morphologic Approach to Anemia • Microcytic Anemia (MCV <80) – Iron deficiency – Decreased heme synthesis • lead, sideroblastic anemia – Decreased globin synthesis By Dr Graham Beards (Own work) [CC BY-SA 3.0 (thalassemia, hemoglobinopathy) (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0)], via Wikimedia Commons – Chronic illness/inflammation (uncommon) Morphologic Approach to Anemia • Normocytic, normochromic anemia – Acute blood loss – Acute hemolysis (retic >2) – Hypersplenism – Anemia of chronic disease (most common) Iron Deficiency Anemia • Low iron, high iron binding capacity, low ferritin • Identify WHY the patient is iron deficient! – Menstrual blood loss – GI malignancy/blood loss • Response to iron therapy: • Retic count 5-7 days • Hct 4-6 weeks • Iron stores 4-6 months *Low dose iron (15mg elemental iron)- effective, less side effects- SOR C Am Fam Physician.2018;98(7):437-442

A 3-year-old patient is brought to see you after moving to your community. He has Hgb SS disease. The parents ask you whether he needs to continue taking the prophylactic penicillin prescribed by another physician. You should recommend: Sickle Cell Disease • Most common single gene disorder in African-Americans • Diagnosed by hemoglobin electrophoresis • Due to homozygous abnormal hemoglobin S (SS disease) – Sickle SC disease (less severe disease, more retinal disease) – Sickle-Thal (variable) • Hemoglobin S is poorly soluble when deoxygenated and forms polymers that deforms the cells Sickle Cell Disease • Clinical Manifestations – Mod-severe anemia (hgb <8) w/ high retic count, normal/ high MCV – Vaso-occlusive crises – muscular, CVA, renal, priapism, retinopathy • Tx: IV narcotics if severe pain (SOR-B), NSAIDs if mild-moderate pain – Infections – pneumococcus, hemophilus, salmonella – Aplastic crisis – parvovirus B19 marrow suppression – Acute chest syndrome – pneumonia + infarct – Splenic sequestration crisis – acute anemia Sickle Cell Disease • Infection Prophylaxis – Immunizations • Strep pneumonia (PCV13 and 20) • Neisseria meningitides (Menactra and Men-B) • H. influenza, Type B • Hepatitis B • Influenza – Penicillin prophylaxis (for all types of SSD) • Pen V p.o. 125 mg BID from age 3 month to 3 years • Pen V p.o. 250 mg BID from age 3 to 5 years • After age 5 is controversial Sickle Cell Disease Annual Screening with Transcranial Doppler Ultrasound to evaluate risk of stroke age 2-16 years (SORT A) Bone Marrow/ Stem Cell Transplant-potentially curative Hydroxyurea: age 9-42 months or adults who have 3 or more crises in 12 months decrease vaso-occlusive effects and risk of acute chest syndrome (SORT B) Disease modifying therapy (less VOC, ACS, mortality, transfusion, hosp) Increases levels of HbF Initiation requires contraceptive counseling, CBC and reticulocyte q4w Risks: neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, gets into breast milk, teratogen Am Fam Phys 2015 Dec 15; 92(12): 1069-1076 Multiple Myeloma • Incidence: 1.6% of all cancer, 10% of hematologic cancer in U.S. • Risk factors: age >65, African Americans • Signs/ sx: anemia (73%) bone pain (58%) elevated creatinine (48%) hypercalcemia (28%) N/V, weakness, recurrent infections, weight loss Multiple Myeloma Dx: abnormal plasma cells in marrow (immature, atypical) monoclonal protein in serum/ urine bone lesions Work Up: • Labs: CBC w/ diff, Cr, LDH, beta-2 macroglobulin, immunoglobulin studies • Skeletal survey • BM biopsy AM Fam Physician. 2017 Mar 15:95(6): 373-383A. Multiple Myeloma Treatment: • Primary care: Bisphosphonate txwhen first diagnosed DVT prevention-DOAC or LMWH Infection prevention • Prophylactic ABX (TMP/ SMZ + quinolones 1st3 moof tx) • PCN for recurrent pneumococcal infections • Vaccines: PCV, Hib, flu IVIG for serious infections Avoid nephrotoxic meds Nutrition support Pain control • Referral to oncology: chemo, stem cell transplant AM Fam Physician. 2017 Mar 15:95(6): 373-383A. Hemophilia • Inherited bleeding disorders – Hemophilia A – Factor VIII deficiency – Hemophilia B – Factor IX deficiency (Christmas Disease) • X-linked recessive – predominantly males affected • Become symptomatic within first two years of life – Only 50% have bleeding with circumcision – 3-5% have bleeding in perinatal period Hemophilia • Bleeding sites – Muscles – Hematuria – Gastrointestinal – Epistaxis and oral cavity – Joints • Late joint destruction • Joints preserved by early initiation of factor concentrate treatments Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura “ Pentad” • Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia • Thrombocytopenia usually with purpura • Acute renal insufficiency (HUS) • Neurological symptoms (TTP) • Fever • Actually rare for all five to be present Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura • Causes – Usually idiopathic – ADAMTS13 deficiency • Protease of VWF – inhibitory auto-antibody • Long polymers of VWF attract platelets – Shiga-toxin from E. coli 0157:H7 – Collagen vascular diseases, cancer and transplants – Medications • Ticlopidine, clopidogrel, quinine, mitomycin, tacrolimus • Treatment – plasma exchange therapy curative Leukemia • Acute Leukemia – Acute Lymphoblastic – Children • Fever, bleeding, musculoskeletal symptoms, • Hepatosplenomegaly (75%), lymphadenopathy (60%) – Acute Myelogenous –Adults • Fever, fatigue, weight-loss, bleeding problems • Anemia, thrombocytopenia • Rouleaux formation on smear Leukemia • Chronic Leukemias – Adults • Most asymptomatic at time of diagnosis • Discovered with leukocytosis on blood count • Work Up: repeat CBC, smear, flow cytometry, onc referral • Chronic lymphocytic leukemia – 50% • Hepatosplenomegaly and lymphadenopathy • Chronic myelogenous leukemia – 20% • Splenomegaly Am Fam Physician.2014;89(9):731-738 Board Frequent Fliers • D Dimer only helpful for low pretest probability • if not CTPA for PE and doppler for DVT • DOAC preferred over coumadin (LMWH if cancer) • Dose adjust DOACs with renal disease • No coumadin in pregnancy (DOACs with insufficient data) • Anemia of Chronic DZ: treat underlying DZ • Macrocytic anemia- from PPIs due to B12 deficiency • Sickle Cell: transcranial U/S screening • Multiple Myeloma: start bisphosphonate tx • TTP pentad: fever, anemia, thrombocytopenia, AKI, neuro sx • Acute Leukemia: fever, bleeding • Chronic Leukemia: asymptomatic, elevated WBC-work up: repeat WBC, smear, flow cytometry, oncreferral Answers 1. A 2. D 3. A 4. C 5. D 6. C
choice_A: Stop the penicillin to avoid antibiotic resistance
choice_B: Take penicillin V 125 mg daily for the rest of his life
choice_C: Take penicillin V 250 mg BID until age 5 at least
choice_D: Take penicillin V 250 mg daily until age 12

stem: A 3-year-old girl presents with 4-day history of fever without other symptoms. Ears, throat, lungs and abdominal exams are normal, but she appears lethargic. What should be your next step? Urinary Tract Infection • Most common serious bacterial infection in children< 3 years (look for it when there are no obvious sources) • 70-90% E. coli • Up to 7% of girls and 2% of boys will have a UTI by age 6 • Urine culture needed: catheter or suprapubic tap Veauthier B, Miller MV. Urinary Tract Infections in Young Children and Infants: Common Questions and Answers. Am Fam Physician. 2020 Sep 1;102(5):278-285. Treatment of Urinary Tract Infection • Diagnosis - UA with pyuria + positive culture > 50,000 CFU/mL • Treatment - Cefixime, amoxicillin/clavulanate or trimethoprim- sulfamethoxazole for 7-14 days (afebrile UTI can be 2-4 days) • Follow-up renal ultrasound If first febrile UTI < 2 years of age, or Recurrent UTIs (more than 1) • Follow up with Voiding CystoUrethroGram (VCUG) If US reveals hydronephrosis/scarring, or Recurrent febrile UTIs (more than 1)
choice_A: Treat fever and reevaluate her in 24 hours

stem: A 16-year-old female patient is evaluated for a 4-day history of sore throat, nonproductive cough and rhinorrhea. She is afebrile, and exam reveals enlarged erythematous tonsils without exudate, and no lymphadenopathy. What is the next step? Modified Centor Criteria for Group A Streptococcus Pharyngitis Criteria Points Total Management Temperature > 38 degrees C 1 0-1 No testing or treatment Absence of cough 1 (can consider test at 1) Swollen, tender anterior cervical nodes 1 Tonsillar swelling and/or exudate 1 2-3 Test with rapid antigen Age detection testing, treat if 3-14 1 positive (culture if 15-44 0 negative in kids) 45+ -1 4-5 No testing, treat Common Questions About Streptococcal Pharyngitis. Am Fam Physician.2016 Jul 1;94(1):24-31. Pharyngitis Viral: most common Group A β-hemolytic streptococcus: 15% Poststreptococcal illness • Rheumatic fever – treat with NSAIDs • Glomerulonephritis – gross hematuria, hypertension and edema • Reactive arthritis • Pediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorders Associated with Streptococcal Infections Treatment with penicillin/amoxicillin for 10 days (macrolide or clindamycin if pcn allergic) - prevents rheumatic fever but not glomerulonephritis ***2012 guidelines from the Infectious Diseases Society of America (IDSA) indicate that GABHS infection is uncommon in children younger than three years – recommend against testing in this age group Peritonsillar & Retropharyngeal Abscess Symptoms • Severe throat or neck pain • High fever • Poor oral intake due to dysphagia Physical • Painful cervical lymphadenopathy • Uvular deviation • “Hot potato” muffled voice with trismus, drooling • Elevated WBC with a left shift Treatment: surgical drainage, IV antibiotics, ?steroids Galioto NJ. Peritonsillar Abscess. Am Fam Physician. 2017 Apr 15;95(8):501-506. Epiglottitis • Rare since Haemophilus influenzae vaccine • Can visualize swollen, cherry red epiglottis • X-ray shows “thumb print” sign • Airway management • Antibiotic treatment Cephalosporin +/- clindamycin • Steroids and racemic epinephrine are not used Viral Upper Respiratory Infections • OTC cough/cold medications NOT recommended under the age of 4 • Treatment Honey (over age of 1) Nasal saline irrigation Vapor rub (over age of 2) Analgesics • Don’t use antibiotics DeGeorge KC, Ring DJ, Dalrymple SN. Treatment of the Common Cold. Am Fam Physician. 2019 Sep 1;100(5):281-289. PMID: 31478634. Lymphadenopathy • 50% of healthy children have palpable lymphadenopathy at some point • Management: • Watchful waiting up to 4 weeks (in absence of red flags). • Oral antibiotics can be considered if inflammation is evident. • If lymphadenopathy persists or other red flags are present, evaluate with CBC/smear, ESR, CXR • Best initial imaging for neck masses: Under age 14 - ultrasound Over age 14- CT • FNA if persists Gaddey HL. Riegel AM. Unexplained Lymphadenopathy: Evaluation and Differential Diagnosis. Am Fam Physician. 2016 Dec 1;94(11):896-903. Mouth Sores Herpetic gingivostomatitis Cause: HSV Prodrome: fever, LAD Treatment: Oral acyclovir Herpes labialis Cause: HSV Prodrome: Tingling, burning, itching Treatment: Oral/topical acyclovir Mouth Sores Herpangina Cause: Coxsackie virus Prodrome: fever, headache, sore throat Treatment: Symptomatic Aphthous ulcers (canker sores) Cause: unknown, ? virus Prodrome: burning, itching Treatment: Symptomatic/topical steroids Usatine RP Tinitigan R. Nongenital herpes simplex virus. Am Fam Physician. 2010 Nov 1;82(9):1075-82.
choice_C: Treat with oral penicillin for 10 days

stem: A 2-year-old child is brought to the emergency department with a barking cough, stridor that worsened tonight, and a temperature of 101º F. What is the treatment? Croup (Laryngotracheobronchitis) • Viral illness causing edema of upper airways • Etiology Parainfluenza viruses 1, 2, 3, cause 75% Adenovirus, rhinovirus Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) Influenza A and B • Symptoms: URI symptoms, “barky” cough, hoarseness, tachypnea, mild stridor—worse at night • X-rays show subglottic narrowing (“steeple” sign) in 40-50% • No testing recommended to confirm diagnosis Treatment of Croup • Dexamethasone 0.15 - 0.6 mg/kg IM (or oral) reduces hospitalization rates and shortens ED stay Single dose (multiple dose may lead to bacterial or fungal infections) Indicated for croup of ANY severity • Moderate to severe croup (cough with stridor and retractions): Nebulized racemic epinephrine Followed by admission or at least 4-hr observation Still give dexamethasone • Beta-agonist bronchodilators NOT effective Smith DK,McDermott AJ,Sullivan JF.Croup: Diagnosis and Management. Am Fam Physician.2018 May 1;97(9):575-580.

9-month-old child is admitted in January for cough, wheezing, feeding, and fever of 38ºC. CXR shows mild peribronchial cuffing. Which treatment is indicated? Bronchiolitis • Most common cause of bronchiolitis and pneumonia in infants under 1 year – respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) • Rhinorrhea, pharyngitis, cough, wheezing, rhonchi, rales, fever and WBC inconsistent • Diagnosis: clinical (antigen detection assays only if in question) • Treatment is supportive (oxygen, acetaminophen, fluids, nebulized hypertonic saline) – no bronchodilators!! • No evidence for steroids, antibiotics, or ribavirin • Palivizumab (monthly) or nirsevimab (once) should be given to infants born <29 weeks or <32 weeks with a chronic lung condition during RSV season Clinical Practice Guideline: The Diagnosis, Management, and Prevention of Bronchiolitis. Shawn L. Ralston, et al. Pediatrics2014. Pertussis • Admission for most children < 6 months • Apnea (50% of patients), pneumonia (20%), seizures (1%), and death (1%) are the most common complications in infants who are hospitalized with pertussis • Test with PCR/Culture if treating (reportable disease) • Supportive: hydration, oxygen if needed • Antibiotics: erythromycin, clarithromycin, azithromycin, TMP-SMX (halts spread, does NOT hasten recovery) • Close contacts –azithromycin • Adults should get Tdap as single booster dose Pertussis: A Reemerging Infection. Am Fam Physician.2013 Oct 15;88(8):507-514. Pneumonia • Most common cause – VIRUSES! (RSV 40% in < 2 years) • CXR only needed with O2 saturation < 90% or diagnosis in question • Hospitalize for hypoxemia, suspected MRSA or significant respiratory distress and dehydration (or unable to comply with outpatient) • Bacterial causes • Streptococcus pneumoniae and Mycoplasma pneumoniae • Treat with amoxicillin outpatient for 5-7 days (second choice azithromycin or clarithromycin – use first if suspect atypical cause) SMITH, DK, KUCKEL, DP. Community-Acquired Pneumonia in Children: Rapid Evidence Review. Am Fam Physician. 2021 Dec ;104(6):618-625. Gastroenteritis Most common cause – VIRUSES!!!! (75-90%) • Rotavirus and norovirus Bacterial causes • E. coli 0157 – leads to hemolytic uremic syndrome in 10-15% of cases • Bloody stools – salmonella, shigella, campylobacter enterocolitis, C. difficile Prevention – breastfeeding, rotavirus vaccines, handwashing Gastroenteritis in Children: Part I. Diagnosis. Am Fam Physician.2012 Jun 1;85(11):1059-1062. Hartman S, Brown E, Loomis E, Russell HA. Gastroenteritis in Children. Am Fam Physician. 2019 Feb 1;99(3):159-165. Erratum in: Am Fam Physician. 2019 Jun 15;99(12):732. Gastroenteritis Signs of dehydration – lethargy, dry mucous membranes, decreased tears Stool studies only needed for diarrhea >14 days or bloody Main treatment – early oral rehydration • Half strength apple juice followed by preferred fluids • Resume normal diet as soon as possible • 1-2 doses of ondansetron • Probiotics? Can prevent antibiotic-associated diarrhea • No anti-diarrheals! Gastroenteritis in Children: Part II. Prevention and Management. Am Fam Physician.2012 Jun 1;85(11):1066-1070.

A 2-year-old boy has very red cheeks and a fine rash but does not appear ill. He had a fever a couple of days ago. When can he return to day care? Fifth Disease (Erythema Infectiosum) • Common disease, rarely clinically significant – parvovirus B19 • Rash immune-mediated • Occurs after acute infection, so children with rash may attend school or day care • Arthralgias uncommon in children, more common in adolescents and young adults • Pregnant women: rare complication – fetal hydrops/fetal demise Phase I Phase II Phase III facial flushing (slapped macular rash in reticular rash may appear with cheeks) – 2-4 days pattern – 1-6 weeks heat, stress, sun Measles • Occurs in under-vaccinated populations • Prodrome – fever, malaise, anorexia • Conjunctivitis, coryza, cough, Koplik’s spots • Maculopapular, blanching rash beginning on the face and spreading down to the neck, upper trunk, lower trunk, and extremities • Complications – bronchopneumonia, encephalitis, myocarditis, subacute sclerosing panencephalitis Roseola Infantum (Exanthema Subitum) • Human herpesvirus HHV-6 Usually mild – children appear well • Children 6 months to 4 years • Natural course Days 1-4: Abrupt onset of high fever, otherwise mild symptoms Day 4: Rash begins as fever abates Pink almond-shaped macules begin on trunk, spreads peripherally Confluent then fade in hours to 2 days Roseola • Pink, blanchable, discrete maculopapular rash mainly on trunk; resolves in 1-2 days • Nearly 100% of children have antibodies to HHV-6 by 3 years of age Courtesy: Wikipedia Mumps • Occurs in under-vaccinated populations • Prodrome – myalgia, fatigue, loss of appetite, fever and headache • Parotitis is the most common manifestation • Complications – infertility, meningitis and encephalitis Henoch-Schönlein Purpura • 90% of cases occur in children under 10 • Usually follows URI with low-grade fever, fatigue • Triad of purpura, colicky abdominal pain, and arthritis • Rash: pink maculopapules progressing to nonthrombocytopenic palpable purpura on buttocks and legs • Renal disease most serious complication (occurs in 40-50% of patients) Check for hematuria and proteinuria • Treat for pain symptoms and/or renal involvement with oral prednisone 1-2 mg/kg for 2 weeks (reduces pain and persistent renal disease) Henoch-Schönlein Purpura. Am Fam Physician.2009 Oct 1;80(7):697-704. Hand, Foot, and Mouth Disease • Usually in children < 5 years, late summer, early fall • Coxsackie A16 virus (usually) • Oral-oral, oral-fecal spread • Incubation period 4-6 days • Prodrome of fever, sore throat, and anorexia 1-2 days before the rash • Small vesicles, erythematous base • Hands (nail borders), feet (heel margins), buttocks Ngufra at Wikipedia - https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c5/Han • Spontaneous resolution in a few days d_foot_and_mouth_disease_on_child_feet.jpg • Sometimes magic mouthwash preparations needed Kawasaki Disease • Diffuse vasculitis of unknown etiology • Leading cause of acquired cardiac disease in children • Diagnostic criteria: Fever > 5 days duration and 4/5 of the following: • Bilateral bulbar conjunctival injection • Oropharyngeal erythema, strawberry tongue, fissuring and crusting of the lips • Induration of hands and feet, erythema of palms and soles; desquamation of fingertips and toes • Erythematous rash (scarlatiniform or morbilliform) • Enlarged lymph node mass (> 1.5 cm) Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in Children (MIS-C) • Individuals younger than 21 years presenting with fever > 24 hours, laboratory evidence of inflammation, and evidence of clinically severe illness requiring hospitalization, with multisystem (≥ 2) organ involvement (cardiac, renal, respiratory, hematologic, gastrointestinal, dermatologic, or neurologic) • Positive for current or recent SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19) infection by reverse transcriptase polymerase chain reaction, serology, or antigen test or exposure to a suspected or confirmed COVID-19 case within 4 weeks before the onset of symptoms • 36% of patients with MIS-C had symptoms consistent with Kawasaki Disease Kawasaki Disease and MIS-C • Risks for both KD and MIS-C – coronary artery aneurysms • MIS-C - hypotension and shock • Treatment: • Single dose of IV immune globulin 2g/kg over 8-12 hrs • Low dose aspirin (3-5 mg/kg/day) until inflammatory markers normalize (continue if aneurysms develop) • ?corticosteroids • ECHO at onset and at 6-8 weeks Darby JB, Jackson JM. Kawasaki Disease and Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in Children: An Overview and Comparison. Am Fam Physician. 2021 Sep 1;104(3):244-252. Meningococcal Meningitis • Neisseria meningitidis: gram negative diplococcus. • Clinical manifestations of meningococcemia or meningitis • Abrupt onset: fever, chills, malaise, prostration • Rash: maculopapular, macular, petechial • Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome: purpura, DIC, shock, coma, death Meningitis Ages Bacterial Causes (Viruses Empiric Treatmet most common!) 0-1 month Group B Strep Ampicillin + cefotaximeOR E.coli ampicillin + gentamycin (+/- Listeria acyclovir) Klebsiella 1-23 months Streptococcus pneumoniae Ceftriaxone + vancomycin OR Neisseria meningitidis meropenem + vancomycin E. coli (+ dexamethasone 10-20 H. influenzae type b and minutes before abx) nontypeable Streptococcus agalactiae 2-18 years Streptococcuspneumoniae Ceftriaxone + vancomycin OR Neisseria meningitidis meropenem + vancomycin (+ dexamethasone 10-20 minutes before abx) Mount HR,Boyle SD. Aseptic and Bacterial Meningitis: Evaluation, Treatment, and Prevention. Am Fam Physician.2017 Sep 1;96(5):314-322 Frequent Flyers!!! • Community-acquired pneumonia • Viral URI treatment • Croup • Urinary tract infections • Bronchiolitis • Sepsis work up for newborns • Pharyngitis – strep vs. viral (remember admit for temp> 100.4 • Kawasaki’s Answers 1. B 2. A 3. C 4. C 5. A

Of the following, which is the most likely diagnosis? Vaginal Discharge • Herpes simplex virus (HSV) infection From: CDC •Painful vesicles that ulcerate • Trichomonas vaginalis infection •Malodorous discharge, occasional vulvar and vaginal irritation •Cervix can be somewhat tender to touch; patients often c/o non-specific pelvic pain • Candida albicans infection •Very itchy with thick white discharge, typically • Bacterial vaginosis (mixed vaginal flora) •Shift/overgrowth in bacterial flora •Malodorous discharge, vulvar itching; NO cervicitis or pelvic pain

According to the CDC 2021, which one of the following is the the recommended regimen for Trichomonas vaginal infection in women? nightly for 5 days Trichomoniasis CDC 2021 • Recommended regimens: • Metronidazole (resistance 4-10% of infections ) o Women: 500 mg 2x/day PO for 7 days; Men:2 g single PO dose • Tinidazole (resistance 1% of infections) o 2 g single dose o Appropriate treatment option for metronidazole-resistant trichomoniasis • Metronidazole gel considerably less efficacious (< 50%) compared to oral preparations • Treat the sexual partner Treatment Failure … • Women o If treatment failure occurs after completing a regimen of metronidazole 500 mg 2 times/day for 7 days AND she has been reexposed to an untreated partner, a repeat course of the same regimen is recommended. o If no reexposure has occurred, she should be treated with metronidazole or tinidazole 2 g once daily for 7 days. • Men o PersistentT. vaginalis after a single 2-g dose of metronidazole and has been reexposed to an untreated partner, he should be retreatedwith a single 2-g dose of metronidazole. o If he has not been reexposed, he should be administered a course of metronidazole 500 mg 2 times/day for 7 days. Drug Allergy Nitroimidazoles • Metronidazole and tinidazole are both nitroimidazoles • Patients with an IgE-mediated-type hypersensitivity reaction to 5- nitroimidazole antimicrobials should be managed by metronidazole desensitization according to published regimens and in consultation with an allergy specialist. • The optimal treatment for patients with T. vaginalis who are unable to be desensitized has not been systematically investigated and is based on case reports, some of which report using paromomycin or boric acid for treating T. vaginalis. CDC, 2021 Is There Necessary Follow-up? • High rates of reinfection – (17% in one series) o CDC 2021 – retesting for T. vaginalis (test for reinfection) is recommended for ALL sexually active women within three months following initial treatment REGARDLESS of whether they believe their sex partners were treated o Data are insufficient to support retesting men • Most recurrent infections thought to result from having sex with an untreated partner Trichomoniasis: Pregnancy CDC 2021 • Symptomatic pregnant women, regardless of pregnancy stage, should be tested and treated o T. vaginalisinfection associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes, particularly premature rupture of membranes, preterm delivery, and delivery of infants who are small for gestational age • Women can be treated with standard recommended metronidazole protocol at any stage of pregnancy o Multiple studies and meta-analyses have not demonstrated an association between metronidazole use during pregnancy and teratogenic or mutagenic effects in infants. o Can be used in breast feeding • Data from studies involving human subjects are limited regarding tinidazole use during pregnancy; however, animal data indicate this drug poses moderate risk. Tinidazole should be avoided for pregnant women, and breastfeeding should be deferredfor 72 hours after a single 2-g oral dose of tinidazole Bacterial Vaginosis • Current clinical criteria (Amsel Criteria, 3 of 4) o Discharge – homogeneous grayish-white o pH > 4.5 • Greatest sensitivity; lowest specificity • Need vaginal pH paper o Clue cells • > 20% on HPF microscopy
choice_A: Metrogel - Vaginal (topical vaginal metronidazole) 5 g applied
choice_B: Metronidazole (Flagyl) 2 g orally in a single dose
choice_C: Metronidazole 500 mg orally BID for 7 days
choice_D: Tinidazole – 2 grams orally in a single dose

stem: A 39 yo diabetic female (HgbA1c 11.4) presents to the office with an 8-day history of thick white vaginal discharge and vulvar itching. She tried a 7-day over the counter treatment for a yeast infection, but admits to only completing 5 days of the therapy. On examination, you find extensive vulvar erythema, excoriations, and edema. There is a thick curd-like white discharge at the introitus with the vagina being erythematous. A KOH preparation of vaginal secretions shows budding yeast and hyphae.

Which of the following medications would be the most appropriate treatment for this condition? weekly application for 3 weeks VVC CDC 2021 • Infrequent • Mild to moderate symptoms Uncomplicated • Candida albicans • Immunocompetent, nonpregnant • Recurrent • Severe symptoms • Nonalbicans species Complicated • Women with uncontrolled diabetes, debilitation, immunosuppression, pregnancy Predisposing Factors Associated With VVC • OCP use • Sponge and IUD • Tight clothing • Antibiotic use • Pregnancy • Diabetes • Poorly controlled* • Immunosuppression* • HIV* * Best Evidence Nonalbicans Species • Prevalence of 17% oC. glabrata, C. tropicalis, C. krusei • ? Increased OTC use or incomplete courses of therapy oElimination of more sensitive albicans and selection for more azole-resistant nonalbicans species • No hyphae on wet prep o Buds present, but can be missed Uncomplicated VVC Cochrane Systematic Review: 2002 • Clinical and mycological cures are the same – oral OR topical treatment. • All topical agents are highly effective. o No evidence that one formulation is superior to others • Oral fluconazole present in vagina for at least 72 hours o More side effects, but preferred by women Severe VVC • Extensive vulvar erythema, edema, excoriation, fissure formation • Treatment o 7-14 days of topical azole o 150 mg fluconazole in two sequential oral doses (second dose 72 hours after initial dose) Recurrent VVC • Defined • Four specific episodes occurring in 12 months or • At least 3 episodes unrelated to antibiotic therapy within 12 months Recurrent VVC CDC 2021 • Induction therapy o Fluconazole – 150 mg q 72 hours x 3 initially or 7-14 days of topical treatment • Maintenance therapy o Fluconazole 150 mg once weekly for 6 months • Long-term cure remains elusive Genital Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV) • Most infections are subclinical or mild. • HSV is a potent facilitator of HIV. • Screening – USPSTF 2023 o Routine serologic screening of asymptomatic persons is not recommended • Latex condoms are effective in preventing transmission. o 37% of couples do not use condoms as advised Primary HSV • No antibodies to HSV 1 or 2 • 75% asymptomatic • Lesions appear 2-14 days following exposure o Tender vesicles, ulcers • Fever, HA, myalgias • Viral shedding for 12 days From: Wikipedia First Clinical Episode of Genital Herpes* • Most patients should receive antiviral therapy. Agent Dose Duration Acyclovir 400 mg po TID 7-10 days Famciclovir 250 mg po TID 7-10 days Valacyclovir 1 g po BID 7-10 days • Treatment can be extended if healing is incomplete after 10 days of therapy. * CDC 2021 Recurrent HSV • 50% of patients will develop prodromal symptoms such as tingling or shooting pains into the hips or buttocks. oUsually unilateral and much smaller than primary consistent area of outbreak • 90% of persons with primary HSV-2 will have at least one recurrence in the next 12 months. Photo from library of David. G Weismiller, MD, ScM Episodic Therapy for Recurrent Genital Herpes - CDC 2021 • Initiate therapy within 1 day of lesion onset or during prodrome that precedes some outbreaks Agent Dose Duration Acyclovir 800 mg po TID 2 days Acyclovir 800 mg po BID 5 days Famciclovir 125 mg po BID 5 days Famciclovir 1 g BID 1 day Famciclovir 500 mg times 1, then 250 2 days mg BID for 2 days Valacyclovir 500 mg po BID 3 days Valacyclovir 1 g q day 5 days HSV: Suppressive Therapy CDC 2021 • Reduces frequency of recurrences by 70%-80% (with frequent recurrences > 6/year) • Reduces but does not eliminate subclinical viral shedding – therefore ? prevention of transmission Agent Dose Acyclovir 400 mg po BID Famciclovir 250 mg po BID Valacyclovir* 500 mg po q day Valacyclovir 1 g q day *Valacyclovir 500 mg q day might be less effective in patients who have very frequent recurrences (i.e., > 10 episodes per year).
choice_B: Fluconazole 150 mg orally in two doses 72 hours apart
choice_C: Miconazole intravaginal daily for 7 days followed by once
choice_D: Clotrimazole intravaginal daily for three days

stem: A 22 yo sexually active female comes to your office for a Pap test and STI screening. Her Pap test is normal but she tests positive for gonorrhea. Chlamydia testing is negative. Which one of the following is the recommended treatment? single dose Gonorrhea — Rates of Reported Cases by County, United States #1 -Mississippi * Per 100,000 CDC recommended regimens for uncomplicated gonococcal infections (cervix, urethra, rectum), 2020 • Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM as a single dose for persons weighing <150 kg (300 lb) • For persons weighing ≥150 kg (300 lb), 1 g of IM ceftriaxone should be administered. • If chlamydial infection has NOT been excluded, providers should treat for chlamydia with doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days. (During pregnancy, azithromycin 1 g as a single dose is recommended to treat chlamydia) St. Cyr S, Barbee L, Workowski KA, et al. Update to CDC’s Treatment Guidelines for Gonococcal Infection, 2020. MMWR Morb Mortal Wkly Rep 2020;69:1911–1916. DOI:http://dx.doi.org/10.15585/mmwr.mm6950a6external icon CDC recommended regimens for uncomplicated gonococcal infections (cervix, urethra, rectum), 2020 • Alternative regimens for uncomplicated gonococcal infections of the cervix, urethra, or rectum if ceftriaxone is NOT available: • Gentamicin 240 mg IM as a single dose plus azithromycin 2 g orally as a single dose OR • Cefixime 800 mg orally as a single dose. If treating with cefixime, and chlamydial infection has not been excluded, providers should treat for chlamydia with doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days. During pregnancy, azithromycin 1 g as a single dose is recommended to treat chlamydia. CDC recommended regimens for uncomplicated gonococcal infections of the pharynx, 2020 • Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM as a single dose for persons weighing <150 kg (300 lb) • Persons weighing ≥150 kg (300 lb), 1 g of IM ceftriaxone should be administered. • Chlamydia coinfection is identified when pharyngeal gonorrhea testing is performed, providers should treat for chlamydia with doxycycline 100 mg orally twice a day for 7 days. (During pregnancy, azithromycin 1 g as a single dose is recommended to treat chlamydia.) • No reliable alternative treatments are available for pharyngeal gonorrhea. For persons with a history of a beta-lactam allergy, a thorough assessment of the reaction is recommended.* • For persons with an anaphylactic or other severe reaction (e.g., Stevens Johnson syndrome) to ceftriaxone, consult an infectious disease specialist for an alternative treatment recommendation. *CDC. Sexually transmitted diseases treatment guidelines. MMWR Recomm Rep 2015;64(No. RR-3).https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/preview/mmwrhtml/rr6403a1.htm. Test of Reinfection? CDC 2021 • A high prevalence of gonorrhea infection is observed in men and women who were treated for N. gonorrhea infection in the preceding several months. • Men and women who have been treated for gonorrhea should be retested approximately 3 months after treatment, regardless of whether they believe that their sex partners were treated Gonorrhea USPSTF 2021 • For sexually active women, including pregnant persons: Screenfor Gonorrhea if they are o 24 years or younger o Women aged 15 to 24 years have the highest infection rated o 25 years or older and it increased risk for infection o Previous or coexisting STI o New or more than 1 sex partner o Sex partner having sex with other partners at the same time o Sex partner with an STI o Inconsistent condom use when NOT in a mutually monogamous relationship o History of exchanging sex for money or drugs o History of incarceration • Evidence is insufficient to assess the balance of benefits and harms of screening for chlamydia and gonorrhea in men. (I recommendation)
choice_A: Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID for 7 days
choice_B: Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM as a single dose
choice_C: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM as a single dose
choice_D: Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM plus azithromycin 1 g PO each as a

stem: A 21 yo ♀ had a pelvic examination and a normal Pap test 1 week ago. Her screening test for Chlamydia returned positive. She is now being treated for chlamydia cervicitis with Doxycycline 100 mg BID for seven days. When should she have a test of cure for Chlamydia? Chlamydia — Rates of Reported Cases by County, United States * Per 100,000 #1 -Alaska Test of Cure? • Except in pregnant women, test of cure (repeat testing 3-4 weeks after completing therapy) is not recommended when treated with the recommended or alternative regimens. Exceptions: o Therapeutic compliance is in question o Symptoms persist o Reinfection is suspected • Pregnant women diagnosed with a chlamydial infection during the first trimester should not only receive a test to document chlamydial eradication but be retested 3 months after treatment. Test of Reinfection? CDC 2021 • A high prevalence of C. trachomatis infection is observed in men and women who were treated for chlamydial infection in the preceding several months. • Men and women who have been treated for chlamydia should be retested approximately 3 months after treatment, regardless of whether they believe that their sex partners were treated Chlamydia USPSTF 2021 • For sexually active women, including pregnant persons: Screenfor Chlamydia if they are o 24 years or younger o Women aged 15 to 24 years have the highest infection rated o 25 years or older and it increased risk for infection o Previous or coexisting STI o New or more than 1 sex partner o Sex partner having sex with other partners at the same time o Sex partner with an STI o Inconsistent condom use when NOT in a mutually monogamous relationship o History of exchanging sex for money or drugs o History of incarceration • Evidence is insufficient to assess the balance of benefits and harms of screening for chlamydia and gonorrhea in men. (I recommendation) Nonculture Tests for Chlamydia and GC (NAAT) • DNA-amplification tests (gold standard now) o More sensitive than culture (detects 20% more) and 100% specific for GC and chlamydia (CDC 2007) • Appropriate specimens include o Endocervical secretions or urine samples in females o Urethral swab or urine in males • Gram stain of a male urethral sample for intracellular gram (−) diplococci, highly sensitive and specific; diagnostic in symptomatic men. o Anorectal swab o Throat swab Chlamydia Treatment CDC 2021 • Recommended – no test of cure required • Doxycycline 100 mg po BID x 7 d • Alternative regimens • Azithromycin 1 gm orally in a single dose OR • Levofloxacin 500 mg orally 1x/day for 7 days Chlamydia in Pregnancy CDC 2021 • Recommended o Azithromycin 1 g po times 1 • Alternative o Amoxicillin 500 mg po TID for 7 days CDC 2021 Summary • Very high rates of reinfection • Men and women with gonorrhea and/or chlamydia should return in 3 months for test of reinfection regardless of treatment status of partner. • Women with trichomoniasis should return in 3 months for test of reinfection regardless of treatment status of partner. Expedited Partner Therapy (EPT) • Clinical practice of treating the sex partners of patients diagnosed with chlamydia or gonorrhea by providing prescriptions or medications to the patient to take to his/her partner without the health care provider first examining the partner • Provision of expedited partner treatment lessens the risk of reinfection for patients treated for N. gonorrhoeae or C. trachomatis infection. • Studies have shown that patients whose partners received EPT were 29% less likely to be reinfected than those who simply told their partners to visit the doctor • CDC has concluded that EPT is a useful option to facilitate partner management, particularly for treatment of male partners of women with chlamydial infection or gonorrhea https://www.cdc.gov/std/ept/default.htm Pelvic Inflammatory Disease • Clinical diagnosis, not laboratory-based • Untreated PID has significant morbidity and mortality – empiric treatment is recommended if the patient meets the following minimal diagnostic criteria: oUterine/adnexal tenderness or oCervical motion tenderness, and oNo other cause for illness identified • Empiric therapy unlikely to impair diagnosis and management of other important causes of lower abdominal pain – do not delay treatment while pursuing additional evaluation if history and physical are suggestive of PID. Other Helpful (but Not Necessary) Criteria • Mucopurulent discharge • Temperature > 38.3°C (101°F) • WBC on wet prep • ↑ ESR • ↑ C-reactive protein • Laboratory evidence of GC or chlamydia Management of PID • Start empiric therapy if minimal criteria present. oIncreased risk of ectopic and infertility; Delay > 3 days = 3x risk (observational data) • Polymicrobial infection is likely present if the patient has symptoms. oBroad-spectrum regimen necessary • Treat sexual partner if had sex with patient during 60 days preceding onset of symptoms. PID:IM/Oral treatment CDC 2021 Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM in a single dose6 PLUS doxycycline 100 mg orally 2x/day for 14 days WITH metronidazole 500 mg orally 2x/day for 14 days OR Cefoxitin 2 gm IM in a single dose AND probenecid 1 gm orally, administered concurrently in a single dose PLUS doxycycline 100 mg orally 2x/day for 14 days WITH metronidazole 500 mg orally 2x/day for 14 days OR Other parenteral third-generation cephalosporin (e.g., ceftizoxime or cefotaxime) PLUS doxycycline 100 mg orally 2x/day for 14 days WITH metronidazole 500 mg orally 2x/day for 14 days Criteria for Admission for the Treatment of PID • Uncertain diagnosis • Surgical emergencies like appendicitis cannot be excluded • Suspected pelvic abscesses • Concurrent pregnancy (due to high risk of maternal mortality, fetal wastage, and preterm delivery) • Adolescent patient with uncertain compliance with therapy • Severe illness • Patient cannot tolerate outpatient regimen (e.g., severe vomiting) • Lack of response to 72 hours of treatment • Concurrent HIV infection • Clinical follow-up cannot be arranged within 72 hours PID: ParenteralTreatment CDC 2021 Recommended Alternatives  Ceftriaxone 1 gm by IV every 24  Ampicillin-sulbactam 3 gm by IV every hours PLUS doxycycline 100 mg 6 hours PLUS doxycycline 100 mg orally or by IV every 12 hours PLUS orally or by IV every 12 hours OR metronidazole 500 mg orally or by IV  Clindamycin 900 mg by IV every 8 every 12 hours OR hours PLUS gentamicin 2 mg/kg body  Cefotetan 2 gm by IV every 12 hours weight by IV or IM, FOLLOWED BY PLUS doxycycline 100 mg orally or 1.5 mg/kg body weight every 8 hours. by IV every 12 hours OR Can substitute with 3–5 mg/kg body weight 1x/day  Cefoxitin 2 gm by IV every 6 hours PLUS doxycycline 100 mg orally or by IV every 12 hours The complete list of recommended regimens can be found in Sexually Transmitted Infections Treatment Guidelines, 2021. HIV Screening USPSTF 2019 • Screen all patients aged 15-65 for HIV, regardless of risk level(Level A). Younger adolescents and older adults who are at increased risk of infection should also be screened. • Substantially decrease the HIV disease burden across the country. • Dramatically reduce transmission of the virus. • Markedly curtail infected patients’ progression to AIDS and death. • Clinical guidelines have moved away from risk-basedto routinevoluntary screening • Convincing evidence shows "with high certainty" that identifying and treating HIV infection in asymptomatic people will substantially benefit public health as well as the health of those individual patients. • Insufficient evidence to establish optimal interval for screening • Reasonable to rescreen “at-risk” people at 1-year intervals: Engage in high-risk behaviors. Live in or receive medical care in high-prevalence settings, eg, correctional facilities, homeless shelters, TB clinics, STI clinics, and clinics that serve men who have sex with men. HIV Testing Options • Rapid assay (Sensitivity & Specificity > 99.5%) oInitial positive = “preliminary positive”  requires confirmation with conventional assay oOTC availability (OraQuick) • Conventional/“standard” assay: serum immunoassay with confirmatory WB or indirect immunofluorescent assay (Sensitivity & Specificity > 99.5%) • Today: 4th generation screen and “cascade” testing Prevention of HIV Infection: Preexposure Prophylaxis USPSTF – August 2023 •Recommends that clinicians offer preexposure prophylaxis (PrEP) with effective antiretroviral therapy to persons who are at high risk of HIV acquisition. (Grade A) Persons considered for PrEP • Men who have sex with men, are sexually active, and have one of the following characteristics: • Serodiscordantsex partner (i.e., in a sexual relationship with a partner living with HIV)* • Inconsistent use of condoms during receptive or insertiveanal sex • STI with syphilis, gonorrhea, or chlamydia within the past 6 months • Heterosexually active women and men who have one of the following characteristics: • Serodiscordantsex partner (i.e., in a sexual relationship with a partner living with HIV) • Inconsistent use of condoms during sex with a partner whose HIV status is unknown and who is at high risk (eg, a person who injects drugs or a man who has sex with men and women) • STI with syphilis or gonorrhea within the past 6 months • Persons who inject drugs and have one of the following characteristics: • Shared use of drug injection equipment • Risk of sexual acquisition of HIV (see above) Preexposure Prophylaxis (PrEP) Adolescents (at least 35kg) and Adults ▪ Use of antiretroviral medication by HIV-uninfected people for the purpose of preventing HIV infection. ▪ Three FDA Approved Medications ▪ Once daily oral therapy = 200mg emtricitabine/300mg tenofovirdisoproxilfumarate (TDF) [Truvada®] o TDF nephrotoxicity is alargely reversibleform of toxic acute tubular necrosis targeting proximal tubules ▪ Once daily oral therapy = 200mg emtricitabine/25mg tenofoviralafenamide(TAF) [Descovy®] o TAF -novel prodrug, causes less renal impairment, can improve renal function in patients switched from TDF. ▪ Intramuscular cabotegravir(CAB) (FDA Approved 12/20/2021) o 600 mg cabotegraviradministered as one 3 ml intramuscular injection in the gluteal muscle (Initial dose) o Second dose 4 weeks after first dose (month 1 follow-up visit) o Every 8 weeks thereafter (month 3,5,7, follow-up visits etc.) ▪ HIV resistance if poor adherence ▪ Abrupt withdrawal of Truvadaor Descovy in HBV-infected patients may lead to HBV flare/VL rebound ▪ Not recommended if estimated CrClless than 30mL/min Minimum Baseline Testing • HIV Ag/Aby and HIV-1 RNA − Exclude HIV, as use of PrEP in the setting of undiagnosed HIV could lead to antiretroviral resistance • Hepatitis B − Assess for chronic hepatitis B infection, as the medication used for PrEP is active also against hepatitis B − CDC recommends a triple panel test in screening (anyone) for Hepatitis B (March 2023) o HBsAg, anti-HBsAg and total HBcAg • Creatinine (mild increase expected) − Ensure that the creatinine clearance is greater than 30 mL/min, as one of the components in PrEP (tenofovir) should not be used in the setting of renal failure * months Monitoring Daily Oral PrEP Frequency Test HIV Infection • Daily Oral PrEP–every 3 m* HIV Ag/Ab andHIV-1 RNA Renal • Patients aged >50 Basic Metabolic Panel Function Patients with eCrCl< 90 ml/min at PrEP Initiation –every 6 m • All patients –every 12 m Bacterial STI • MSM and transgender women who have sex Oral rectal, urine, blood Screening with men –every 3 m • Bacterial STI screening for all sexually-active Vaginal, oral, rectal, urine- patients –every 6 m as indicated; blood • Chlamydia screening for heterosexually active Vaginal, urine women and men –every 12m Monitoring Cabotegravir Injection PrEP Monitoring At follow-up visit 1 month after first injection • HIV Ag/Ab test and HIV-1 RNA assay At follow-up visits every 2 months (beginning with the third injection –month 3) provide: • HIV Ag/Ab test and HIV-1 RNA assay At follow-up visits every 4 months (beginning with the third injection-month 3) provide: • Bacterial STI screening Ba for MSM and transgender women who have sex with men2 –oral, rectal, urine, blood At follow-up visits every 6 months (beginning with the fifth injection –month 7) provide: • Bacterial STI screening1 for all heterosexually-active women and men –[vaginal, rectal, urine -as indicated], blood At follow-up visits at least every 12 months (after the first injection) provide the following: • Assess desire to continue injections for PrEP • Chlamydia screening for heterosexually active women and men –vaginal, urine Time to Effectiveness • PrEP reaches maximum protection from HIV for receptive anal sex at about 7 days of daily use (maximum rectal tissue intracellular concentrations of tenofovir diphosphate (the activated form of the medication) reached • For receptive vaginal sex (cervicovaginal tissues) and injection drug use, PrEP reaches maximum protection at about 20 days of daily use maximum intracellular concentrations of tenofovir diphosphate reached (in blood) • No data are yet available about how long it takes to reach maximum protection for insertive anal or insertive vaginal sex • When stopping PrEP, individuals should continue using PrEP for four weeks after the last significant exposure Effectiveness in Prevention • Daily PrEP reduces the risk of getting HIV from sex by 99% o Taken DAILY – 99% effective o Combine with condoms to reduce risk AND to prevent other STIs • Among people who inject drugs, it reduces the risk by 74% How to introduce the Topic ? • Given ongoing disparities in PrEP access and utilization, and potentially stigmatizing language such as “HIV risk”, general counseling messages and questions may be considered – examples include: “Are you interested in hearing more about medications that can prevent HIV? “Have you heard of PrEP?” “How do you think your [sex] life would improve if you didn’t have to worry about HIV?” Nonoccupational postexposure prophylaxis (nPEP) – CDC 2016 • HIV testing (rapid if possible) of patient +/- source* • Should be initiated within 72 hours of exposure • 28-day course of 3-drug antiretroviral regimen • Preferred: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (300 mg) once daily with emtricitabine (200 mg) [Truvada] q day plus raltegravir (400 mg) BID or dolutegravir 50 mg daily * Can D/C nPEPif source found to be HIV negative nPEP Follow-Up • Repeat testing at 4-6 weeks and 3 months after exposure • Oral HIV tests ARE NOT recommended for use among persons being evaluated for nPEP • Any person whose HCV antibody tests is negative at baseline but positive at 4-6 weeks after the exposure, HIV antibody tests at 3 and 6 months to rule out delayed (HIV) seroconversion nPEP Effectiveness •No RCTs •Observational and Animal Data (synthesis) −If started soon after exposure, PEP can reduce the risk of HIV infection by over 80%. −Adherence to a full 28-day course of ARVs is critical to the effectiveness of the intervention •Recent evidence shows nPEP uptake has been insufficient −57% of the people who initiated nPEP have completed the full course −Rates even lower at 40% for victims of sexual assault who initiated PEP Serologic Testing for Syphilis: Benefits and Challenges of a Reverse Algorithm. 36(24). Advantages of Reverse Algorithm • Detection of early infection (before nontreponemal antibodies can be detected), latent infection (after nontreponemal antibodies have disappeared), automated workflow, and objective results reporting. • Many countries have moved primarily to the reverse algorithm, and some identify it as the preferred approach (specifically using an assay capable of detecting both IgM and IgG),though traditional testing is still standard. • One concern with reverse testing is managing discordant results: initial treponemal assay is reactive, but the nontreponemal is nonreactive. o Resolution is essential, as this could indicate early or late-latent infection need treatment, a previously treated condition, or a false-positive result. • Choice of algorithm may primarily be driven by the volume of syphilis testing, low-volume laboratories opting for the more manual and less costly traditional algorithm.
