A 43-year-old male presents to your office with five days of nasal congestion and headache. His temperature is 100.8oF, he has purulent rhinorrhea, and minimal tenderness to palpation over the frontal and maxillary sinuses. Your next step would be? Acute Rhinosinusitis Symptoms <4 weeks • Purulent nasal drainage • Nasal obstruction • Facial pain, pressure or fullness Etiology • Viral is most common etiology • Bacterial rhinosinusitis – 0.5-2% Indicators of Bacterial Rhinosinusitis  Symptoms >7 days  Worsening of symptoms  Moderate to severe pain and Fever >101oF  Bimodal illness – worsening after initial improvement  Risk factors: tobacco exposure, allergies Am Fam Physician.2016Jul15;94(2):97-105 SORT C Non-helpful Tests  Trans-illumination of sinuses  Viral or bacterial cultures (except endoscopic)  Diagnostic imaging not recommended unless complication or alternative dx suspected  Sinus x-rays  Sinus CT scans  MRI or ultrasound AAmm FFaamm PPhhyyssiicciiaann..22001166JJuull1155;;9944((22))::9977--110055 SSOORRTT CC Treatment of Viral Rhinosinusitis  Self-limited disease – treatment does not shorten the course  Supportive care: SORT A  Analgesics (NSAIDs, acetaminophen)  Saline nasal sprays (irrigation)  Intranasal corticosteroids • Consider: Ipratropium- NIH data for rhinorrhea only, not pain or congestion Topical decongestants: do not use longer than 72 hours, risk of rebound congestion • Not recommended:  Oral decongestants: no benefit in RCT  Mucolytics: no good RCTs  Antihistamines: worsen congestion by over-drying; consider if h/o allergy  Zinc-no benefit for sinusitis (for URIs-decreases duration by 1/3) Antibiotic Treatment of Bacterial Rhinosinusitis  Watchful waiting without ABX if has follow up SORT A  Supportive treatment for 7 d- 70% resolve by 1 week  ABX if sx > 7 d w/o improvement or if worsening SORT C  AAFP 2019: NNT with ABX for benefit 17, NNH 8 Am Fam Physician.2016Jul15;94(2):97-105 Avoid prescribing antibiotics in ED for uncomplicated sinusitis Do not routinely obtain imaging Do not routinely prescribe antibiotics for acute, mild to moderate sinusitis unless sx >7 days or bimodal What does IDSA say? Empiric antibiotics for bacterial rhinosinusitis if: • Severe sx or fever >102oF and purulent nasal discharge or facial pain lasting at least 3-4 days • Persistent symptoms lasting 10 days w/o improvement • Worsening symptoms, new onset of symptoms lasting 5-6 days after initially improving (“double sickening”) • Immunocompromised • IDSA- more strict criteria IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline. Clinical Infectious Diseases 2012;54(8):1041–5 Antibiotics for Bacterial sinusitis (IDSA) Amoxicillin-Clavulanate empiric antibiotic choice due to resistance Duration: 7-10 days adults, 10-14 days for kids High dose (1 g BID or 90 mg/kg/day) if: >10% PCN resistant S. pneumonia Recent hospitalization ABX within past month Daycare attendance <2 y.o. or >65 y.o. Severe infections Immunocompromised Same adjunctive therapy as viral: NSAIDs, saline spray, nasal steroids IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline. Clinical Infectious Diseases 2012;54(8):1041–5 SORT C Cochrane Database 2023 http://www.ncbi/nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=18056902SOR B Antibiotics for Bacterial Rhinosinusitis (IDSA)- 2nd Line  Adults: fluoroquinolones and doxycycline No longer recommended due to resistance: ‾ Macrolides ‾ Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole ‾ Cephalosporins  Kids: cephalosporins IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline. observation period of three days after 10-day illness • Amoxicillin (80-90 mg/kg) is the drug of choice for children • Amoxicillin-Clavulanate or ceftriaxone are alternatives • Recommend against any imaging studies AAP: Amoxicillin IDSA: Augmentin AAFP: either Amoxicillin or Augmentin 1stline in adults or kids Chronic Rhinosinusitis 12 weeks of symptoms ‾ Nasal obstruction ‾ Facial pain ‾ stem: A 57-year-old female patient of yours presents with dizziness and a sensation that she is spinning. It occurs when she turns in bed or lifts her head to look in an upper cabinet. Episodes are brief but are becoming more frequent. She has no tinnitus or hearing loss. The most likely diagnosis would be? Vertigo Illusion of movement ‾ Spinning, tilting, swaying ‾ Subjective or objective (patient or environment) Must be distinguished from pre-syncopal faintness and disequilibrium Central and peripheral causes Peripheral Vertigo Central Vertigo Imbalance Mild-Moderate Severe; cannot stand Hearing Loss Yes No Tinnitus Yes No Other Neuro Symptoms No Yes Peripheral Vertigo  Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo ‾ Canalithiasis ‾ Torsional up-beating nystagmus (85-95% in posterior semicircular canal) ‾ Brief spinning spells (seconds) when head moved ‾ Nausea, but rarely vomiting ‾ No hearing loss, ear pain or tinnitus  Vestibular Neuronitis ‾ Viral or post-viral inflammation of labyrinth ‾ Saccade, horizontal nystagmus ‾ With unilateral hearing loss, it is called “labyrinthitis” ‾ Lasts 1-2 days before resolution Am Fam Physician.2017Feb1;95(3):154-162. Peripheral Vertigo  Meniere’s Disease ‾ Endolymphatic Hydrops ‾ Associated with tinnitus, hearing loss and ear fullness  Acoustic neuroma ‾ Vertigo minor, tinnitus and hearing loss main complaints  Herpes Zoster Oticus (Ramsay Hunt Syndrome) Courtesy: Dr. Christopher Chang, Hearing loss, vertigo, taste loss, facial paralysis Fauquier ENT Am Fam Physician.2017Feb1;95(3):154-162. Central Vertigo  Vestibular Migraine  Wallenberg’s syndrome ‾ Infarction of lateral medulla  Cerebellar hemorrhage or infarction ‾ Sudden intense vertigo and vomiting ‾ Markedly impaired gait – falls to the side of the lesion Evaluation of Vertigo  Careful history  Neurological exam  Lab tests help < 1% of the time  MRI: if other neuro signs, hearing loss  Dix-Hallpike Maneuver  Great NPV for BPPV Treatment of Vertigo  Medications ‾ Most useful for vertigo that lasts hours or days – not BPPV ‾ Lots of side effects: ‾ sedation ‾ risk of falls and urinary retention in older patients ‾ Anticholinergics – scopolamine ‾ Antihistamines – meclizine, dimenhydrinate ‾ Phenothiazines – promethazine, metoclopramide ‾ Benzodiazepines – diazepam, lorazepam Treatment of Vertigo  Vestibular Rehabilitation (PT)  CNS compensation for peripheral vestibular injury – ? Central?  Start early: balance and function improve vs controls  Home exercises also effective Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo ‾ Medications generally not helpful ‾ Canalith repositioning – Epley Maneuver Bells Palsy • Idiopathic facial paralysis • Early dx and tx decreases chronic partial paralysis and pain • DDx: stroke/ central pathology of facial nerve spare the forehead • Tx: Antivirals with corticosteroids more effective than either alone • NNT 15 • Do not use antivirals alone to treat (SORT A) Antiviral Therapy for Bells Palsy. Seehusen, DA and Egitto, EA. Am Fam Physician.2016May1;93(9):742-743. Albers, JR, TamangS. Common Questions about Bell Palsy. Am Fam Physician.2014Feb1;89(3):209-212. stem: Which of the following statements regarding opioids is true? related chronic pain prior to beginning opioid therapy with immediate acting, rather than extended-release products, and then titrated to optimal effect Opioid Epidemic • appx 645,000 opioid overdose deaths from 1999-2021 • 20,100 deaths in 2010 • 47,600 deaths in 2017 • 46,000 deaths in 2018 • 49,860 deaths in 2019 • 69,710 deaths in 2020 • 107,000 deaths in 2021 • Waves • 1st – 1990’s – prescription opioids • 2nd – 2010 – heroin • 3rd – 2013 – fentanyl (Rx and illicit) • 4th – 2020– fentanyl and methamphetamines Opioid Epidemic • Overdose death rate increase paralleled increase in prescription analgesic drug sales • Drug overdose is now the leading cause of accidental death in US in 29 states (2016 data) • MVA (#2) is leading cause in 11 states • 2016 data (Per CDC) 63,000 lethal drug OD 40,000 OD related to prescription analgesics • 2021 data 107,000 overdose deaths Efficacy of Opioids for Chronic Pain • Good evidence for effectiveness in pain from cancer • Cochrane review 2010 • Weak evidence that opioids reduce chronic non- cancer pain in some highly selected patients • Busse & Kamaleldin 2018 • Opioids provide benefit in non-cancer related pain and physical functioning but magnitude of benefit is small Evidence is low-mod quality Efficacy of Opioids for Chronic P ain • Patients with chronic diffuse centralized pain • HA, fibromyalgia, abdominal pain, pelvic pain • Much less likely to benefit from opioids • More likely to be harmed from opioids CDC Opioid Guidelines 2022 - #2 • Before starting opioid therapy for subacute or chronic pain, clinicians should discuss with patients the realistic benefits and known risks of opioid therapy • Should work with patients to establish treatment goals for pain and function, and • • Should consider how opioid therapy will be discontinued if benefits do not outweigh risks (recommendation category: A; evidence type: 2). Opioid Therapy—Realistic Goals • Complete pain relief rarely achieved • Common goals include • Pain reduction • Improvement in selected areas of function • Improved mood • Improved work Prior to Opioid Use • Focal or treatable pain generators should be identified and treated • Use physical and psychological modalities • Attempt restoration of sleep • Treat mood disorders • Use nonopioid/adjuvant analgesics If Initiating Opioid Therapy • Informed consent & treatment agreement • Consider opioid therapeutic trial < 90 days • Begin with short-acting agents • Titrate to optimal effect • Aggressively/proactively manage side effects • Drug screening at onset and at least every 12 months • Reassessment • Is the medication improving function? • If not, implement exit strategy. CDC Opioid Guidelines 2022 - #3 • When starting opioid therapy for acute, subacute, or chronic pain, clinicians should prescribe immediate-release opioids instead of extended-release and long-acting (ER/LA) opioids • (recommendation category: A; evidence type: 4). CDC Opioid Guidelines 2022 - #9 • Should review the patient’s history of controlled substance prescriptions using state prescription drug monitoring program (PDMP) data • (recommendation category: B; evidence type: 4). stem: Which of the following is correct? concurrently acting opioids, ie, methadone typically severe and often life threatening CDC Opioid Guidelines 2022 - #7 • Clinicians should evaluate benefits and risks with patients within 1–4 weeks of starting opioid therapy • Clinicians should regularly reevaluate benefits and risks of continued opioid therapy • (recommendation category: A; evidence type: 4). Opioid Side Effects • Constipation • Respiratory depression • Sedation • Nausea/vomiting • Dizziness/orthostatic hypotension • Mental confusion • Itching • Edema • Decreased libido • Opioid-induced hyperalgesia CDC Opioid Guidelines 2022 - #11 • Clinicians should use particular caution when prescribing opioid pain medication and benzodiazepines concurrently • Consider whether benefits outweigh risks of concurrent prescribing of opioids and other central nervous system depressants • (recommendation category: B; evidence type: 3). CDC Opioid Guidelines 2022 - #8 • Clinicians should work with patients to incorporate into the management plan strategies to mitigate risk, including offering naloxone • (recommendation category: A; evidence type: 4). Naloxone •Medical setting • IV/IM/SC • 0.4-2.0 mg q 2-3 min until response •Auto-injector (Evzio) • 2 auto-injectors/carton • 0.4 mg (old) – 2 mg subcut or IM •Nasal spray (Narcan) • Single use, single dose • 4 mg/actuation • Now available OTC Nalmefone •Nasal spray (Opvee) • 2.7 mg/spray • Prescription only •IV/IM/SC • 0.5 mg/70 kg weight Opioid Withdrawal • After cessation of opioid • Use of partial agonists •Buprenorphine (Buprenex) • Use of agonist-antagonists •Nalbuphine (Nubain) • Use of antagonist •Naloxone (Narcan) Opioid Withdrawal • May begin 6 to 12 hours after the last dose of a short-acting opioid • Persist for several days • 24 to 48 hours after cessation of methadone • Persist for several weeks • May begin immediately after reversal with antagonist • Usually mild, but potentially life-threatening in compromised patients • Peak within 24 to 48 hours of onset Opioid Withdrawal • Symptoms • Nausea, vomiting • Abd pain • Diarrhea • Goose bumps • Excessive yawning • Tremors • Myalgias & arthralgias • Rhinorrhea • Lacrimation • Anxiety & restlessness Opioid Withdrawal • Physical findings •Mydriasis •Yawning •Hyperactive bowel sounds •Piloerection •Tachycardia •Hypertension or hypotension •Tachypnea Opioid Withdrawal •Treatment •Naturally occurring withdrawal • Either opioid or nonopioid adjunctive medication • Clonidine (Catapres) • 0.1-0.3 mg PO every hour • Benzodiazepines • Antiemetics • Antidiarrheals Opioid Withdrawal • Treatment •Iatrogenic withdrawal (from an opioid antagonist) • Adjunctive medications should be used rather than opioids Opioid-Associated States • Tolerance • Physical dependence • Addiction • Pseudo-addiction Tolerance • Continued exposure to a drug induces changes that result in a decrease in drug effects over time Physical Dependence • State of adaptation manifested by drug class – specific withdrawal syndrome • Precipitated by •Abrupt cessation •Dose reduction •Decreased blood level •Administration of antagonist Addiction (ASAM 2019) “Addiction is a treatable, chronic medical disease involving complex interactions among brain circuits, genetics, the environment, and an individual’s life experiences. People with addiction use substances or engage in behaviors that become compulsive and often continue despite harmful consequences. Prevention efforts and treatment approaches for addiction are generally as successful as those for other chronic diseases.” Pseudo-Addiction •Iatrogenic syndrome of behaviors developing as a result of inadequate pain control •Behaviors disappear when pain is adequately controlled, unlike addiction •Controversial –Not recognized in latest Federation of State Medical Boards definition list Aberrant Drug-Related Behaviors— Addiction • Rx forgery • Concurrent use of illicit drugs • Recurrent Rx losses • Selling Rx drugs • Stealing or borrowing drugs • Getting meds from nonmedical sources • Frequent unsanctioned dose escalations CDC Opioid Guidelines 2022 - #12 • Clinicians should offer or arrange treatment with evidence-based medications to treat patients with opioid use disorder • Detoxification on its own, without medications for opioid use disorder, is not recommended for opioid use disorder • because of increased risks for resuming drug use, overdose, and overdose death (recommendation category: A; evidence type: 1).

stem: Which of the following statements is true? been proven to dramatically decrease the incidence of addiction recommended for all chronic opioid-receiving patients by the Federation of State Medical Boards mandate regularly scheduled, as opposed to periodic, drug testing methadone are usually detected in immunoassay-based urine drug testing CDC Opioid Guidelines 2016 - #3 Before starting and periodically during opioid therapy, discuss with patients  Known risks and realistic benefits of opioid therapy  Patient and clinician responsibilities for managing therapy Pain Management Agreements (Informed Consent & Treatment Agreements) • Frequently recommended by experts in chronic pain management • Recommended by Federation of State Medical Boards and CDC • No studies have demonstrated a reduction in the incidence of addiction or abuse when contracts are used Pain Management Agreements (Informed Consent & Treatment Agreements) • Sample PMA • Sample opioid prescribing checklist • New Hampshire Medical Society • https://www.nhms.org • Resources • Opioid prescribing resources • Opioid prescribing clinical tools Pain Management Agreements (Informed Consent & Treatment Agreements) • Potential risks & benefits of opioid Rx • Potential side effects • Likelihood of tolerance/physical dependence • Risk of opioid misuse, addiction, overdose • Risk of drug interactions, oversedation, and impaired motor skills Pain Management Agreements (Informed Consent & Treatment Agreements) • Goals of treatment • Patient responsibility for safe medication use • Patient responsibility to obtain opioids from only one source • Patient agreement to periodic drug testing • Provider’s prescribing policies and expectations • Provider’s responsibility to be available or have coverage for unforeseen problems/scheduled refills • Reasons why therapy may be changed/stopped CDC Opioid Guidelines 2022 - #10 • When prescribing opioids for subacute or chronic pain, clinicians should consider the benefits and risks of toxicology testing • to assess for prescribed medications as well as other prescribed and nonprescribed controlled substances (recommendation category: B; evidence type: 4). Urine Drug Testing • Enzyme-linked immunoassay testing of antibodies is usually the first urine drug test done because it is quick and may be used to test for multiple drugs at once • Immunoassay testing is not always accurate for detecting all drugs and there is sometimes cross- reactivity with others • Use gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GCMS) for specific drug testing Urine Drug Testing • Morphine and codeine usually are detected accurately • Synthetic and semisynthetic opioids like oxycodone, fentanyl, and methadone are not always detected with immunoassay •Can be specifically tested with GC/MS Urine Drug Testing • Cocaine testing has a low degree of cross-reactivity and is generally a true positive (within 48 hrs of last exposure) • A negative test for benzodiazepines may not be accurate because it sometimes does not detect recent use • Tests for amphetamine/methamphetamine have a high degree of cross-reactivity to other sympathomimetics like ephedrine or pseudoephedrine Continue Opioid Therapy • If there is • Improvement in ADLs and psychosocial functioning • Effective pain relief • Lack of significant side effects • Lack of aberrant behaviors •If not, consider exit strategy • Tapering off opioids Opioid Exit Strategy • Discontinue opioids • Test positive for illicit drug use • Threatening behavior to provider/staff • Forgery/diversion • Drug OD/intoxication • Tapering off opioids • Gradual • 10% dose reduction every 1-4 weeks • Rapid (sig side effects) • 25% dose reduction every 3-7 days CDC Opioid Guidelines 2022 - #5 • Unless there are indications of a life-threatening issue such as warning signs of impending overdose (e.g., confusion, sedation, or slurred speech), opioid therapy should not be discontinued abruptly, • and clinicians should not rapidly reduce opioid dosages from higher dosages (recommendation category: B; evidence type: 4). When to Refer to Pain Specialist • > 90 mg/day of morphine (oral) equivalent • History of abuse or drug-related offenses • Failure to improve with multiple trials of medications • Selected patients who could benefit from invasive therapy, ie. steroid epidural injection • Consider psychological evaluation in all patients Thank you! Answers 1. A 2. A 3. A 4. D 5. B 6. A 7. B

stem: How much daily vitamin D supplementation do infants need during the first month of life? supplementation should have 200 IU of Vitamin D daily month of life Vitamin D • Breastmilk contains small amounts of vitamin D • ALL infants should have 400 IU vitamin D/day starting in first few days of life • Formula-fed infants can stop when taking 1 L or 32 oz. vitamin D-fortified infant formula • Breastfed infants should continue until on vit D-fortified milk • Alternatively, Breastfeeding mothers can take 6400 IU of vit D Prevention of Rickets and Vitamin D Deficiency in Infants, Children, and Adolescents. Carol L. Wagner, Frank R. Greer, and the Section on Breastfeeding and Committee on Nutrition. Pediatrics. November 2008; 122:5 1142-1152. Iron Supplementation • Breastfed infants – 1 mg/kg/day of a liquid iron supplement from 4 months until 1 year or adequate iron-containing solid foods • Formula-fed infants – use iron-fortified formula • Premature infants (<37 weeks) • Breastfeeding – begin iron 2 mg/kg/day • Iron-fortified formula feeding – begin iron 1/mg/kg/day • Start at 1 month of age until 1 year Breastfeeding • 6 months of exclusive breastfeeding recommended by the AAP • After complementary foods introduced at 6 months, continued breastfeeding as long as mutually desired for 2 + years • Infants with skin-to-skin at birth breastfeed longer and more effectively • Professional and layperson support increases breastfeeding rates • Adequate milk supply should be monitored by weighing before and after feeding (1 g weight = 1 mL milk intake) • Frenotomy for ankyloglossia reduces breastfeeding–related nipple pain Westerfield KL, Koenig K, Oh R. Breastfeeding: Common Questions and Answers. Am Fam Physician. 2018 Sep 15;98(6):368- 373. PMID: 30215910. Breastfeeding Infant Benefits: decreased risk of… • Childhood leukemia • Obesity • Hypertension • Otitis media • Necrotizing enterocolitis • Asthma • Acute diarrheal disease • Atopic dermatitis • Inflammatory bowel disease • Sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) • Lower respiratory illness • Type 1 and 2 diabetes 16 Breastfeeding Maternal Benefits: Decreased risk of… Few contraindications • Breast cancer • Infant Galactosemia • Ovarian cancer • Maternal HIV • Endometrial cancer • Untreated brucellosis • Type 2 diabetes mellitus • Ebola virus • Hypertension • Rare Medications/chemotherapy • Maternal substance use 17 Feeding Pearls • Formula- should contain iron, at room temperature, not in microwave • Switch to Vitamin D fortified cow’s milk at 1 year • Soy: not as allergenic, vegetarian • Starting foods • Cereals (4-6 months) • Vegetables and fruits, then table foods • No honey for first year • If at high risk for peanut allergy –introduce peanut containing foods at 4-6 months Other Nutrition Pearls • Stools 1-3/day • Wet diapers ≥ 6/day • Regain birth weight by 14 days • Acceptable weight loss in first 2 weeks of life is < 10% • Double birth weight by 4-6 months, triple at 1 year
choice_A: Breastfed infants should have 400 IU of Vitamin D daily
choice_B: Milk-based formula fed infants do not need Vitamin D
choice_C: Infants fed combined breastmilk and milk-based formula
choice_D: No Vitamin D supplementation is needed during the first

stem: Following a scheduled repeat C-section delivery, a term infant has a respiratory rate >60. What is the treatment for the most common cause of this condition? Respiratory Issues Transient tachypnea of newborn (TTN) respiratory rate > 60/min • Most common cause of respiratory distress (> 40%) • Diagnosis of exclusion • CXR—diffuse parenchymal infiltrates and fluid in the pulmonary fissures • 93% resolve in 2 hours; may last for 72 hours • Treatment is supportive (oxygen and nasal CPAP) Respiratory Issues Other common causes: • Respiratory distress syndrome in premature infants • Meconium aspiration • Pneumonia • Pneumothorax Newborn Respiratory Distress Am Fam Physician. 2015 Dec 1;92(11):994-1002 Heart Murmurs • Most are transitional and benign • Usually not associated with other signs or symptoms • Pathologic murmurs • Grade III or louder • Harsh • Continuous • Abnormal second heart sound • Diastolic • Other signs or symptoms –poor feeding, cyanosis
choice_C: Intubation and suction immediately after delivery

stem: Breastfeeding jaundice is characterized by: Physiologic Jaundice • Newborns conjugate bilirubin slower, have higher RBC turnover, and have decreased excretion • Term infants • rapid rise of serum bilirubin in range of 3 to 12 mg/dL • peaks at 3-5 days • declines over 2 weeks • Preterm infants • rapid rise of serum bilirubin up to 15 mg/dL • decline to normal can take up to a month Neonatal Hyperbilirubinemia • Hyperbilirubinemia definition: • Total serum or plasma bilirubin >95th percentile on the hour-specific Bhutani nomogram • Screening recommendation: • American Academy of Pediatrics –universal newborn screening with transcutaneous bilirubin, followed by blood draw OR targeted screening Kemper AR, Newman TB, Slaughter JL, et al. Clinical Practice Guideline Revision: Management of Hyperbilirubinemia in the Newborn Infant 35 or More Weeks of Gestation. Pediatrics. 2022;150(3):e2022058859 Severe Hyperbilirubinemia • Severe neonatal hyperbilirubinemia is defined as a Total Bilirubin >20 mg/dL • Associated with an increased risk for bilirubin-induced neurologic dysfunction (BIND), which occurs when bilirubin crosses the blood-brain barrier and binds to brain tissue • Acute bilirubin encephalopathy (ABE) – acute manifestations of BIND • Kernicterus – chronic and permanent sequelae of BIND Severe Hyperbilirubinemia Risk Factors • Pre-discharge bilirubin close to • Macrosomic infant of diabetic mother phototherapy threshold • Cephalohematoma/significant bruising • Jaundice observed in first 24 hours • Hemolytic disease or ABO incompatibility • Lower gestational age –risk increases • Often have rapid rise of > 0.3 mg/dl per hour in first with each additional week < 40 24 hours or > 0.2 mg/dL per hour after 24 hours • Phototherapy before discharge • Family history or genetic ancestry suggestive of • Parent or sibling received phototherapy inherited RBC disorders –including glucose-6- and/or exchange transfusion phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency • Exclusive breastfeeding with suboptimal • One of the most important causes of hazardous hyperbilirubinemia leading to kernicterus globally intake • 13% of AA males and 4% AA females have G6PD • Down syndrome deficiency Neurotoxicity Risk Factors • Albumin < 3.0 • Isoimmune hemolytic disease (Coombs test positive) • G6PD Deficiency • Positive direct antiglobulin test if Rh(D) immune globulin not given during pregnancy • Prematurity < 38 weeks • Sepsis • Significant clinical instability in the previous 24 hours Breastfeeding Jaundice (now Breastmilk called “suboptimal intake Jaundice hyperbilirubinemia”) Characteristic Exaggeration of physiologic Prolongation of physiologic jaundice jaundice caused by a factor in human milk Onset 2-5 days of age 5 to 10 days of age Duration Transient –up to 10 days Persists up to 3 months Feeding Pattern Insufficientintake of breastmilk Frequent feeds (less than 8 feeds/day) Stool Pattern Infrequent Normal Other Interrupting breastfeeding ↓ Serum bilirubin returns to normal chances of success without stopping breastfeeding Treatment of Hyperbilirubinemia • Treat the underlying cause • Increase breastfeeding if insufficient – 8+ times in 24 hours • Risk stratify to determine total bilirubin level at which to initiate interventions • Use validated tool to calculate level for intervention and escalation of care • Phototherapy – decreases the need for exchange transfusion • Exchange transfusion used for signs of encephalopathy at any bilirubin level • If jaundice persists beyond 2-3 weeks, consider other causes Failure to Stool 70% of infants pass meconium in first 12 hours of life After 24 hours consider secondary causes • Hirschsprung’s • Imperforate anus/tight anus or fistula • Cystic fibrosis • Necrotizing enterocolitis • Most common cause of acute intestinal obstruction and septic abdomen in neonates Constipation Treatments • Rectal stimulation with thermometer • Glycerin suppositories • 100% fruit juice (apple, pear, prune) • Miralax (polyethylene glycol) if > 6 months – most effective treatment • High-fiber foods if older (cereals, fruits, vegetables) to prevent constipation • However, increasing water and/or fiber does not treat constipation Gastroesophageal Reflux Gastroesophageal reflux (GER) • Normal exam and weight gain, no symptoms besides spitting up • Treatment is reassurance Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) • Can be characterized by poor weight gain/loss, pain with feeding, chronic cough/wheezing Baird DC, Harker DJ, Karmes AS: Diagnosis and treatment of gastroesophageal reflux in infants and children. Am Fam Physician 2015;92(8):705-714. Gastroesophageal Reflux - Treatment • Thickened feeds associated with fewer episodes of regurgitation – first line treatment • Histamine H2 blockers are an option • Poor evidence for PPIs in infants • Trial of soy or hydrolyzed formula in formula fed infants • Maternal diet changes in breastfed infants Pyloric Stenosis • Over first 2-8 weeks of life, boys > girls, white > black/Asian • Nonbilious and projectile vomiting • Infant appears hungry and feeds often • Other causes of vomiting • Overfeeding • Reflux - small amounts of vomiting; normal weight gain • Midgut volvulus: bilious vomiting with “double-bubble” sign • Gastroenteritis: fever, diarrhea • CNS injury: other neurological signs Infantile Colic • Unknown cause • Uncontrolled crying using the “rule of three” • Crying more than three hours per day • More than three days per week • For longer than three weeks • Appropriate weight gain and normal exam Infantile Colic: Recognition and Treatment. Am Fam Physician. 2015 Oct 1;92(7):577-582. Infantile Colic-Treatment • Elimination of allergens (e.g., cow's milk, eggs, fish, peanuts, soy, tree nuts, wheat) from the diet of breastfeeding mothers may relieve colic symptoms • The probiotic Lactobacillus reuteri (strain DSM 17938) may reduce crying in breastfeeding but not formula-fed infants with colic. • Switching formula-fed infants to a hydrolyzed formula may improve colic symptoms. • Majority outgrow this by 6 months Undescended Testes • More common in premature boys • Most will descend by 6 months • Refer by 6 months if still not able to palpate – imaging NOT recommended prior to referral • If needed, surgery will help preserve fertility • Testicular atrophy is complication of surgery • Higher risk of testicular cancer (decreased, but not eliminated by surgery) Nguyen V, Ngo L, Jaqua EE. Cryptorchidism (Undescended Testicle). Am Fam Physician. 2023 Oct;108(4):378-385. Herpes Simplex Manifests one of three ways: 1/3 each • Localized to skin, eyes, and mouth • Localized central nervous system disease • Disseminated disease involving multiple organs, primarily liver and lungs (60% mortality) Treatment • IV acyclovir 10-20 mg/kg q8 hours (preemie 10 mg/kg)for 2-3 weeks Hepatitis B HBsAg+ mother Mother’s status unknown • Hepatitis B immune globulin • Hepatitis B vaccine given within 12 within12 hours of birth hours of age • Hepatitis B vaccine within 12 • Mother tested for Hepatitis B, and if hours positive, HBIG given as soon as possible (prior to 1 week)
choice_B: Persistence longer than 1 month of life

stem: What advice would you give parents to reduce the risk of death from sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) in their infant? Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS) • Peak incidence at 2-3 months of age • Increased risk associated with • Prone position • Bed sharing (particularly with impaired adults) • Cigarette smoke exposure • Protective → pacifiers, fans, breastfeeding • Racial disparities persist Sleep – AAP Recommendations • Back to sleep for every sleep • “It is recommended that infants sleep in the parents’ room, close to • Skin-to-skin care for at least the the parents’ bed, but on a separate first hour of life for healthy surface designed for infants, newborns ideally for at least the first 6 • Firm, safe, flat sleep surface months” • Breastfeeding recommended • Discontinue swaddling when • Return infant to own bed after shows signs of rolling over breastfeeding • No naps in car seats, strollers • Avoid overheating and head coverings Moon R, Carlin R. Sleep-Related Infant Deaths: Updated 2022 Recommendations for Reducing Infant Deaths in the Sleep Environment. Pediatrics(2022) 150 (1): e2022057990
choice_B: Avoid exposure of the infant to second-hand smoke
choice_C: Place baby in the prone position for sleep
choice_D: Discourage the use of a pacifier

stem: The majority of failure to thrive cases in infants is due to: Failure to Thrive (now Growth Faltering) • Inadequate physical growth diagnosed by observation of growth over time using standard growth chart • WHO growth charts ages 0-2 • CDC growth charts ages 2-20 • Occurs in 5-10% primary care practices • Weight falls below the 5th percentile, or crosses two major percentile lines • Most common cause inadequate caloric intake Growth Faltering Etiology Inadequate caloric intake Increased metabolism • Improperly prepared formula • Hyperthyroidism • Reflux disease • Chronic infection (HIV) • Neglect • Hypoxemia/congenital cardiac disease • Mechanical feeding problems • Congenital lung disease • Cleft lip/palate • Cystic fibrosis • Poor latch • Renal disease Inadequate absorption • Celiac disease or milk allergy • Vitamin or mineral deficiencies • Biliary atresia or liver disease Failure to Thrive Treatment • Aimed at underlying cause • High-calorie diet and effective feeding behaviors • Further testing • Complete Blood Count • Chemistry panel • Erythrocyte sedimentation rate • Urinalysis • Hospitalization rarely indicated Lacrimal Duct Obstruction • Persistent tearing, usually unilateral • Can try “milking” the duct • Antibiotics or steroids are not indicated • Usually resolves by 6 months • Treat if not resolved in 1 year – referral to ophthalmologist Positional Plagiocephaly • Unilateral occiput flattening • Due to continuous supine positioning • Fixed by routine switching of head position and supervised “tummy time” daily • Check for torticollis Frequent Flyers • Hip dysplasia screening • Breastfeeding • Hyperbilirubinemia • Vitamin D • SIDS prevention Answers 1. A 2. D 3. C 4. B 5. D

To age 5 years • Vision screening for all children once between ages 3-5 to detect amblyopia or its risk factors • Obesity screening – ages 6-17 • Skin cancer prevention counseling – ages 6 months – 24 years Maternal Depression • Use standardized depression instrument • Patient Health Questionnaire-2 (PHQ-2) or Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale • Screen all postpartum women at 1,2,4 and 6 month well child visits • USPSTF B recommendation Dental Care • Dental caries are most common chronic disease of young children • Brush twice with fluoride toothpaste daily as soon as teeth erupt • “Rice-sized" amount of toothpaste until age 2 • Then “pea-sized” amount • Floss as soon as teeth are touching • Dental home no later than age 3 U.S. Preventive Services Task Force Prevention of Dental 0.3 ppm F 0.3-0.6 ppm F > 0.6ppm F Birth -6 months 0 0 0 6 months -3 years 0.25 mg 0 0 3 -6 years 0.50 mg 0.25 mg 0 6 years up to at least 1.00 mg 0.50 mg 0 16 years abnormal red reflex Amblyopia due to strabismus is the leading cause of monocular vision loss in children • AAP recommends screening yearly beginning age 4, “instrument-based” at 12 and 24 months Obesity Screening Screen for obesity in children and adolescents age > 6 years and offer/refer them to comprehensive, intensive behavioral interventions (>25 hours of face-to-face, family based, multicomponent treatment over 3-12 months) to promote improvements in weight • 19.7% of children and adolescents ages 2 to 19 years in the United States have obesity (defined as an age- and sex-specific body mass index [BMI] in the ≥95th percentile). • 32% of children and adolescents are overweight (defined as an age- and sex-specific BMI in the 85th to 94th percentile) or have obesity. Obesity Associated morbidity • Mental health and psychological issues • Asthma • Obstructive sleep apnea • Orthopedic problems • Adverse cardiovascular and metabolic factors (e.g., high blood pressure, abnormal lipid levels, and insulin resistance). Which of the following is true of postpartum depression? week postpartum to severe PPD the risk of the subsequent development of PPD Peripartum Mood Disturbances Why is this important? The prevalence of depression is similar for pregnant and non-pregnant women. Pregnancy is NOT protective! “Baby Blues” • Common: 26 to 85% (not pathologic) • Temporary: 1-2 weeks • Signs/Symptoms • Tearfulness, excessive worry, irritability, mood swings, sleep problems • Treatment • Time, support, and encouragement to ask for help • An independent risk factor for postpartum depression, especially if symptoms are severe Postpartum Depression • An episode of MDD occurring up to one year after pregnancy • Appears most often within the first 3 months following delivery • 15% prevalence • High rates of comorbid anxiety and psychomotor agitation • Distressing and unwanted thoughts about harming the infant • Predisposing factors: Psychosocial Biological • History of childhood trauma • Personal history of depression or • Poor support system PPD • Low SES • Depression during pregnancy • Stressful events during pregnancy • Young maternal age • Interpersonal violence • Family history of depression or • Colicky infant bipolar disorder • Marital conflict • Substance use disorder Postpartum Depression: Treatment Treatment is the same as that for major depression • Mild symptoms: Nonpharmacotherapyis preferred as first-line • Moderate-severe symptoms: Pharmacotherapy + nonpharmacotherapy • Informed consent (individualized risk:benefitanalysis) • Use what has worked in the past • Relapse risk 5x higher if an antidepressant is discontinued during pregnancy • Monotherapy is preferable • Escitalopram • Fluoxetine NEW • Sertraline Zuranolone: Analogue of neuroactive • Mirtazapine progesterone metabolite • Venlafaxine • Monitor, re-evaluate, and adjust treatment as needed Postpartum Psychosis • A medical emergency • Risk Factors: • History of bipolar disorder or previous postpartum psychosis • Occurrence: Rare (0.1-0.2%) • Onset: Sudden with a rapid deterioration • Often on days 1-3 post-partum, but usually within 2-12 weeks postpartum • Signs/Symptoms • Bizarre disorganized behavior, lack of insight, delusions about infant • Self-neglect, sleep disturbance, agitation • Treatment: inpatient hospitalization is usually indicated Key Points for the Exam • Screening for depression • Children: NO • Adolescents and adults: YES • Treatment of depression • Continue treatment for 4-9m following remission for a first episode of MDD • “Maintenance” treatment is indicated if there is a history of 3 or more episodes of depression • Adequate trial of antidepressant: 4-6 weeks • Pregnancy is NOT protective for depression • Antidepressants are OK in lactation Answers 1. A 2. C 3. C 4. B 5. B

or mind going blank • Irritability • Muscle tension • Sleep disturbance Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD) • Epidemiology • 5% prevalence in community samples • 2:1 female/male ratio • Age of onset is frequently in childhood or adolescence • Chronic but fluctuating course of illness • Worsened during stressful periods • Treatment • Pharmacotherapy: SSRI or SNRI • Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) In RCTs, drug vs. placebo, which of the following treatments has failed to demonstrate benefit in the treatment of generalized anxiety disorder? Treatment: Antidepressants • SSRI and SNRIs are first-line agents for the management panic disorder and other anxiety disorders • Broad spectrum of efficacy against common comorbidities • When effective, the patient stops experiencing symptoms of anxiety • Overall well-tolerated and safe in overdose • Lack of concern about benzodiazepine-associated dependence and abuse liability Treatment: Antidepressants • Treatment strategies • The starting dose should be low • Sertraline 25 mg qd • Venlafaxine XL 37.5mg qd • Gradual up-titration starting after about a week • Average effective dose of SSRI and SNRI are in the typical antidepressant dose range • Benefit may be delayed for 4-6 weeks • May wish to “cover this 4-6 weeks with a long-acting scheduled benzodiazepine Treatment: Benzodiazepines • Benefits • Effective • Quick onset of action • Promote sleep • Risks • Possible tolerance and withdrawal with chronic use • Potential for misuse • Sedation • Ataxia and falls • Memory impairment • Treatment: Benzodiazepines • The use of a benzodiazepine as monotherapy for a chronic anxiety disorder is very rarely appropriate • Risks of benzodiazepine use without the benefit of sustained recovery • Presents a double-bind: • Scheduled dosing places the patient at risk for tolerance, escalating doses, and withdrawal (if discontinued) • PRN dosing allows the patient to suffer anxiety Treatment: Buspirone • FDA-approved for GAD • Advantages Buspirone Dose Range • Safety profile Initial dose: 5-7.5mg bid • Lack of dependence/withdrawal PO Target dose: 30-60mg daily, in divided doses • Lack of sexual dysfunction • Disadvantages • Delayed response Treatment: Hydroxyzine • FDA approved for… • “Anxiety and tension” • “Acute disturbance/hysteria” Hydroxyzine • “Antianxiety adjunct in organic Dose Range disease” Label: 50 to 100 mg 4 times • Advantages daily (scheduled or PRN) PO Off-label: 37.5 to 75 mg daily in • Little abuse potential divided doses (scheduled or • No dependence or withdrawal PRN) • Disadvantages • Anticholinergic effects • Sedating Treatment: Propranolol • Often prescribed only on PRN basis for performance- related anxiety • Not effective for GAD • Administer test dose to assess for drug effect on blood pressure Propranolol Off-label: Immediate-release PO formulations: 40mg 60-90 minutes prior to anxiety-provoking event Key Points for the Exam • Bipolar Disorder I has mania and bipolar disorder II as hypomania • Avoid antidepressants as monotherapy in bipolar disorder I and II • Valproate is to be avoided in women of childbearing age due to concerns of teratogenesis • SSRIs are considered first-line pharmacotherapy for anxiety disorders • Cognitive behavioral therapy is considered first-line evidence-based psychotherapy for anxiety disorders Answers 1. A 2. A 3. D 4. C Supplementary Slides Personality Disorders • Pattern of behavior that causes problems • Behavior is rigid and lifelong • Patient experiences little anxiety (ego-syntonic) • Seldom seek treatment for personality disorder • Etiology unclear – Maladaptive patterns are the result of dysfunctional early environments that prevent the evolution of adaptive patterns – Some evidence points to a genetic basis – Diagnosis should not be made until age 18 years and personality development is complete Personality Disorders: Cluster A (Odd, Eccentric) • Paranoid—suspicious • Schizoid—loner • Schizotypal—odd loner • General principles – Uncomfortable in interpersonal situations, emotionally distant, hard to engage – Do not respond appropriately to cues – May not come in due to fear of personal contact • Respect patient's need for personal distance – Convey info in a clear, straightforward fashion – Do not directly challenge strange ideas about health Personality Disorders: Cluster B (Dramatic, Emotional, Erratic) • Antisocial: breaks rules, feels no guilt • Borderline: suicidal gestures, unstable relationships, splitting • Histrionic: dramatic, somatic complaints • Narcissistic: feel entitled, insensitive to others feelings • General tendencies – Demanding, manipulative, unstable – Personally inappropriate; cross boundaries • Physicians must be aware of their own emotions – Avoid familiarity; set clear limits – Schedule regular visits Personality Disorders: Cluster C (Anxious, Fearful) • Avoidant—shy • Dependent—stays in harmful relationships • Obsessive/compulsive (not OCD)—perfectionistic, isolation of affect • General principles: – Underlying anxiety may significantly interfere with the doctor-patient relationship – Provide reassurance; validate concerns; encourage participation, symptom reporting – Schedule regular visits – Set reasonable limits on time, expectations Personality Disorders: Treatment Options • Psychotherapy: core treatment – Psychodynamic – Interpersonal – Cognitive – Group – Dialectical (skill-based, coping) • Medication: symptomatic – Antidepressants: SSRIs, nefazodone, mirtazapine; avoid TCA/MAOIs (OD risk) – Anticonvulsants (Mood stabilizers) – Antipsychotics (atypical) Schizophrenia • Rates are equal in males and females – Onset in the teens and 20s – 2% prevalence – 10% lifetime suicide risk • Requires 6-month history of impaired functioning – Work, education, self-care • Characterized by disorganized thought (bizarre speech, behavior), loose associations – Word salad—words do not relate Schizophrenia • Symptoms can be positive (e.g., hallucinations) or negative (e.g., social withdrawal) • Social/family interventions improve outcomes • Treatment: dopamine D blockers (2nd generation antipsychotics) 2 – Noncompliance rates are high and equivalent across medication classes – Delays in treatment worsen long-term outcomes 2nd Generation Antipsychotics • Clozapine (Clozaril) 100-900 mg/day • Aripiprazole (Abilify) 10-30 mg/day • Olanzapine (Zyprexa) 2.5-20 mg/day • Iloperidone (Fanapt) 12-24 mg/day • Quetiapine (Seroquel) 25-1000 • Lurasidone (Latuda) 40-80 mg/day mg/day • Asenapine (Saphris) 10-20 mg/day • Risperidone (Risperdal) 1-6 mg/day • Brexpiprazole (Rexulti) 0.5–4 • Ziprasidone (Geodon) 20-200 mg/day mg/day • Cariprazine (Vraylar) 1.5-6 mg/day • Paliperidone (Invega) 3-12 mg/day • Pimavanserin (Nuplazid) 34 • Lumateperone (Caplyta) 42 mg/day mg/day 2nd Generation Antipsychotics • Have low D receptor binding and blockade, plus serotonin receptor blockade 2 • Fewer extrapyramidal side effects • Less increase in prolactin • Reduced risk of tardive dyskinesia • Increased risk of weight gain • Increased risk of metabolic syndromes, monitor glucose & lipids Key Points for the Exam • Personality disorders: psychotherapy first-line treatment • Seldom seek treatment for personality disorders; are “ego- syntonic” • 2nd generation antipsychotics (atypicals) reduce the risk of TD and EPS; but may increase the risk of metabolic syndrome

inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms are present in two or more settings • There is clear evidence that the symptoms interfere with or reduce the quality of social, academic, or occupational hyperactive/impulsive Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder Diagnosis • Meets DSM-5 TR criteria • Various checklists ‾ Conners Comprehensive Behavior Rating Scales (ages 6-18) ‾ Vanderbilt Rating Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder Development and Course • Childhood • Main manifestation is hyperactivity • Difficult to distinguish from highly variable normative behaviors before age 5-6 • Adolescence • Inattention becomes more prominent • Signs of hyperactivity are less common and may be confined to an inner feeling of restlessness or impatience • Adulthood • Symptoms of motoric hyperactivity continue to be less obvious • Difficulties with restlessness, inattention, poor planning, and impulsivity persist Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder Epidemiology - Adults • Prevalence in adults aged 19–45 years at 2.5% • Longitudinal studies following children with ADHD document a general decline in symptoms as one ages • 15% of patients will persist with full diagnostic ADHD Treatment • The optimal treatment approach for children with ADHD is medication with behavior modification • Multimodal Treatment Study of Children with ADHD Randomly assigned to 4 groups 1. Medication 2. Behavior modification 3. Combined 4. Neither • Combined approach superior in all areas • Medication alone was superior to behavior modification alone ADHD Treatment: Medications Stimulants Nonstimulants Methylphenidate NE reuptake inhibitors • Short-acting (Ritalin) • Viloxazine • Long-acting (Concerta) • Atomoxetine Amphetamine Alpha-2 agonists • Short-acting (Adderall) • Guanfacine ER • Long-acting (Adderall XR, • Clonidine ER Vyvanse) ADHD Treatment: Stimulant Medications Mechanism • Methylphenidate: blocks presynaptic dopamine and norepinephrine transporters • Amphetamine: blocks presynaptic dopamine and norepinephrine transporters but also increases presynaptic efflux of dopamine Efficacy • Children and adolescents • ES of amphetamine 0.99-1.02 • ES of methylphenidate 0.72-0.78 • Adults • ES of amphetamine 0.49 • ES of methylphenidate 0.79 ADHD Treatment: Stimulant Medications • Adverse effects • The side-effect profiles of all stimulants are similar • Amphetamine might be more prone to side-effects • Side effects more common in younger children Most common side-effects Other concerns • Appetite suppression • Growth trajectories • Insomnia • Substance use • Dry mouth • Cardiovascular risks • Nausea ADHD Treatment: Stimulant Medications • AAP did not support the AHA recommendation that EKG be performed in ALL patients in advance of stimulant use • Testing (EKG) should be performed: ‾ Family history of sudden cardiac death ‾ Patient reports chest pain, shortness of breath, syncope/dizziness either before or after medication use ‾ Abnormal examination findings ADHD Treatment: Non-Stimulant Medications Indications • Typically reserved for patients who respond poorly or have intolerable side-effects to stimulants, or have a substance abuse history • Not controlled substances Efficacy • Atomoxetine • Children: ES 0.64 • Adults: ES 0.33 • Guanfacine • Children: ES 0.5-0.6 • Adults: ? ADHD Treatment: Behavioral • The evidence for behavior management supports its use in children with ADHD in early and middle childhood • Parent training • Classroom management • Routine • Behavioral interventions use contingent behavior to promote positive responses • Parent-directed behavioral management is the recommended first- line intervention for preschoolers with ADHD ADHD Treatment: Behavioral For adults, there is less evidence of the effectiveness of behavioral treatments Some suggestions include • Organize home and office items, tasks, and schedule • To-do lists • Routine • The book Driven to Distraction

stem: Research has shown that the optimal treatment approach for children with ADHD is: Autism Spectrum Disorders In DSM-5, four separate diagnoses are combined into a single condition known as Autism Spectrum Disorders They previously were: 1. Autistic Disorder 2. Asperger’s Disorder 3. Childhood Disintegrative Disorder 4. Rett Syndrome Autism Spectrum Disorders Diagnostic Criteria A neurodevelopmental disorder defined by social communication impairments and restricted, repetitive behaviors Social Communication Restricted, Repetitive Behaviors Impairments Stereotyped or repetitive motor movements, use of Deficits in social-emotional reciprocity objects, or speech Insistence on sameness, inflexible adherence to routines, or ritualized patterns of behavior Deficits in nonverbal communicative behaviors used for social interaction Highly restricted, fixated interests that are abnormal in intensity or focus Deficits in developing, maintaining, and understanding Hyper-or hyporeactivity to sensory input or unusual relationships interest in sensory aspects of the environment Autism Spectrum Disorders (ASD) • Epidemiology • Among children aged 8 years in the US, the estimated prevalence of ASD has increased from approximately 1.1% in 2008 to 2.3% in 2018 • Potential explanations for increased prevalence • Changes in diagnostic criteria (broader) • Increased awareness • Greater access to services • Prevalence of ASD is higher in boys (3.7%) than in girls (0.9-1.5%) at 8 years of age • Possible reasons for increased prevalence in boys • Girls are better able to minimize ASD symptoms (“camouflaging”) • Diagnostic tools are less sensitive to the presence of ASD among females Autism Spectrum Disorders • Pathogenesis and Pathophysiology • A variety of genetic and environmental factors have been associated with ASD, but none are specific • Estimated heritability: 81% • Environmental factors: 14-22% • Medication use during pregnancy and risk of ASD • Valproic acid: 4.4% with exposures during pregnancy compared with the risk of 1.5% without exposure • Antidepressant: Studies are confounded by the indication for antidepressant use and the association between maternal depression and ASD • Vaccines: A large body of research refutes any linkage between vaccines and ASD Autism Spectrum Disorders ytidibromoC ADHD 28% in ASD vs 8.5% in the general population Intellectual disability 23% in ASD vs 0.7% in the general population Anxiety disorder(s) 20% in ASD vs 7% in the general population Depressive disorder(s) 11% in ASD vs 5% in the general population Epilepsy 21% in people with ASD and intellectual disability vs 0.8% in general population 8% in those with only ASD vs 0.8% in general population Constipation 22% in ASD Sleep problems 13% in ASD vs 5% in the general population Autism Spectrum Disorders • Screening • AAP recommends that all children be screened at 18 and 24 months of age for ASD • USPSTF concluded that evidence was insufficient to recommend screening for ASD in young children for whom no concerns were raised by their parents • Screening tools • The Modified-Checklist for Autism in Toddlers, Revised (M-CHAT-R) • Children whose M-CHAT-R total score is higher than 2 are considered at risk for ASD and require follow-up questions (M-CHAT-R/F) Autism Spectrum Disorders • Diagnostic Assessment • The gold standard is clinical consensus, defined as “agreement within a multidisciplinary professional team based on a detailed developmental history and observation of the individual’s behaviors using standardized diagnostic tools” • Autism Diagnostic Interview, Revised (ADI-R): a semi-structured interview with the parent(s) • Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule-2 (ADOS-2): a semi-structured direct observation of a child’s behavior • The AAP recommends genetic testing for individuals who are diagnosed with ASD Autism Spectrum Disorders • Behavioral Interventions • Early intervention based on well-established behavioral analytic principles • Focus on behavior change based on understanding and changing the predisposing environmental conditions or events that reinforce specific behaviors • Associated with an improvement in children’s language, play, and social communication • At least 25 hours per week • In children who have no or mild cognitive impairment, social deficits and friendship quality typically worsen during adolescence • Social skills training delivered in a group format is associated with modest effects on social competence Autism Spectrum Disorders • Pharmacotherapy • Risperidone and aripiprazole are currently approved by the FDA for irritability and aggression in patients with ASD • Risperidone: ES 1.10 • Aripiprazole: ES 1.20 • Stimulants (methylphenidate) and nonstimulants (atomoxetine and guanfacine) have demonstrated effectiveness in ADHD symptoms in ASD. Methylphenidate: ES 0.6 Atomoxetine: ES 0.5 Guanfacine: ES 1.2 However, people with both ADHD and ASD are more likely to experience behavioral activation with psychostimulants compared with individuals without ASD Autism Spectrum Disorders • Prognosis • Adults with ASD less frequently live independently and are more frequently unemployed than people without ASD • Better cognitive abilities during childhood are associated with higher levels of independence, education, and employment later in life but are not associated with higher rates of friendship or well-being reported by caregivers • Premature mortality rates are approximately 2-fold higher for individuals with ASD than for the general population Obsessive Compulsive Disorder (OCD) Diagnostic Criteria • Presence of obsessions, compulsions, or both • The obsessions or compulsions are time-consuming (e.g., take more than 1 hour per day) or cause clinically significant distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning Obsessive Compulsive Disorder (OCD) Diagnostic Criteria • Obsessions • Recurrent thoughts, impulses, or images that cause anxiety or distress • These thoughts, impulses, or images are experienced as intrusive and go beyond excessive worry about real-life problems • They are not related to another mental disorder • Compulsions • Ritualistic behaviors or mental acts that are performed in response to an obsession • These behaviors are excessive and performed to decrease anxiety or distress or avoid some dreaded event • They are not realistically connected to those dreaded events OCD: Common Symptoms Compulsions Obsessions • Checking 62% • Contamination 48% • Washing 46% • Doubt 47% • Need for symmetry 40% • Symmetry 45% • Counting 30% • Fear of aggression 36% • Somatic obsessions 35% (Hoarding is now a separate • Sexual obsessions 22% disorder in DSM-5) Obsessive Compulsive Disorder (OCD) • Epidemiology • Lifetime prevalence: 1-2% • Adults: men and women are equally affected • Children: boys>girls • Mean age of onset Males: 6-15 years of age Females: 20-29 years of age • Comorbidity • MDD: 67% • Tourette’s Disorder: 5-7% Obsessive Compulsive Disorder (OCD) • Treatment • Antidepressants (SSRIs or clomipramine) • High-dose • Delayed-onset of action • Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) Key Points for the Exam • Comorbidity is common in ADHD • ADHD must be present in more than 1 setting • Medication plus behavior modification: best ADHD treatment • ADHD adults are primarily inattentive • Treatment of OCD often requires high-doses of serotonergic medications Answers 1. D 2. A 3. B 4. B Supplementary Slides Oppositional Defiant Disorder (ODD) • Pattern of negativism, hostility, defiance • Angry/irritable mood: ‾ Often loses temper ‾ Touchy & easily annoyed ‾ Angry & resentful • Argumentative/defiant ‾ Often argues with adults ‾ Deliberately annoys ‾ Blames others ‾ Actively defies rules • Vindictiveness ‾ Spiteful and vindictive, holds grudges Treatment of ODD • There is no medication for ODD • There is medication for comorbid conditions that may exacerbate ODD • Parent training, behavioral interventions Conduct Disorder • A repetitive and persistent pattern of behavior in which the basic rights of others or major societal norms are violated for at least 6 months. Conduct Disorder • Aggression to people and animals • Destruction of property • Deceitfulness or theft • Serious violations of rules • The disturbance causes clinically significant impairment • Difficult to treat ‾ May require off-label mood stabilizers ‾ May require inpatient treatment, if severe Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder (DMDD) • New Diagnosis in DSM-5 • Non-episodic temper outbursts > 3x a week • Persistently irritable or angry mood in all settings • Diagnosis only made between the ages of 6 and 18 • No symptoms of mania or hypomania Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder (DMDD) • No treatment guidelines yet • Mood stabilizers sometimes helpful • Treat comorbid conditions (ADHD, etc.) • Behavioral and family interventions have been used Specific Learning Disorder • Significant discrepancy between school level, chronological age, intelligence, and academic performance or achievement • This discrepancy significantly interferes with academic and/or social functioning • Treatment: educational modifications • Medication: only if a comorbidity exists that requires medication use Common Learning Disorder Deficits • Developmental speech/language disorders • Articulation, receptive/expressive language • Academic skills disorders • Dyslexia (reading), dyscalculia (math), dysgraphia (writing) • Nonverbal learning disability • Visual-spatial, visual-motor, sensory, motor • Central auditory processing deficit • Distorted or incomplete auditory messages

A 52-year-old patient presents with progressive cough, wheezing and occasional sputum production over the past few years. They have smoked 1 pack of cigarettes per day for 20 years, and their symptoms have worsened lately so that they often feel too short of breath to play with their grandkids. Which one of the following spirometry results would be consistent with a diagnosis of moderate COPD? 1 1 1 1 1 1 1 1 Strategy for Assessing COPD 1. Assess airflow limitation using spirometry 2. Assess symptoms 3. Assess risk of exacerbations 4. Assess comorbidities COPD Diagnosis • Spirometry is the key test to diagnose airway obstruction! • Recommended for all symptomatic adults • NOT recommended as a screening test for asymptomatic patients regardless of COPD risk factors • Reproducible, objective, non-invasive, and readily available • Key features • FEV - volume of air expired in 1 second after a full inspiration 1 • FVC - maximum volume of air exhaled after a full inspiration • Post-bronchodilator FEV /FVC <0.70 confirms COPD 1 Spirometry: Flow-Volume Loop • Flow-Volume loop showing successful FVC maneuver. Positive values represent expiration, negative values represent inspiration. At the start of the test both flow and volume are equal to zero (representing the volume in the spirometer rather than the lung). The trace moves clockwise for expiration followed by inspiration. After the starting point the curve rapidly mounts to a peak (the peak expiratory flow). (Note the FEV1 value is arbitrary in this graph and just shown for illustrative purposes; these values must be calculated as part of the procedure) Spirometry: Volume-Time Curve Spirometry Caveats • Absolute Contraindications • Relative Contraindications • Hemodynamic instability • Patients who cannot use the • Recent MI or ACS device properly (children and patients with dementia) • Respiratory infection, a recent pneumothorax or PE • Conditions that make it difficult to hold the mouthpiece such as • A growing or large (>6 cm) facial pain aneurysm of the thoracic, abdominal aorta • Recent abdominal, thoracic, brain, eye, ear, nose or throat • Hemoptysis of acute onset surgeries • Intracranial hypertension • Hypertensive crisis • Retinal detachment Flattened inspiratory flow loop – upper airway obstruction! ➡ nasopharyngoscopy Pre-COPD and PRISm • Respiratory symptoms and/or structural lung disease (i.e. emphysema) and/or physiologic abnormalities (low FEV1, gas trapping, hyperinflation, reduced lung diffusing capacity, rapid FEV1 decline) WITHOUT airflow obstruction = Pre-COPD • The term PRISm (Preserved Ratio Impaired Spirometry) = those with normal ratio but abnormal spirometry • Those with Pre-COPD and PRISm are at increased risk of developing airflow obstruction over time COPD Classification of Severity* Gold Stage Spirometry GOLD 1 (Mild) FEV > 80% predicted 1 GOLD 2 (Moderate) 50% < FEV <80% predicted 1 GOLD 3 (Severe) 30%< FEV <50% predicted 1 GOLD 4 (Very severe) FEV1 <30% predicted *Based on post-bronchodilator FEV /FVC <0.7 1 COPD Morphologic Subtypes on CT • Centrilobular (Centriacinar) • Classically associated with smoking-induced COPD • Spreads peripherally from the bronchioles • Panlobular (Panacinar) • Associated with homozygous alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency • Lower lobe predominant • Uniformly destroys alveoli • Paraseptal (Distal Acinar) • Involves distal airways

Due to the weak correlation between the severity of airflow limitation and the symptoms experienced by most patients with COPD, which one of the following should be obtained at each office visit?? and Procalcitonin Strategy for Assessing COPD 1. Assess airflow limitation using spirometry 2. Assess symptoms 3. Assess risk of exacerbations 4. Assess comorbidities COPD Symptoms and Airflow • Patient perception of symptoms is highly variable • Only 60% of patients with moderately severe COPD complain of any symptoms at all! • For this reason, formal symptomatic assessment is required: • COPD Assessment Test (CAT) recommended • Others • Modified Medical Research Council Dyspnea Scale (mMRC) • Clinical COPD Questionnaire (CCQ) • St. Georges Respiratory Questionnaire (SGRQ) • Chronic Respiratory Questionnaire (CRQ) Modified Medical Research Council (mMRC) Dyspnea Scale Grade Level of Dyspnea 0 I only get breathless with strenuous exercise 1 I get short of breath when hurrying on level ground or walking up a slight hill 2 I walk slower than people of the same age because of breathlessness; or I have to stop to catch breath when walking at my own pace on level ground 3 I stop to catch breath after walking 100 m (328 ft) or after a few minutes on level ground 4 I am too breathless to leave the house or I am breathless when dressing or undressing Stephens, et al. Diagnosis of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease, Am Fam Physician. 2008;78(1):87-92. COPD Assessment Test (CAT) • Multidimensional patient-completed questionnaire assessing global impact of COPD (cough, sputum, dyspnea, chest tightness, limitation on activities, confidence leaving home and going out, sleep, energy level) on health status • 8 questions on a 1-5 scale, max 40 points • 10 or more considered significant COPD symptoms https://www.catestonline.org/hcp-homepage.html COPD Assessment Test (CAT) Strategy for Assessing COPD 1. Assess airflow limitation using spirometry 2. Assess symptoms 3. Assess risk of exacerbations 4. Assess comorbidities COPD Risk of Exacerbations Increased risk: • Two or more exacerbations within the last year • One or more hospitalizations for COPD exacerbation Strategy for Assessing COPD 1. Assess airflow limitation using spirometry 2. Assess symptoms 3. Assess risk of exacerbations 4. Assess comorbidities Common COPD Comorbidities • Cardiovascular Disease • Others • CHF • Lung Cancer • Ischemia • Sleep Apnea • Arrhythmias (A. Fib risk • Depression tied with COPD) • Osteoporosis • PVD • Diabetes • HTN • GERD (independent risk factor for exacerbations!) Assess COPD Comorbidities • Assess how the comorbid illness may impact COPD • Many are underdiagnosed or misdiagnosed • Dyspnea could be CVD, right heart failure • Cough could be GERD • Fatigue or reduced activity could be depression • Comorbid illnesses should not alter COPD treatment, and they should be treated per usual standards COPD – Putting it All Together • Assess airflow limitation using spirometry • Assess symptoms • Assess risk of exacerbations • Assess comorbidities • GOLD ABE Assessment Tool Goals of COPD Management •Reduce Symptoms •Reduce Risk • Relieve dyspnea • Prevent disease • Improve exercise progression tolerance • Prevent and treat • Improve health status exacerbations • Prolong life COPD Management • Smoking cessation has the greatest capacity to influence the natural history of COPD! • No clinical trial evidence that any pharmacotherapies for COPD modify the long-term decline in lung function Treatment is therefore entirely symptomatic!

stem: Your patient with GOLD Stage 2 COPD comes in for a routine visit. They had been controlled on a LABA/LAMA inhaler, but for the past several months they have been complaining of increased breathing difficulty and sputum production requiring one visit at Urgent Care. They deny chest pain or fever. When considering adding an inhaled corticosteroid, which test would be most helpful to order? COPD Management Beta -agonists 2 • First-line therapy • Relax airway smooth muscle by stimulation of beta receptors • Lower airway resistance and increase FEV 1 • Long-acting (LABA) preferred over short-acting (SABA)* • Inhaled preferred over oral • Combination therapy if symptoms not improved with single agents • Can cause tachycardia, arrythmias, tremor *Remember monotherapy with LABA is contraindicated in Asthma, but not in COPD COPD Management Antimucarinics • Block the bronchoconstrictor effect of acetylcholine on muscarinic receptors • Long-acting muscarinic antagonists (LAMA) preferred Improve symptoms, health status, and benefit of pulmonary rehabilitation Reduce exacerbations and hospitalizations • Main side effect is dry mouth COPD Management Methyxanthines • Theophylline most commonly used • Controversial • Limited and contradictory evidence regarding effectiveness • Toxicity dose-related • Therapeutic ratio is small • Most benefit occurs at near toxic doses COPD Management Combination Bronchodilator Therapy • Increased effectiveness with lower side-effects • LABA/LAMA have best results • Improved lung function vs. monotherapy • Improved quality of life • Reduced exacerbations • Numerous combinations in a single inhaler available COPD Management Inhaled Corticosteroids (ICS) • Benefit in COPD more limited vs. Asthma • Increases risk of pneumonia, especially in severe COPD • If used, should only be used in combination with bronchodilators for GOLD Stage 2-4 with risks of exacerbations ICS/LABA more effective than either alone Triple therapy with ICS/LAMA/LABA reduces exacerbations • Blood eosinophil count >300 more likely to benefit from ICS • Consider if concomitant asthma Factors to Consider for ICS Treatment Strong Support Consider Use Against Use  History of  1 moderate  Repeated hospitalizations for exacerbation for pneumonia COPD exacerbation COPD in past year  Blood eosinophil  >2 moderate  Blood eosinophil count <100 exacerbations for count 100-300  History of COPD in past year mycobacterial  Blood eosinophil infection count >300  History of Asthma COPD Management Oral Glucocorticoids • Useful only for exacerbations, not for maintenance Phosphodiesterase-4 (PDE-4) Inhibitors • Roflumilastreduces exacerbations and is useful add-on for GOLD 3-4 • Caution for those with depression Antibiotics • Azithromycin 250mg/day reduced exacerbations in those at high risk • Bacterial resistance, prolonged QTc interval, impaired hearing limit use Mucolytics • Regular treatment may reduce exacerbations (Spiriva,Tudorza, Arcapta) Breath activation strong inhalation) Nebulizer Simple Electricity (Albuterol, Pulmicort, Maximum dose delivered Tubing maintenance Duoneb) Soft Mist Inhaler Convenience Coordination (Combivent, Striverdi) Similar to nebulizer Expiration after 3 mo COPD Management Pulmonary Rehabilitation • Comprehensive interventional therapies including exercise, education, nutrition, and psychological support • Improves symptoms and exercise tolerance in stable patients • Reduces hospitalizations • Reduces anxiety and depression • Indicated for ALL stages of disease in stable patients • Benefit after single 6-week program • Longer programs confer additional benefits Indicated for resting PaO <55 or 2 SaO < 88% OR PaO 55-60 with 2 2 right heart failure or erythrocytosis COPD Management: Prescription of Prescribe supplemental O and 2 titrate to SaO >90% 2 Supplemental Oxygen Recheck in 60-90 days and reassess if supplemental O still 2 indicated and if effective COPD Management Palliative Care • Opiates, neuromuscular electrical stimulation (NMES), oxygen, and fans blowing air onto the face can all help with breathlessness • Nutritional supplementation may improve muscle strength Ventilatory Support • Noninvasive positive pressure ventilation (NPPV) during exacerbations • CPAP in those with OSA improves survival and reduces exacerbations Surgical Interventions • Long Volume Reduction Surgery • Bullectomy • Lung Transplantation • Bronchoscopic interventions to reduce hyperinflation COPD Management by ABE Group COPD Management: Follow Up • Dyspnea: for patients with • Exacerbations: for patients persistent breathlessness or with persistent exacerbations exercise limitation • On long-acting bronchodilator, • On long-acting bronchodilator, add second bronchodilator or add second bronchodilator ICS • On LABA/ICS, add LAMA • On LABA/LAMA, add ICS for triple therapy • Dyspnea due to other causes besides COPD should be • Consider Roflumilast investigated and treated • Consider Azithromycin appropriately Remember to Assess inhaler technique and adherence!

Your patient with COPD developed an upper respiratory infection last week and now presents with increased cough, yellow sputum, and increasing dyspnea and wheezing. Exam reveals no respiratory distress with an SaO of 89% on room air, and no signs of 2 consolidation on lung auscultation. In addition to steroids, which one of the following would be most appropriate for you to prescribe at this time? Acute COPD Exacerbations • Acute worsening in symptoms requiring additional therapy • Most common cause: URI (viral or bacterial) • Increased dyspnea is key symptom • Other symptoms include increased sputum purulence and volume, increased cough, increased wheeze • Often underreported! • Assess severity (RR, SaO or ABG) 2 • Assess for other clinical conditions as needed COPD Exacerbation Differential • Pneumonia • Pulmonary embolism • CXR • D-dimer or venous doppler • Consider C-RP or BLE procalcitonin • CT chest • Pneumothorax • Pulmonary edema • CXR or U/S • EKG and 2-D echo • Pleural effusion • Cardiac enzymes • CXR or U/S • Cardiac arrhythmias • EKG COPD Exacerbation Classification Severity Description Can be controlled with short-acting Mild bronchodilators (SABA) SABA plus antibiotics and/or oral Moderate corticosteroids Requires hospitalization or evaluation in Severe the emergency department Adapted from: EvensenAE. Management of COPD Exacerbations. Am FamPhysician. 2010;81(5):607-613. Acute COPD Exacerbations • Indications for admission: • Marked increased symptoms (dyspnea, tachypnea, confusion, drowsiness) • Acute respiratory failure • Onset of new physical signs (cyanosis, edema) • Failure to respond to initial management • Serious comorbidities (CHF, arrythmias, etc.) • Insufficient home support Acute COPD Exacerbations • Oximetric evaluation is mandatory* • O sats 80-90% on RA – mild exacerbation 2 • O sats < 80% on presentation – severe exacerbation 2 • ABG evaluation • Admission if pH < 7.32 secondary to risk of respiratory failure *Oxygen should be used to titrate O sats > 88% 2 Acute COPD Exacerbations Bronchodilators (SOR: C) • Inhaled SABA with or without short-acting anticholinergics Systemic Corticosteroids (SOR: A) • Improve lung function and oxygenation; shorten recovery and hospital stays • Should be used no longer than 5-7 days • Prednisone 40mg/d recommended Antibiotics (SOR: B) • When indicated, can shorten recovery, reduce relapse, treatment failure, and hospital duration Acute COPD Exacerbations • Antibiotics should be given in exacerbation of COPD with all three symptoms: (SOR B) • Increased dyspnea • Increased sputum volume • Increased sputum purulence – most important! • Or if hospitalized and mechanical ventilation (invasive or non-invasive) is required • Duration of therapy recommended for 5-7 days • Choice of drug based on local resistance patterns • Amox/clav, macrolides, doxy for initial empiric therapy Acute COPD Exacerbations • Methylxanthines no longer recommended due to increased side effect profiles • Non-invasive mechanical ventilation should be the first mode of ventilation with acute respiratory failure • Improves gas exchange • Reduces work of breathing and need for intubation • Decreases hospitalization duration • Improves survival • Following an exacerbation initiate preventive measures Thank you! References https://goldcopd.org/2023-gold-report-2/ Answers 1. B 2. D 3. A 4. A

A 12-year-old is brought into your office for a well visit. Their parents report they have nighttime cough and wheezing for the past several months. They deny chest pain and are otherwise healthy and up-to-date on immunizations. Physical exam is unremarkable. Which one of the following tests would be most appropriate at this time? Verify a history of variable and recurrent respiratory symptoms Asthma Document evidence of expiratory Diagnosis airflow limitation Spirometry (PFT) is recommended To make the To assess level of To exclude severity based on diagnosis alternate causes impairmentand risk Asthma Diagnosis Do not diagnose or manage asthma without spirometry. Asthma Diagnosis Spirometry • Recommended for every pt ≥ 5 yrs of age. (In patients under age 5, a therapeutic trial of meds recommended) Studies specific to individual patients • Allergy testing • Bronchial provocation testing • Fractional Exhaled Nitric Oxide (FeNO) Levels > 50 ppb (or greater than 35 ppb in children ages 5–12 years) are consistent with elevated inflammation and support a diagnosis of asthma Useful if diagnosis uncertain Also elevated in allergic rhinitis, atopy, and smoking

When performing spirometry on your 12-year-old wheezing patient, after the administration of a bronchodilator, what percentage of airway reversibility and change in FEV is 1 consistent with the diagnosis of asthma? Asthma Diagnosis: Spirometry • FEV is decreased to < 80% predicted 1 • TLC is normal to elevated • FRC is usually elevated • Significant reversibility of bronchoconstriction (measured by FEV1) after inhaling a SABA – Increase by ≥ 12% and – 200 mL in FEV 1 Spirometry Measures: TLC, total lung capacity; V, tidal volume; IC, inspiratory capacity; FRC, functional residual capacity; ERV, expiratory reserve volume; VC, vital capacity; RV, residual volume. symptoms since birth Bronchopulmonary dysplasia Cough and sputum production; GI symptoms Cystic fibrosis Differential Diagnosis of Asthma Age Symptoms – previous plus: Condition – previous plus: Dyspnea, inspiratory wheeze (stridor) Inducible laryngeal obstruction 12 - 39 Hyperventilation, dysfunctional Dizziness, paresthesias, sighing breathing Years Dyspnea, family history of early COPD Alpha -antitrypsin deficiency 1 Cough, sputum, SOB, smoke exposure COPD 40+ Exertional SOB, nocturnal symptoms, edema Cardiac failure years Treatment with ACEI Iatrogenic cough Sudden onset SOB, chest pain Pulmonary embolism All Cough, hemoptysis, night sweats, weight loss Tuberculosis Asthma Diagnosis in Other Contexts • Occupational Asthma – especially if adult-onset • Pregnant women – all should be asked about asthma • The elderly – often underdiagnosed • CHF, ischemia or COPD may coexist with underlying asthma • Smokers and ex-smokers – often coexist or overlap • Athletes – exercise-induced bronchoconstriction (EIB) common • Patients with persistent cough as the only respiratory symptom • May be due to upper airway cough syndrome (aka ”postnasal drip”), GERD, chronic sinusitis, ACE-I associated cough, bronchitis • Cough-variant Asthma characterized by cough and airway hyperresponsiveness and is often worse at night Assessment of Asthma Severity Severity: • Level of treatment required to control symptoms and exacerbations • Assessed retrospectively, after a minimum of 2-3 months of treatment • Lung function should be assessed: At diagnosis After 3-6 months of treatment to determine their Personal Best Periodically thereafter  Patients with apparently “mild asthma” are still at risk of serious adverse events  30–37% of adults with acute asthma Had symptoms less than weekly  16% of patients with near-fatal asthma in previous 3 months (Dusser,  15–27% of adults dying of asthma Allergy 2007;
