Your patient with asthma had been managed with a daily ICS along with a SABA prn. On a routine follow-up visit their Asthma Control Test score was 15 (uncontrolled). After assessing for proper inhaler adherence and technique, the next best step in management would be which one of the following? Asthma Management: ICS Inhaled Corticosteroids (ICS) • Reduce hospitalizations and death • Prevent exacerbations, reduce symptoms, improve lung function • Daily use is the preferred controller therapy for patients of all ages who have persistent asthma (SOR: A) • Intermittent use: Used for brief periods, usually in response to symptoms Add-on with or without a long-acting beta2-agonist (LABA) Triple therapy inhaler with LAMA/LABA now approved for asthma as well as COPD Asthma Management: SABA Inhaled short-acting beta -agonists (SABA) 2 • Have been first-line treatment for asthma for 50 years • This dates from an era when asthma was thought to be a disease of primarily bronchoconstriction • Patient satisfaction with and reliance on SABA treatment is reinforced by its rapid relief of symptoms, its prominence in ED and hospital management of exacerbations, and low cost • Patients commonly believe that “My reliever gives me control over my asthma”, so they often don’t see the need for additional treatment Asthma Management: SABA Inhaled short-acting beta -agonists (SABA) 2 • Regular or frequent use of SABA is associated with adverse effects b-receptor downregulation, decreased bronchoprotection, rebound hyperresponsiveness, decreased bronchodilator response Increased allergic response, and increased eosinophilic airway inflammation • Higher use of SABA is associated with adverse clinical outcomes Dispensing of ≥3 canisters per year (average 1.7 puffs/day) is associated with higher risk of emergency department presentations Dispensing of ≥12 canisters per year is associated with higher risk of death Asthma Management: LABA • Inhaled long-acting beta -agonists (LABA) 2 • Used concomitantly with low- to medium- dose ICS, LABA are the preferred combination therapy for long- term control and prevention of symptoms in moderate and severe persistent asthma (SOR: A, B) • Formoterol most studied but salmeterol, vilanterol, arformoterol, olodaterol, indacaterol other options • Not recommended as monotherapy! Asthma Management: Single Maintenance And Reliever Therapy • ICS/formoterol MDI in a single inhaler now preferred 1-2 puffs qd-BID as maintenance 1-2 puffs as needed for asthma symptoms Max dose 8 puffs/day age 4-11 and up to 12 puffs/day over age 12 • Formoterol has a rapid onset of action vs. other LABA • Consistently reduced exacerbations and need for oral steroids (SOR: A) • Reduces potential adverse effects of chronic steroids • Simplifies patient’s regimen • (Generic available, but cost and formulary may limit use in some cases) Asthma Management: Caveats • Budesonide/formoterol not approved by the U.S. FDA for use as a quick relief agent • The device most studied was the inspiratory driven, dry-powder Symbicort Turbuhaler (not available in the United States) • Studies suggest that salmeterol (the LABA in Advair) is less effective, and it is not recommended as an alternative to formoterol • Studies of as-needed use of vilanterol (the LABA in Breo Ellipta) or mometasone/formoterol (Dulera) have not been published • Most allergists and pulmonologists also endorse mometosone/formoteroal(Dulera) as a SMART option Asthma Management: LAMA Inhaled long-acting muscarinic antagonists (LAMA) • More limited use in asthma than COPD • If LABA cannot be used, ICS/LAMA acceptable alternative • Use of LAMAs as add-on therapy for severe persistent asthma for those age > 12 • In those with uncontrolled persistent asthma, the expert panel recommends considering a LAMA to ICS-LABA therapy (SOR: C) • May increase hospitalizations in one real-world study in POC • Do not use for those with urinary retention or glaucoma • Triple therapy available in a single inhaler with ICS/LAVA now approved for asthma as well as COPD Asthma Management Inhaled cromolyn • Used as alternative (not preferred) medications for the treatment of mild persistent asthma (SOR: A, B) Leukotriene modifiers • Alternative medication for the treatment of mild persistent asthma (SOR: B) • New Black Box warning for montelukast re: psych issues so now only recommended for those with allergic rhinitis Oral corticosteroids Biologics Asthma Management: Summary Intermittent: inhaled SABA prn Mild persistent: daily or prn ICS with inhaled SABA prn Moderate to severe persistent: ICS-formoterol therapy as SMART (single maintenance and reliever therapy) Step Up or Down as needed Last step: consider higher ICS dose, referral to Pulmonologist, or use of Biologic agents for severe persistent asthma Severe Asthma Options  Anti-IL4R* (dupilumab) for severe eosinophilic/Type 2 asthma  Not suggested if blood eosinophils (current or historic) >1500/µl  Dupilumab now also approved for children ≥6 years with severe eosinophilic/Type 2 asthma, not on maintenance OCS (Bacharier, NEJMed 2021)  Anti-TSLP* (tezepelumab) now approved for severe asthma (age ≥12 years)  Greater clinical benefit with higher blood eosinophils and/or higher FeNO  Insufficient evidence in patients taking maintenance OCS Asthma Management Avoid stepping up asthma therapy (adding new drugs or going to higher doses) before assessing adherence, appropriateness of device, and technique with current asthma medications. Asthma Management: Allergies Indoor Allergen Mitigation* • Recommended only if sensitive to indoor allergens Dust mites, pets, cockroaches, mice, mold • Consider allergy testing before implementation • Air purifiers with HEPA filters • Impermeable mattress and pillow covers • Integrated pest management • Overall data is low, and cost can be high *Unproven and may increase costs Asthma Management: Allergies Immunotherapy • May reduce the severity of mild or moderate asthma over time in patients with proven allergies • Subcutaneous (SCIT) or sublingual (SLIT) Recommends SCIT for those >5 with mild to moderate asthma Not recommended in severe asthma or exacerbations SLIT not recommended for asthma, but approved for allergic rhinitis Asthma Management: Thermoplasty Bronchial Thermoplasty (BT) • Procedure using heat to remove muscle tissue from airways of adults with moderate to severe asthma • May reduce severe exacerbations vs. standard care • Short term risks include exacerbations, hemoptysis, atelectasis • Long term risks unknown • Not recommended • Consider ONLY in moderate to severe persistent asthma in those willing to accept risks, at an experienced medical center Risk Factors for Poor Outcomes Reviewing response and adjusting treatment • Follow-up visits • Every 1-3 months after starting therapy • Every 3-6 months thereafter • Assess asthma control, inhaler technique, adherence, triggers, comorbid conditions Asthma • Measures of control are the same as those to assess severity (validated questionnaires like Monitoring the ACT) • FeNO levels may be useful for persistent allergic asthma Asthma is highly variable so periodic adjustments are needed! Stepping up Asthma Treatment Day-to-day adjustment • With MART (ICS/formoterol) the patient adjusts the number of doses from day to day according to symptoms Short-term step up (for 1-2 weeks) • A short-term increase in maintenance ICS dose may be necessary during URI or allergen exposure • This may be initiated by the patient per their Asthma Action Plan Sustained step up (for at least 2-3 months) • Some patients who are uncontrolled on low-dose ICS/LABA may need increasing to medium dose ICS • If no response after 2-3 months reduce the dose, use alternative treatments, and consider specialist referral Stepping Down Asthma Treatment • Consider stepping down if symptoms are well-controlled for minimum 3 months • Choose an appropriate time (no URI, travel or pregnancy) • Approach each step as a therapeutic trial • Document asthma status • Provide clear instructions • Provide a written asthma action plan • Ensure enough medication is available to patient • Monitor symptoms • Schedule a follow-up visit! Asthma Monitoring • Evidence neither supports nor refutes the benefits of peak expiratory flow monitoring (PEF) • Expert Panel opinion (SOR: B) • PEF monitoring should be considered for • Patients with moderate or severe persistent asthma • Difficulty recognizing signs of exacerbations • History of severe exacerbations • It may enhance clinician-patient communication • It may increase patient and caregiver awareness of the disease status and control Asthma Action Plans • Benefits: reduce hospitalizations, ED visits, symptoms, nightly awakenings, time off work, and improve quality of life • Action plans should: • Be written and easy to understand • Describe how to recognize and respond to worsening asthma • Address individual symptoms • Provide advice about a change in ICS • Base responses on patient PEF personal best Guided self-management of asthma includes: • Self-monitoring of symptoms and/or Peak Expiratory Flow (PEF) • A written plan to show how to respond to worsening asthma • Regular review of asthma control by a provider Asthma Proven to reduce asthma morbidity in Action Plans children and adults Also proven to reduce hospitalizations, ED visits, symptoms, nightly awakenings, time off work, and improve quality of life Asthma Action Plan • • Yellow Zone: • • • • Asthma Action Plans
choice_C: Change therapy to low-dose ICS/formoterol daily and prn

stem: Your patient's asthma had been well controlled until an abrupt onset of nasal congestion, clear drainage, body aches, and worsening cough of 5 days duration. They are now wheezing and requiring use of their SABA every 3-4 hours. Exam reveals a slightly anxious patient in no acute distress; RR 18, SaO 97% and Temp 99.40F; no retractions; auscultation shows mild 2 inspiratory and marked expiratory wheezes. In addition to continued frequent use of their SABA, which one of the following would be the next best step in the management of their problem? Asthma Flare-ups (Exacerbations) Acute worsening in symptoms and lung function May be the initial presentation ‘’Flare-up” preferred when discussing with patients Management is along a continuum: Self-care via a written Asthma Action Plan Primary Care management Emergency Department care Hospitalization Asthma Flare-ups (Exacerbations) Characterized by • Decreased PEF (< 70% predicted normal) FEV1 may be more useful in predicting exacerbations • Failure to respond to a beta -agonist 2 • Physical signs and symptoms Anxiety due to breathlessness Gasping for air, sweaty, or cyanotic Rapid deterioration over a few hours Retractions and nasal flaring Hunched forward Acute Asthma Exacerbation • Historical risk factors for death from asthma • History of severe exacerbations requiring intubation • Prior admission to ICU for asthma • Emergency care visit for asthma in the past year • Current use or recent withdrawal from systemic corticosteroids • Not currently using ICS • H/O psychiatric disease or psychosocial problems • Overuse (>1 canister) per month of inhaled SABA • Poor compliance with meds or action plan Acute Asthma Exacerbation • Physical risk factors for death from asthma • Altered mental status • Absence of wheezing (silent chest) • Paradoxical chest or abdominal movement • PaCO >42 mm Hg 2 • FEV <40% predicted after initial treatment 1 Asthma Exacerbation Management • Recommended • Oxygen prn • SABA by MDI or nebulizer (continuous superior to intermittent) • ICS age 0-4 at start of RTI for 7-10 days • Systemic corticosteroids – Oral or IV • Inhaled Ipratropium • Not recommended • Methylxanthines • Chest PT • Mucolytics • Sedation Acute Asthma Exacerbation • Antibiotics: Benefit from antibiotic therapy for asthma exacerbations has not been demonstrated unless strong evidence of lung infection (SOR: B) Fever Purulent sputum CXR evidence of pneumonia • Aggressive treatment with steroids should be implemented before antibiotics considered Asthma Exacerbation Management Degree of Signs & Initial PEV Clinical Severity Symptoms Or FEV1 Course Dyspnea only PEF >70% predicted of Rx athome, PRN SABA, Mild with some activity personalbest possible oral steroids Dyspnea limits most PEF 40-69% predicted Office or ED visit; frequent Moderate activity of personal best use of SABA,oral steroids ED visit and likely Dyspnea interferes with PEF <40% predicted of admission, frequent Severe conversation personalbest SABA, oral steroids and adjunctive RX ED visit, possible ICU Life PEF <25% predicted of admission, continuous Too dyspneic to speak Threatening personalbest SABA, IV steroids, adjunctiveRx Follow-up After an Exacerbation All patients must be followed until symptoms normalize! Could represent a failure in care, so it’s an opportunity to review • Patient understanding of exacerbation • Modifiable risk factors (e.g., smoking) • Choice of treatment track (ICS-formoterol reduces risks) • Patient understanding of medications • Inhaler technique skills • Adherence with meds • Written Asthma Action Plan – revise as needed

Your patient is now 18 years old, and their asthma severity level is presently intermittent, requiring a SABA only occasionally. They recently started training for a marathon, and they are now complaining of worsening cough and decreased performance when they run, requiring daily use of their SABA. Which one of the following would be the next best step in their treatment regimen? warming up) until symptoms improve Exercised-induced Bronchoconstriction Due to dry air, cold air, ozone, particulates, chlorine Occurs in up to 90% of asthmatics and up to 20% of athletes Diagnosed with spirometry • ⬇ in FEV of 10% after exercise diagnostic 1 • Direct testing with methacholine challenge • Indirect testing with exercise challenge test In a controlled, dry environment More specific for Exercise-induced Bronchoconstriction (EIB) Some studies suggest FeNO may replace FEV 1 Exercised-induced Bronchoconstriction • Pretreatment prior to exercise • SABA preferred 15 minutes before exercise (SOR: A) Lasts 2-4 hours Tolerance can develop • LABA no longer recommended • Daily low-dose ICS if not controlled (SOR: B) • Recent 6-week study showed budesonide-formoterol use before exercise equal to daily ICS with prn SABA Exercised-induced Bronchoconstriction • Other pharmacologic • Non-pharmacologic treatments to consider: interventions (SOR: A) • Leukotriene Receptor • Warming up before Antagonists (LTRA) exercise • Mast cell stabilizers • Masks/mouth coverings • Short-acting muscarinic • Avoiding triggers antagonists (SAMA) • Antihistamines Asthma COPD Overlap Syndrome • Heterogenous and often overlapping conditions • Evidence-based treatment recommendations differ • LABA/LAMA for COPD but not for Asthma • High dose ICS for Asthma but not for COPD due to pneumonia • Smokers often have symptoms of both • Diagnosis starts with recognition of a chronic airway disease • Lung function testing essential • Patients with Overlap Syndrome have worse outcomes than with either Asthma or COPD alone! • Consider early referral to Pulmonology COPD vs Asthma Symptom COPD Asthma Chronic cough & sputum: Common Variable Breathlessness on exertion Persistent Intermittent,reversible or poor lung function: Onset prior to 40 yrs: Uncommon Common Tobacco use: Almost always Sometimes Airway hyper- Common Always responsiveness: Progression: Slowly; little variability Episodicand variable Identifiabletriggers: Uncommon Common Bronchodilator response: Modest Often marked Asthma COPD Overlap Syndrome Treat toward predominant symptoms (asthma or COPD) • Asthma predominant Emphasis on ICS No LABA as monotherapy • COPD predominant Emphasis on combination bronchodilators No ICS as monotherapy • Non-pharmacological strategies including smoking cessation, pulmonary rehabilitation, vaccinations, treatment of comorbidities Asthma COPD Overlap Syndrome Triple therapy inhalers (ICS/LAMA/LABA) • Fluticasone/umeclidium/vilanterol (Trelegy Ellipta) approved for both Asthma and COPD Breath activated Not approved as a rescue device • Budesonide/glycopyrrolate/formoterol (Breztri Aerosphere) approved for COPD and currently seeking FDA approval for Asthma Metered-dose inhaler (MDI) Asthma During Pregnancy Extremely variable • 1/3 worsen • 1/3 improve • 1/3 remain stable Exacerbations and poor symptom control increase risks both to mother (preeclampsia) and the fetus: • Perinatal mortality • Preterm birth • LBW infants (greater risk for DM, hypertension, heart disease as adults) Asthma During Pregnancy • ICS reduce risk of exacerbations • Cessation of ICS poses significant risk for exacerbations • Albuterol is the preferred SABA • Budesonide preferred ICS (most reliable safety profile) • Cromolyn, LTRAs, LABAs, and theophylline may be alternatives, but have lower efficacy or less safety data • LABA should not be used as monotherapy • In severe asthma, evidence on biologics limited but omalizumab has been used safely Asthma During Pregnancy • Monthly monitoring recommended • Do not step down treatment during pregnancy • Do not stop ICS before or during pregnancy • Treat exacerbations aggressively with SABA, O and early 2 systemic steroids to avoid fetal hypoxia • During labor and delivery, usual controller medication should be used along with reliever meds as needed • If high dose SABA used during labor monitor blood glucose in baby for first 24 hours Asthma During Pregnancy Avoid allergens and irritants (smoking) Recognize and manage comorbid conditions • Allergic rhinitis Nasal corticosteroids Antihistamines (loratadine and cetirizine preferred) • Sinusitis • GERD Inadequate control of asthma poses a greater risk to the fetus than asthma medications! Thank you! Answers 1. D 2. C 3. C 4. C 5. A References https://www.nhlbi.nih.gov/sites/default/files/publications/AsthmaCliniciansGuideDesi gn-508.pdf https://ginasthma.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/05/Whats-new-in-GINA- 2021_final_V2.pdf https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK557554/#article-17925.r1 https://www.aafp.org/afp/2020/0301/p310.html https://www.aafp.org/afp/2019/0801/p185.html
choice_A: Add a daily inhaled low-dose corticosteroid
choice_D: Substitute a LABA prior to exercise

stem: When deciding whether to treat your 55-year-old with pneumonia as an outpatient or to admit them to the hospital, in addition to clinical judgement which one of the following tools or tests would be most helpful? CAP Biomarkers? Procalcitonin not routinely recommended (SOR: B) Elevated levels correlate with higher likelihood of typical bacterial pathogens Sensitivity of procalcitonin to detect bacterial infection ranges from 38% to 91% Empiric antibiotic therapy should not be withheld if CAP suspected or if confirmed radiographically May be useful in setting of superimposed CHF CAP: Inpatient or Outpatient? Costs of hospitalization high Possible complications • Nosocomial infections • Thromboembolic events Risk stratification scores: (SOR: A) • Pneumonia Severity Index (PSI) (prognostic model) recommended over CURB-65 score (severity of illness) 28 CURB-65 Scores for CAP (>2 Hospital) Clinical Factors Points Confusion 1 Uremia (BUN >19mg/dl or 7mmol/L) 1 Respiratory Rate >30/min 1 Blood Pressure: SBP <90 or DBP <60 1 Age >65 1 Pneumonia Severity Index (PSI) PSI Risk Levels ARisk Class I or Risk Class II pneumonia patient can be sent home on oral antibiotics ARisk Class III patient, after evaluation of other factors including home environment and follow-up, may be either: • Sent home with oral antibiotics • Admitted for a short hospital stay with antibiotics and monitoring Patients with Risk Class IV-V pneumonia patient should be hospitalized for treatment

You decide to to treat your 55-year-old with pneumonia and a history of DM and HTN as an outpatient. The appropriate choice for outpatient treatment in this case is which one of the following? CAP Treatment Categories 1. Outpatient • If healthy, low risk for DRSP or MRSA; no antibiotics in past 3 months 2. Outpatient with comorbidities • Chronic heart, lung, liver, or renal disease; DM; alcoholism; malignancy; immunosuppression; recent antibiotics in past 3 months CAP Treatment Categories 3. Non-severe Inpatient • Expanded coverage including Gm negatives associated with aspiration 4. Severe Inpatient/ICU • Extensive coverage (Staph, Legionella, H. flu, Gram negatives) CAP Management 1. Outpatient, previously healthy, no risk for DRSP Amoxicillin (SOR: A) OR Doxycycline (SOR: A) OR Macrolide (only if local pneumococcal resistance is <25%!) CAP Management 2. Outpatient with comorbidities or recent abx Beta-lactam PLUS a Macrolide or Doxy (SOR: A) Amox/clavulanate (2g bid) preferred Alternatives include ceftriaxone, cefpodoxime, cefuroxime OR Respiratory fluoroquinolone (SOR: A) Moxifloxacin, gemifloxacin, or levofloxacin FDA Statement on Quinolones - 2016 “Fluoroquinolones should be reserved for use in patients who have no other treatment options for acute bacterial sinusitis, (ABS), acute bacterial exacerbation of chronic bronchitis (ABECB), and uncomplicated urinary tract infections (UTI) because the risk of these serious side effects generally outweighs the benefits in these patients. For some serious bacterial infections, the benefits of fluoroquinolones outweigh the risks” Caution for those with AAA or uncontrolled HTN due to increased risk of aortic rupture or dissection! CAP Management 3. Non-severe Inpatient Respiratory fluoroquinolone (IV or PO) (SOR: A) OR Beta-lactam (IV or IM) Ampicillin/sulbactam, cefotaxime, ceftriaxone, ceftaroline PLUS Macrolide* (IV or PO) (clarithromycin, azithromycin) *Doxycycline may be substituted (SOR: C) CAP Management 4. Severe Inpatient/ICU: requires dual therapy! Beta-lactam* (IV) PLUS Macrolide OR Beta-lactam PLUS Fluoroquinolone (SOR: A) (*If PCN allergic fluoroquinolone and aztreonam recommended) American Thoracic Society/Infectious Diseases Society of America CHANGES Recommendation 2007 ATS/IDSA Guideline 2019 ATS/IDSA Guideline Sputum culture in patients with severe disease in patients with severe disease as well as in all inpatients empirically treated for MRSA or Pseudomonas aeruginosa Blood culture in patients with severe disease in patients with severe disease and all inpatients empirically treated for MRSA or P. aeruginosa Macrolide monotherapy Strong recommendation for outpatients Based on resistance levels Use of procalcitonin Not covered Not recommended Use of corticosteroids Not covered Not recommended--consider in septic shock Use of healthcare-associated Introduced in the 2005 ATS/IDSA Abandoning this categorization. pneumonia category hospital-acquired and ventilator- Emphasis on local epidemiology and risk associated pneumonia guidelines for MRSA or P. aeruginosacoverage. Standard empiric therapy for severe β-Lactam/macrolide and β- Stronger evidence in favor of β- CAP lactam/fluoroquinolone combinations lactam/macrolide combination Routine use of follow-up chest x-ray Not addressed Recommended not to obtain. Adult Pertussis (“Whooping Cough”) • Due to highly contagious Bordetella pertussis • Characterized by persistent cough • Most patients afebrile • Up to 20% of adults with cough >14 days have pertussis • Cough paroxysms of such severity complications may occur: • Syncope • Bladder incontinence • Rib fractures • Subconjunctival hemorrhages • Stroke Adult Pertussis Stage 1 – Catarrhal: URI symptoms for 1-2 weeks • If recognized, treatment can shorten disease course Stage 2 – Paroxysmal: Uncontrolled paroxysms of cough • Treatment has little effect on disease but may prevent spread • Lasts from 2-4 weeks • May lead to vomiting • Marked fatigue and possible dyspnea Stage 3 - Convalescent: Recovery typically lasts 1-2 weeks • Patient still infectious • Goal is eradication of carrier state Adult Pertussis • Diagnosis with nasal PCR or culture • Treatment: Azithromycin • Preferred due to shorter duration and better side effects • Alternatives: Clarithromycin or TMP-SMX • Post-exposure prophylaxis for higher risk household contacts within 21 days • Prevention: Tdap vaccine (pregnancy, family members of infants) Viral Lung Infections (Influenza) • Abrupt onset of respiratory/constitutional symptoms (cough, fever, myalgia, headache, malaise, sore throat, and rhinitis) • Aerosol spread in winter, incubation ~2 days, lasting 3-7 days • Rapid tests available, but false-negative rate is high • Antiviral meds should be started ASAP (within 48 hours) for severe cases, hospitalized patients, or those at high risk: • Chronic disease (e.g., Asthma, COPD, DM, CHD, morbid obesity) • Immunosuppression (HIV/AIDS, Cancer) • Pregnancy • Age <5 or >65 Influenza and COVID-19 Cocirculating • Testing for both viruses recommended in all hospitalized patients with acute respiratory illness • Testing for both viruses recommend in outpatients with acute respiratory illness if the results will change the clinical management strategy • Clinicians should consider testing patients for other pathogens based on their specific clinical circumstances • Influenza treatment is the same with or without COVID-19 • Antiviral treatment for influenza can be stopped when influenza has been ruled out Antivirals for Influenza • Oseltamivir (Tamiflu) preferred (SOR: A) • Adults: 75 mg po BID x 5 days (QD x 7 days for *prophylaxis) • Childhood dose with oral suspension is weight dependent • Zanamivir (Relenza) - except in asthma, COPD (SOR: A) • Adults and children >7: 2 inhalations BID x 5 d (QD x 7d for *prophylaxis) • Peramavir (Rapivab) • Adults: 600 mg IV infusion once • Baloxavir marboxil (Xoflusa) • Single oral dose of 40 mg (40-80kg) or 80 mg (over 80kg) *Chemoprophylaxis considered for recent exposure in high-risk patients and recommended for outbreaks in an institutional setting Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) • Most adults have mild URI for 5 days • Higher risk adults may have more severe illness c/w pneumonia • Infection maylead to exacerbation of serious conditions: • Asthma • Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) • Congestive heart failure • Should be considered in any adult with URI symptoms during the fall/winter season • Diagnosis with PCR (best), antigen testing or culture • Treatment is supportive • Prevention with vaccination COVID-19 (SARS-Cov-2) Infection People with COVID-19 have had a wide range of symptoms reported – ranging from mild symptoms to severe illness. Symptoms may appear 2-14 days after exposure to the virus. Anyone can have mild to severe symptoms. Possible symptoms include: COVID-19: What’s In or Out in 2023? ✅ 🚫 • Monoclonal antibodies • Risk assessment • Hydroxychloroquine • Vaccinations • Ivermectin • Ventilation • Pre-exposure prophylaxis • Space and distancing • Post-exposure prophylaxis • Masks • Testing • Antivirals COVID-19 Risk Factors Risk of Transmission Risk of Illness • Exposure time • Age • Cough of heavy breathing • Immune status • Symptoms of illness • Underlying health conditions • Masks • Vaccination • Ventilation • Timely treatment • Distance • Previous illness COVID-19 Treatment COVID-19 Treatment Pulmonary Disease Prevention: Pneumococcal Vaccination • S. pneumoniae consequences: • 400,000 possibly preventable deaths per year due to pneumonia, bacteremia, and meningitis PCV13—Pneumococcal Conjugate vaccine (Prevnar 13) • T-cell response PPSV23—Pneumococcal Polysaccharide vaccine (Pneumovax) • 23 serotypes that cause 80% of invasive pneumococcal disease • B-cell response • 96% drop in pneumonia caused by susceptible strains

Your 55-year-old patient with DM and HTN comes in for a routine health maintenance visit. They have no history of receiving a pneumococcal vaccine. When discussing vaccination for prevention of pneumonia, which one of the following options would be preferred in this case? Pneumococcal Vaccines - 2021 Conjugate Vaccines Polysaccharide Vaccine • PPSV23 previously • PCV13 given in childhood recommended at age >65 for and previously considered all adults, and age 2-64 with at age 65 certain underlying medical • PCV20 and PCV15 are conditions new for adults >18 (2021) PCV20 alone, or PCV15 in series with PPV23 effective at reducing pneumococcal disease in adults >65 or 19-64 with certain underlying medical conditions* NEW Pneumococcal Vaccine Recommendations - 2022 • Adults aged ≥65 years who have not previously received PCV or whose previous vaccination history is unknown should get 1 dose of PCV20 (OR 1 dose of PCV15 followed by a dose of PPSV23 ≥1 years later) • Adults aged 19–64 years with certain underlying medical conditions or other risk factors* who have not previously received PCV or whose previous vaccination history is unknown should get 1 dose of PCV20 (OR 1 dose of PCV15 followed by a dose of PPSV23 ≥1 years later) Pneumococcal Vaccine Guidance * Underlying medical conditions for adults aged 19-64: • Alcoholism • CSF leak • Chronic heart, liver, or lung • Congenital/acquired asplenia disease • Sickle cell disease • Chronic renal failure • Immunodeficiencies or (Stage 3 or beyond) immunosuppression • Cigarette smoking • Leukemia, lymphoma, MM • Diabetes mellitus • Nephrotic syndrome • Cochlear implant • Solid organ transplant 58 Pneumococcal Vaccine Guidance • Dosing schedule: When PCV15 is used, the recommended interval between administration of PCV15 and PPSV23 is ≥1 year. A minimum interval of 8 weeks can be considered for adults with an immunocompromising condition, cochlear implant, or cerebrospinal fluid leak • Adults with previous PPSV23: Adults who have only received PPSV23 may receive a PCV (either PCV20 or PCV15) ≥1 year after their last PPSV23 dose. When PCV15 is used in those with history of PPSV23 receipt, it need not be followed by another dose of PPSV23 Pneumococcal Vaccine Guidance Adults with previous PCV13: The incremental public health benefits of providing PCV15 or PCV20 to adults who have received PCV13 only or both PCV13 and PPSV23 have not been evaluated. These adults should complete the previously recommended PPSV23 Coadministration with other vaccines: • PCV15, PCV20, or PPSV23 can be coadministered with the Influenza Vaccine • No data currently available on coadministration with other vaccines • Evaluation of coadministration with COVID-19 vaccines ongoing Pneumococcal Vaccine Guidance BOTTOM LINE: 2 vaccine options: PCV15 then PPSV23 in 1 year OR PCV20 Only need ONE option ONCE as an adult Pneumococcal Vaccine Guidance The PneumoRecs VaxAdvisor mobile app helps vaccination providers determine which pneumococcal vaccines a patient of any age may need and when https://www.cdc.gov/vaccines/vpd/pneu mo/hcp/pneumoapp.html Recommended Respiratory Vaccines Updated COVID-19 • For age 6 months and older Influenza (Quadrivalent) • For age 6 months and older • Those 65 and older should receive the high-dose Tdap • Adults who have never received • Can be given at any time Recommended Respiratory Vaccines RSV (2 vaccines licensed) • Adults age 60 and older at higerh risk: Cardiopulmonary disease Kidney disorders Liver disorders Neurologic or neuromuscular conditions Hematologic disorders Diabetes mellitus Moderate or severe immune compromise Those who are frail, advanced age, reside in nursing home, or anyone with underlying conditions their provider determines might increase their risk Recommended Respiratory Vaccines RSV • Vaccination should occur prior to the Fall/Winter RSV season • Single IM dose only • Those with minor illnesses can receive the vaccine • Only contraindication is for h/o anaphylaxis to vaccine component (hamster ovary cell incubation) • Common reactions: Injection site soreness (61%) Fatigue (34%) Myalgias (29%) Headache (27%) Severe reactions less than 4% Respiratory Infection Avoidance • Avoid close contact with those who are sick • Stay home when sick • Cover mouth and nose when coughing or sneezing • Clean hands often • Avoid touching eyes, nose, mouth • Practice other good health habits • Clean and disinfect frequently touched surfaces • Get plenty of sleep, eat well, be active, manage stress Answers 1. C 2. B 3. D 4. B References https://www.atsjournals.org/doi/full/10.1164/rccm.201908-1581ST http://www.cdc.gov/tb/publications/factsheets/testing/igra.htm https://www.cdc.gov/vaccines/vpd/pneumo/hcp/recommendations.html https://www.idsociety.org/practice-guideline/diagnosis-of-tb-in-adults-and-children/ References https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/volumes/69/rr/rr6901a1.htm?s_cid=rr6901a1_w https://www.covid19treatmentguidelines.nih.gov https://www.idsociety.org/practice-guideline/covid-19-guideline-infection-prevention/ https://www.cdc.gov/fungal/diseases/coccidioidomycosis/health-professionals.html
choice_A: PCV15 now and PPSV23 at age 65

stem: A 38-year-old consults you with marked fatigue, cough, headache, night sweats, and myalgias. PMH unremarkable; social history reveals that they returned from a summer hiking trip in Arizona 2 weeks ago. Physical examination reveals a fever of 101oF but is otherwise unremarkable. CXR shows nonspecific left perihilar patchy infiltrate, and PPD is negative. These findings are most likely a result of? Fungal Lung Infections • Most uncommon in immune • Presentation competent persons • Often asymptomatic • Infection occurs after inhalation of • May present as CAP spores (bird, bat droppings in soil) • Incidental findings (Multiple • Opportunistic (more likely in pulmonary nodules on CXR immunocompromised) or lung CT) • Aspergillosis • Treatment usually not • Candidiasis necessary for endemic • Endemic (farm workers, etc) mycoses unless • Mississippi/Ohio Valley: immunocompromised or Histoplasmosis and symptomatic Blastomycosis • Southwest: Coccidiodomycosis

“Desert Rheumatism” is a colloquial term describing a classic triad in coccidiodomycosis describing fever, myalgias, and which specific dermatologic finding of the bilateral lower legs? Valley Fever (Coccidioidomycosis) • 10-20K cases reported annually, but true incidence higher • Cases rising and endemic area spreading! • Symptomatic cases (up to 40%) present 1-3 weeks after exposure with fatigue, cough, dyspnea, headache, night sweats, myalgias, and rash (erythema nodosum) • Most recover but 5-10% may develop chronic lung disease • Disseminated disease occurs in ~1% of cases • Diagnosis via antibodies, serologies, or sometimes culture • Peripheral blood eosinophilia may support the diagnosis Valley Fever (Coccidioidomycosis) • “Desert Rheumatism” • Classic triad of coccidioidomycosis • Fever • Myalgias • Erythema nodosum – inflammation of sc fat with tender, erthythematous nodules usually bilat extensor LE Erythema nodosum Valley Fever (Coccidioidomycosis) No evidence from randomized trials exists to determine whether antifungal treatment for uncomplicated coccidioidal infections reduces symptom duration or prevents complications* Infectious Diseases Society of America (IDSA) guidelines suggest treatment for primary pulmonary coccidioidomycosis in patients who: • Are immunosuppressed • Have severe or significantly debilitating illness • Have diabetesor are frailbecause of age or comorbidities • Are pregnant • Are of African or Filipino ancestry *Current prospective randomized trial ongoing regarding fluconazole treatment Valley Fever (Coccidioidomycosis) • Cases are not contagious • Follow disease progress via complement fixation titers • Some recommend treatment if titer >1:16 • Titers <1:4 associated with a good prognosis • Watch for complications • Meningitis • Infectious monoarthritis • Disseminated coccidioidomycosis requires antifungal treatment, typically fluconazole or amphotericin B • Fluconazole preferred: 400 mg once daily for 3-12 months Valley Fever (Coccidioidomycosis) C onsider the diagnosis O rder the right tests C heck for risk factors C heck for complications I nitiate management Tuberculosis • Infection with Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) • Spread via respiratory droplet • Several factors determine risk: • Infectiousness of source patient • Host susceptibility • Duration of exposure (longer time increases risk) • Environment of exposure (small, Mtb culture with colonial morphology poorly ventilated space increases risk) • Infectiousness of the Mtb strain Tuberculosis • Individuals infected with Mtb have a broad range of presentations from those with active tuberculosis (TB disease) to those infected but with no clinical disease (latent tuberculosis infection [LTBI]) • TB disease is a leading cause of infectious disease morbidity and mortality worldwide • Guidelines for LTBI testing from the ATS/IDSA/CDC Jan 2017 https://www.idsociety.org/practice-guideline/diagnosis-of-tb-in-adults-and- children/ Who needs TB testing? Goal of testing is to identify those who will benefit from therapy Usually not needed for those at low risk of infection Indicated for higher-risk populations including: • Contact with a patient with active TB • HIV infection or immune suppression • People from a country where TB disease is common (most countries in Latin America, the Caribbean, Africa, Asia, Eastern Europe, and Russia) • Occupational or social situations at risk (homeless shelters, prison or jails, or some nursing homes) • Use of illicit substances • Those with symptoms (unexplained weight loss, fevers, night sweats, cough for at least 2 weeks, hemoptysis) Tests for M. Tuberculosis Infection Mantoux tuberculin skin test (TST) • Injection of PPD into skin that produces delayed-type hypersensitivity reaction in persons with M. tuberculosis infection Interferon-Gamma Release Assay (IGRA) • QuantiFERON® Gold (QFT-G), T-SPOT • Blood test that measures and compares amount of interferon-gamma (IFN-γ) released by blood cells in response to highly specific Mtb antigens Reading a TST • Measure reaction in 48 to 72 hours • Measure induration, not erythema • Record reaction in millimeters, not “negative” or “positive” • Ensure trained health care professional measures and interprets the TST • Positive TST reactions can be measured accurately for up to 7 days • Negative reactions can be read accurately for only 72 hours TB Skin Testing > 5 mm is considered positive if: • Close contact with tuberculosis patient • Clinical or radiographic evidence of current or prior TB • Immunosuppressed patients • HIV sero-positive • Prednisone >15 mg/day • TNF-a antagonists • Organ transplant recipients TB Skin Testing > 10 mm is considered positive if: • Recent immigrant • Long-term care facility (< 5 yrs) from high-risk country resident or employee • Prison inmate • High-prevalence area • IV drug user • Diabetic • Children < 4 yrs of • Renal failure age • Mycobacteriology lab • Cancer personnel TB Skin Testing > 15 mm is considered positive if: All others with no known risk factors •Even if prior BCG vaccination IGRAs • Pros • Cons • More sensitive and specific • Must be processed within • Fewer false positives 8-16 hours (30 hours with T-Spot) • Requires a single visit • Errors in collection or transportation • Results available within 24 hours • Limited data on the use of IGRAs: • No reader bias • Children younger than 5 yrs; persons recently exposed to M. tuberculosis; • No booster phenomenon immunocompromised persons (TST • Not affected by BCG vaccination preferred in those settings) • Preferred overall and for: • More expensive • Poor compliance with TST • Requires phlebotomy • Prior BCG vaccine Guidelines for Diagnosis of LTBI IGRA recommended over TST in those > 5 years who: 1) Are likely to be infected with Mtb 2) Have a low or intermediate risk of disease progression 3) Testing for LTBI is warranted, AND 4) Either have a history of BCG vaccination OR are unlikely to return to have their TST read (SOR: B) (TST is an acceptable alternative, especially in situations where an IGRA is not available, too costly, or too burdensome) Guidelines for Diagnosis of LTBI Those at low risk for Mtb infection and disease progression should NOT be tested for Mtb infection If testing for LTBI performed in those at low risk: • IGRA recommended over TST (SOR: C) • Recommend second diagnostic test if the initial test is positive • The confirmatory test may be either an IGRA or a TST. When such testing is performed, the person is considered infected only if both tests are positive Guidelines for Diagnosis of TB Disease  Often suspected based on pulmonary imaging or positive screening test (IGRA or TST)  CT findings associated with TB: • “Active” TB • “Prior” TB* Centrilobular nodules • Fibrosis “Tree in bud” • Calcifications Miliary nodules • Bronchiectasis Cavitation • Isolated pleural thickening “Ground glass opacities *May sometimes still harbor live Mtb in respiratory samples 2299 Guidelines for Diagnosis of TB Disease • Perform 3 AFB microscopy smears (SOR: B) • Collect both liquid and solid mycobacterial cultures • Adjunctive diagnostic NAAT recommended • Obtain rapid molecular drug susceptibility testing (DST) for rifampin +/- isoniazid if AFB positive OR NAAT positive AND: • Have been treated for TB in the past • Born in or lived > 1 year in a foreign country with moderate TB rates • Contact of patient with Multi-Drug Resistant TB (MDR-TB) • HIV positive (SOR: B) Guidelines for Diagnosis of TB Disease • Collect mycobacterial culture of respiratory specimens in children • Sputum induction recommended over bronchoscopic sampling for adults who are either unable to expectorate sputum or whose expectorated sputum is AFB smear microscopy negative • Obtain AFP smear microscopy, cultures, cell count and chemistries on sites of suspected extrapulmonary TB • Measure adenosine deaminase and IFN-γ levels on fluid collected from patients with suspected pleural TB • Submit one culture isolate from each mycobacterial culture-positive patient for genotyping

A 42-year-old recent immigrant from Cambodia presents with a 1-month history of cough, night sweats, and weight loss. Their PPD is positive at 14 mm, and CXR reveals a LUL cavitary lesion. Induced sputum smear shows acid-fast bacilli. Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial therapy? Guidelines for Treatment of LTBI Once-weekly isoniazid and rifapentine for 12 weeks now recommended over isoniazid for 9 months • Self-administered therapy (SAT) now approved in addition to directly observed therapy (DOT) although slightly less efficacy • Safe from age 2 • Safe in HIV/AIDS • Improved compliance • Reduced hepatotoxicity Guidelines for Treatment of LTBI Other options: • Daily rifampin for 4 months (children and HIV-neg adults) • Daily isoniazid plus rifampin for 3 months (children and all adults including HIV-positive as drug interactions allow) • Daily isoniazid for 6 months (or 9 months if HIV-positive) Guidelines for Treatment of TB Disease Aggressive combination therapy indicated to decrease mortality, transmission, and resistance Intensive Phase treatment daily for 2 months: • Isoniazid (INH)* • Rifampin (RIF) • Ethambutol (EMB) • Pyrazinamide (PZA) Continuation Phase treatment daily for 4 additional months: • INH* and RIF *Pyridoxine (vitamin B6), 25–50 mg/day, is given with INH to all those at risk of neuropathy (pregnant women; breastfeeding infants; HIV; diabetes, alcoholism, malnutrition, or chronic renal failure; or those of advanced age) Post-Treatment Follow-Up of TB • DO NOT RE-TEST with skin test • Patient should receive documentation of • TST or IGRA results, Radiograph results, Dosage and duration of medication • Present this document any time future testing is requested • Re-educate pts about signs and symptoms of TB • Regardless of whether Rx for LTBI was completed, serial or repeat CXRs are not indicated unless signs or symptoms of TB develop

Which work site exposures confer the greatest risk of lung cancer among those workers who develop an occupational lung disease due to their exposure? Occupational Lung Diseases Lung Ca Illness Risk Group Treatment Risk? Construction Supportive Asbestosis ✅ ✅ ✅ workers (incurable) Biodust and Farmers ❌ Like Asthma Aerosols Food workers Chemical Factory workers ❌ Supportive Pneumonitis Miners, stone, Supportive Silicosis ✅ quarry workers (incurable) Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension (PAH) • Defined as a mean pulmonary arterial pressure greater than 25 mm Hg at rest or greater than 30 mm Hg during exercise • Often characterized by a progressive and sustained increase in pulmonary vascular resistance that eventually may lead to right ventricular failure • Can be a life-threatening condition if untreated • Therapy supportive and targeted at the underlying cause and its effects on the cardiovascular system, with success rates varying according to the etiology • Vasodilators and novel therapeutic agents, such as prostacyclin and others undergoing clinical trials Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension (PAH) 5 WHO groups based on similarities in pathophysiology, clinical presentation, and therapeutic options: • Group 1 - Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) • Group 2 - Pulmonary hypertension due to left-sided heart disease • Group 3 - Pulmonary hypertension due to lung diseases and/or hypoxia • Group 4 - Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) • Group 5 - Pulmonary hypertension with unclear or multifactorial etiologies (hematologic, systemic, metabolic, etc.) Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension (PAH) Workup: • CXR • EKG • 2-D echo • PFT • ABG • Sleep study if OSA suspected • Right heart catheterization for confirmation • If this workup is unrevealing, patients should then undergo ventilation- perfusion lung scanning to assess for group 4 disease (thromboembolic) Chronic Adult Cough of Unknown Etiology • Cough of unclear etiology for 8 weeks or more • Can be voluntary or involuntary • Patients who may be on an ACEI should switch to an ARB • Smokers should be advised to quit • Evaluation includes CXR and possibly PFT, but empiric therapy advised prior to undertaking an extensive workup • Most common causes of chronic cough in adults are UACS, asthma, and GERD, alone or in combination Chronic Adult Cough Upper Airway Cough Syndrome (UACS) – most common! • This includes all nasopharyngeal conditions (including allergic rhinitis (AR), rhinosinusitis, and laryngopharyngitis) that may be associated with post-nasal drip and subtle sensation of irritation of secretions in the back of the throat and the upper airways that present with a cough as the only symptom • Throat examination may reveal signs of pharyngitis and cobble-stone appearance • If suspected, a trial of nasal saline rinses, a decongestant, a first- generation antihistamine, and nasal steroids is recommended • Further tests like sinus CT scanning and nasopharyngoscopy are spared for non-responders Chronic Adult Cough Cough-Variant Asthma(C-VA) • A cough can be the only manifestation of asthma • Diagnosis can be confirmed with pre and post-bronchodilator spirometry with or without methacholine challenge test (MCT) • If confirmed, treatment should focus on ICS or ICS/LABA Non-asthmatic eosinophilic bronchitis (NAEB) • NAEB shares eosinophilic airway inflammation with asthma, but there is no demonstrable airflow limitation on spirometry • MCT is negative • Treatment focuses on ICS Chronic Adult Cough Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease(GERD) • Reflux triggers cough by a direct effect of acid on the esophagus and the laryngopharyngeal areas or by indirect vagal stimulation • Other symptoms of acid reflux, such as heartburn, may be absent (silent reflux) in as many as 75% of cases of a reflux-induced cough • pH esophageal monitoring may confirm the presence of silent reflux, although anti-reflux therapy does not always resolve a cough • Empiric treatment for GERD with diet, lifestyle modification, and proton- pump inhibitors for three months should be prescribed before considering 24-hour esophageal pH monitoring Chronic Adult Cough Cough Hypersensitivity Syndrome (CHS) • Defined as “troublesome coughing triggered by a low level of thermal, mechanical, or chemical exposure” characterized by enhancement of the cough reflex through sensitization of afferent neuronal pathways of a cough, both at the peripheral and central levels • Patients with CHS develop a cough in an exaggerated response to stimuli that would not otherwise elicit cough • CHS is the most likely explanation if a full workup is negative • A trial of antitussive drugs like dextromethorphan or gabapentin that can down-regulate cough reflex sensitivity can be used Answers 1. B 2. B 3. C 4. D 5. A

You see a 52-year-old patient with COPD and a 20 pack-year smoking history for a comprehensive well visit. They have successfully quit smoking last year after your recommendation and are otherwise stable with no other comorbidities. Which one of the following screening tests for lung cancer is currently recommended by the USPSTF? Lung Cancer • #1 cause of cancer deaths in men and women • More than 25% of cancer deaths • More die of lung cancer yearly than breast, prostate and pancreatic cancers combined • Death rate decreasing yearly by ~4% since 2009 • Greater success of quitting smoking • Earlier detection due to screening • The 5-year survival rate (26% is lower than many other cancers: colon (65%), breast (90%), prostate (98%) but improving! • 95% of those at risk are not screened! (Potential lives saved almost 50,000 per year) Lung Cancer Deaths Mortality 175 150 125 100 Annual… 75 50 25 0 Lung Cancer Prostate Breast Pancreas Lung Cancer Case Rates U.S. Lung Cancer Death Rates U.S. Risks of Lung Cancer • Smoking is #1 risk factor! (80%) • Primary or second-hand • Cigar use also dangerous • Hookah use may be worse than cigarettes • Vaping?? • Environmental toxins • Radon, asbestos, ionizing radiation (5-10%) • Air pollution 1-2% (higher in developing countries) • Age (average age at diagnosis is 70) • Personal or family history of lung cancer Presentation of Lung Cancer • Cough • Chest pain • Dyspnea • Fatigue • Unexplained weight loss • Hemoptysis • Evaluation should include CXR or CT of chest, and if negative bronchoscopy Lung Cancer Pathology • Small Cell – 10-15% • Central or mediastinal location • Aggressive • Early mets • Poor prognosis • Primary Tx: chemo Lung Cancer Pathology • Non-small Cell – 80% • Peripheral location • Often occur with underlying lung disease • Slower growing • Better prognosis • Primary Tx: resection USPSTF: Lung Cancer Screening - 2021 Population Recommendation Grade Adults Aged 50-80 years Recommends annual screening with who have a 20 pack-year low-dose computed tomography smoking history and B (LDCT). Screening should be currently smoke or have discontinued once a person has not quit within the past 15 smoked for 15 years years Lung Cancer Screening • Low-dose CT has shown high sensitivity and acceptable specificity for the detection of lung cancer in high-risk persons (smokers) • Since the treatment of NSCLC is surgical resection, screening may not be useful for patients with life-limiting comorbid conditions or poor functional status • AATS, ACS, ACCP, ALA recommend to stop screening at 74 years of age (CMS pays for CT up to 74 yrs) • AAFP: neutral (I) statement

Which one of the following diseases is the most common cause of both secondary hypertension and resistant hypertension? Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA) OSA is caused by a blockage of the airway due to soft tissue collapse in throat during sleep, leading to snoring, poor sleep, poor oxygenation, and apneic periods It affects 10-15% of the population (22 million people) Everyone with OSA snores, but not everyone who snores has OSA! (in the absence of craniofacial/airway abnormalities) Risk factors include • Obesity (most important risk!) • Sex (male) • Age over 40, but sleep apnea can strike anyone at any age OSA Consequences • High blood pressure • One of the leading causes of secondary hypertension! (30-40%) • Common in resistant hypertension (60-70%) • CVD • Sleep disturbance • Memory problems • Weight gain, impotence, headaches • May lead to daytime sleepiness, job impairment, and motor vehicle crashes OSA Symptoms • Nocturnal • Daytime • Snoring* • Nonrestorative sleep • Apneas • Morning headache • Choking • Excessive Sleepiness* • Nocturia • Cognitive deficits • Disrupted sleep • Significant other* complaints *3 S’s of OSA OSA Questionnaires • Epworth Sleepiness Scale* • STOP-BANG Score • 8 questions with chance for • 8 questions: Snore, Tired, dozing off with each factor – Observed apnea, Pressure reading, passenger in a car, (HTN), BMI>35, Age >50, watching TV, lying, etc. Neck>40cm, Gender (male) • Scoring (0-3 chance each): • Scoring (yes or no): 0-10: Normal 0-2: Low risk 10-15: Mild to Moderate 3-4: Mod risk 16-24: Severe 5-8: High risk *Subjective only so less sensitive/specific than STOP-BANG OSA Diagnosis Requires a Sleep study • Home-based sleep test (HBST) initially for moderate risk • Full Polysomnography (PSG) in hospital for those with cardiovascular, respiratory, neuromuscular condition or stroke • Split night PSG may be used if high probability of OSA present and the subsequent criteria are satisfied (AHI > 40 for minimum 2 hours, CPAP titration is done for minimum of 3 hours, PSG demonstrates that CPAP has abolished OSA) OSA Diagnosis/PSG Interpretation PSG measures both apneas and hypopneas • Apnea is lack of breathing for >10 seconds • Hypopnea is shallow breathing for >10 seconds • The Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI) is the number of apneas and hypopneas observed per hour 5 to 14: mild 15 to 30: moderate > 30: severe OSA USPSTF: OSA Screening - 2022 Population Recommendation Grade The current evidence is insufficient to assess the Asymptomatic balance of benefits and I adults harms of screening for obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) American Academy of Sleep Medicine Updated Recommendations - 2019 Indications for repeat sleep study under high clinical suspicion • HBST should be followed by PSG • PSG should be repeated Follow-up sleep study in special circumstances • In the presence of significant weight change (+/- 10%) • After operative intervention • In those with clinical improvement after use of oromandibular device • If symptoms warrant investigation for other causes (central sleep apnea or narcolepsy) OSA Treatment Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP) first-line therapy; indicated for: • Mod or Severe OSA (AHI >15) • Mild OSA (AHI 5-15) with comorbidities Bilevel PAP (BPAP or BiPAP) indications • Obesity hypoventilation syndrome • OSA with restrictive lung disease Auto-titrating CPAP can be used for unsupervised in those at low risk with no comorbidities OSA Treatment Oromandibular device • Next best treatment after CPAP • Mandibular advancement mouth insert fitted by Dentist Medical therapy • Weight loss essential (a factor in 70% of cases) • Topical nasal corticosteroid as adjunct therapy Surgical therapy (unproven) • Multiple options including uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP) OSA Novel Treatment Inspire upper airway stimulation therapy • Surgically implanted device using pacemaker technology to stimulate the hypoglossal nerve during sleep • Indications: Moderate-severe OSA (AHI 15-65) Failure on or inability to tolerate PAP therapy BMI of 32 or below Adults aged 18 or more • Contraindications: Anatomical (deviated nasal septum or enlarged tonsils) Central or mixed apnea

A 25-year-old African-American female involved in an MVA is found to have bilateral hilar infiltrates on CXR. They are asymptomatic and have no history of environmental exposures. Remainder of exam is unremarkable; PFT and PPD are negative. Transbronchial biopsy reveals a noncaseating granuloma. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate treatment in this case? 2 Sarcoidosis • Systemic granulomatous disease of unknown cause affecting younger adults under age 40 with a Black predominance • Patients commonly present with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, pulmonary infiltrates, and ocular and skin lesions • Diagnosis is based on clinical and radiologic findings plus histologic evidence of noncaseating epithelioid granulomas, and exclusion of other granulomatous diseases • Prognosis correlates with mode of onset, host characteristics, initial clinical course, and extent of disease Sarcoidosis Differential Diagnoses • Community acquired pneumonia (atypical) • Tuberculosis • Cat scratch disease • Lung cancer • Lymphoma • Occupational lung disease • Fungal lung infection Sarcoidosis • Most patients are asymptomatic and are diagnosed on routine chest radiographs • One third of patients have non-specific symptoms of fever, fatigue, weight loss, and malaise (more common in blacks and Asian Indians) • Symptomatic illness involves the lungs in more than 90 percent of patients, with sarcoid usually presenting as interstitial disease. Symptoms are dry cough, dyspnea, and chest discomfort Sarcoidosis Treatment • Corticosteroids remain the mainstay of treatment, but there is little evidence for the optimal initiation, dosage, or duration of therapy • Topical steroids may be considered for treatment of anterior uveitis and skin lesions • Systemic steroids are indicated for treatment of cardiac, nervous system, severe ocular, and symptomatic or progressive pulmonary involvement • There is little evidence for the efficacy of inhaled steroids Sarcoidosis • Cytotoxic agents and immunomodulators usually are reserved for treatment of complex or refractory disease • Antimalarial agents are used frequently for skin lesions, and they have limited success in the treatment of pulmonary disease • Lung and cardiac transplantation is reserved for end-stage disease • Monitoring for symptoms of drug toxicity is essential, and prevention of osteoporosis must be addressed in patients taking long-term oral corticosteroids Stages of Sarcoidosis Spontaneous Stage CXR Findings Frequency Remission 0 Normal 5-10% -- I Bilat. hilar LAD 50% 55-90% Bilat. Hilar LAD & II 25% 40-70% parenchymal infiltrates Parenchymal infiltrates III 15% 10-20% without LAD IV Sings of Fibrosis 5-10% 0-5% CXR Findings in Sarcoidosis Stage I – hilar LAD Stage III – Infiltrates Stage IV – Fibrosis Sarcoidosis • Patients with stage I or II disease may have no symptoms • Those with stages III and IV can be characterized by progressive dyspnea, loss of lung function, and fibrosis. Airway involvement can occur and may result in airflow limitation, persistent cough and, in severe cases, bronchiectasis • Spontaneous remission often occurs in patients with Löfgren’s syndrome, which consists of bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, ankle arthritis, erythema nodosum, fever, myalgia, and weight loss Sarcoidosis: Extra Pulmonary Sites • 90% confined to lung, but may involve: • Liver – 50-80% elevated LFT • Spleen – 40-80% splenomegaly • Eye – 20-50% acute anterior uveitis May be asymptomatic so yearly eye exam recommended • Skin – 25% have cutaneous involvement, erythema nodosum • Neurosarcoidosis – 10% may have intracranial lesions or peripheral neuropathy • Cardiac granulomas, heart block – 5% cardiomyopathy Sarcoidosis Treatment • Treatment is not indicated for patients with stage I sarcoidosis alone • Treatment of patients with stage II or III sarcoidosis with oral steroids for six to 24 months improves chest radiograph findings during therapy • For pulmonary sarcoidosis, the initiation dosage is 20 to 40 mg per day of prednisone or its equivalent for one to three months • In patients who respond, the prednisone dose should be tapered to 5 to 10 mg per day or every other day for a minimum of 12 months Sarcoidosis Treatment • Inhaled corticosteroids may be considered for the symptomatic relief of cough, although evidence is lacking • Cytotoxic agents and immunomodulators may be considered for patients with complicated or severe refractory sarcoidosis, or as steroid-sparing agents (methotrexate and azathioprine preferred) • Calcitonin and bisphosphonates may be useful in preventing osteoporosis in patients with sarcoidosis taking long-term steroid therapy • Calcium and vitamin D supplementation should be used with caution because of the risk of hypercalcemia • Some recommend to follow angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) levels in the blood to follow disease activity Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (GPA) • Formerly called Wegener’s Granulomatosis • Necrotizing granulomatous vasculitis affecting upper and lower respiratory tract with focal segmental glomerulonephritis • Unknown cause but likely auto-immune • More common in young or middle-aged males • Early diagnosis can lead to full recovery; without treatment can be fatal • Sx: cough, chest pain, dyspnea, malaise, blood in the urine • Dx: ESR; anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic bodies (ANCA); CBC, U/A • Corticosteroids are mainstay of treatment, also azathioprine, methotrexate; rituximab approved by FDA • Consult Pulmonary, Nephrology

You see a previously healthy 60 year old non-smoker who consults you for progressive dyspnea and cough. Vitals are stable and PE is significant for nail clubbing, decreased breath sounds with bilateral coarse crackles. You perform office spirometry. Which one of the following results are consistent with a diagnosis of restrictive lung disease? 1 1 1 1 Restrictive Lung Diseases • Heterogeneous set of pulmonary disorders defined by restrictive patterns on spirometry • Characterized by a reduced distensibility of the lungs, compromising lung expansion, and, in turn, reduced lung volumes, particularly with reduced total lung capacity (TLC) • These functional and other characteristics allow to differentiate them from obstructive pulmonary diseases such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), bronchiectasis, asthma, emphysema, and other obstructive lung diseases • Restrictive syndromes account for about a fifth of pulmonary syndromes, while obstructive syndromes are the majority (80%) Restrictive Lung Diseases • May be caused by the destruction of distal lung parenchyma due to infiltrates from inflammation, toxins, and mechanisms yet to be elucidated (intrinsic conditions) as well as extra parenchymal conditions (extrinsic causes) • Intrinsic conditions characterized by inflammatory changes involving the alveolar interstitium with possible involvement of the peripheral bronchial structures -AKA interstitial lung diseases • Extrinsic conditions result from limitations in neuromuscular function and chest wall movements Restrictive Lung Diseases Intrinsic (Pulmonary Parenchymal Disease) • Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis • Sarcoidosis • Interstitial Pneumonias • Inorganic dust exposure (silicosis, asbestosis, talc, pneumoconiosis, berylliosis, hard metal fibrosis, coal, chemical worker's lung) • Organic dust exposure such as farmer's lung, bird fancier's lung, mushroom worker's lung, humidifier lung, hot tub pneumonitis • Systemic Sclerosis • Pulmonary Vasculitis • Radiation Therapy • Langerhans Cell Histiocytosis • Medications (nitrofurantoin, amiodarone, gold, phenytoin, thiazides, hydralazine, bleomycin, carmustine, cyclophosphamide, methotrexate) Restrictive Lung Diseases Extrinsic (Extrapulmonary Disease) • Decreased muscle tone Neuromuscular disorders like muscular dystrophy, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, polio, and phrenic neuropathies • Deformations of the rib cage (e.g., kyphoscoliosis) • Decreased space Pleural effusions, pneumothorax, ascites, obesity Restrictive Lung Diseases Functional characteristics of a restrictive pattern in pulmonary function tests (PFTs) include decreased TLC and forced vital capacity (FVC) - maximum amount of air exhaled after a maximal inhalation and depends on the elasticity of the lung tissue, the anatomy of the thoracic cage, and the function of the respiratory muscles. TLC < 80% = Restrictive disease • 70-80% predicted: mild restrictive disease • 60-70% predicted: moderate restrictive disease • 50-60% predicted: moderately severe restrictive disease • < 50% predicted: severe restrictive disease Spirometry Findings • Restrictive Patterns: • Obstructive Patterns: • ⬇ FVC and TLC • Normal or ⬆ FCV and • FEV1 is normal TLC • Normal or ⬆ FEV1/FVC • ⬇ FEV1 • Reduced DLCO • ⬇ FEV1/FVC • Reduced DLCO in emphysema, normal in chronic bronchitis, normal or increased in asthma Restrictive Lung Diseases • History and Physical: • Symptoms (severity, time course/origin, rate of worsening), family history, smoking and drug history, as well as occupational and environmental exposure history • Most are characterized by an insidious progression of dyspnea • Intrinsic lung diseases classically produce the "3Cs” • Cough • Clubbing of the nails • Coarse crackles on auscultation Nail Clubbing Nail Clubbing Schamroth’s sign: Loss of the normal <165° angle (Lovibond angle) between the nailbed and the fold (cuticula) Treatment of Restrictive Lung Diseases • Varies depending on the etiology of restriction • Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis usually treated with immunosuppression or newer anti-fibrotic drugs • Autoimmune conditions treated with steroids and DMARDS • Exacerbations treated with: • Steroids • Oxygen prn • Important to treat underlying condition! • Lung transplantation last option Thank you! References https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560880/ https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3916028/ https://www.ijhns.com/doi/pdf/10.5005/jp-journals-10001-1379 https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/303098-overview https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430687/ https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430791/ Answers 1. B 2. B 3. A 4. B

The most likely cause of this patient’s knee pain is: An Approach to Articular Disease Look for 1 of 3 patterns • Monoarticular • Symmetric Polyarticular • Asymmetric Polyarticular Acute Monoarthritis • Inflammation (rubor, calor, pain) in one joint. • Occasionally polyarticular diseases can present with monoarticular Sx: (RA, JRA, reactive and enteropathicarthritis, sarcoid, viral, psoriatic arthritis) • The most critical diagnosis – acute infection. MSSA (most common), MRSA, Strep (GAS, GBS) in adults Monoarticular Differential Common Less Common • Osteoarthritis • Rheumatologic • Ankylosing spondylitis • Crystals (Gout) • JRA • Trauma • Psoriatic arthritis • Infectious • Reactive arthritis • Septic • Sarcoidosis • Viral • SLE • Neoplastic • Hemoglobinopathies • Overuse • Vascular (Necrosis) Symmetric Polyarthritis • RA • Lyme’s • SLE (Skin findings) • CPPD (pseudogout) • Psoriatic Osteoarthritis • Sarcoid (CXR) • Scleroderma • Spondyloarthropathy (IBD) • PMR (older pt, sx shoulder & hip) Key Points in the Approach to Arthritidies 1. Distinguish between: • Active ROM (non-articular) soft tissue syndromes • Passive ROM (intra-articular) implies joint involvement 2. Distinguish single-joint from multiple-joint 3. Mechanical vs inflammatory Diagnostic Clues • Active ROM restricted Periarticular • Back pain/eye inflammation Ankylosing spondy • Coagulopathy Hemarthrosis • Diuretics/renal stones/tophi Gout • Hilar adenopathy Sarcoidosis • Immunosuppression/steroids Septic arthritis • Insidious onset of pain OA • Previous attacks w/ resolution Crystals • Skin Lesions Psoriatic arthritis

choice_C: Duration of symptoms less than 6 weeks

stem: After diagnosing RA, when should disease modulating anti-rheumatic drugs (DMARDs) be initiated? DMARDs (Non-biologics) • Methotrexate: Inhibits dihydrofolate reductase: First line! - Don’t forget 1000 mg of folate daily - Start at 7.5 mg weekly, not to exceed 20 mg/week • Leflunomide (Arava): Inhibits pyrimidine synthesis • Hydroxychloroquine (Plaquenil): Antimalarial, blocks toll-like receptors • Sulfasalazine (Azulfidine): Folate depletion • Minocycline (Minocin): Antimicrobial. MOA unknown. DMARDs (Anti-TNF-α Biologics) • Adalimumab (Humira) Proceed with Caution • Certolizumab (Cimzia) • Confirm PPD • Risk of Hep B reactivation • Etanercept (Enbrel) • Golimumab (Simponi) • Infliximab (Remicade) • Tocilizumab (Actemra) • Abatacept (Orencia) Practice Recommendations • Combination therapy with two or more DMARDs is more effective than monotherapy. However, more than one biologic agent should not be used at one time. (A) • No regimen of monotherapy is clearly superior to any other. (A) • Corticosteroids are effective but have high toxicity. Use lowest dose possible for shortest time. (A) • A guided exercise program can improve quality of life and muscle strength. (B) Practice Recommendations • Cardiovascular disease is the main cause of mortality in persons with RA; therefore, risk factors for CAD should be addressed in these patients. (C) • Patients with inflammatory joint disease should be referred to rheumatology if symptoms last more than six weeks. (C) Major Spondyloarthropathies • Ankylosing spondylitis • Reactive arthritis (Reiter’s syndrome) • Psoriatic arthritis • Inflammatory bowel disease–associated spondyloarthropathy • Undifferentiated spondyloarthropathy. Ankylosing Spondylitis • The most common spondyloarthropathy 0.1 to 0.2%. • Related to the prevalence of HLA-B27. • Most often affects white males between 15 and 40. • Back pain insidious, dull, radiates to gluteal, worse in AM, improves with activity, but nocturnal sx. • Enthesitis common –Achilles and plantar fascia • Anterior uveitis is common • First-line tx: NSAIDs
choice_D: Only after the patient has failed conservative therapy

stem: A 35 yo male presents with a 3-month history of an asymmetric polyarthritis noted in 6 separate joints. PE reveals pitting of the fingernails along with a scaly, silver rash noted on both elbows. Which tx has been shown to delay further progression of this patient’s joint erosion? Psoriatic Arthritis © American Academy of Dermatology © Richard Usatine, MD Psoriatic Arthritis • Asymmetric, oligoarticular-associated dactylitis • Predominant DIP involvement – nail changes • Polyarthritis “RA-like” – lacks RF or nodules • Arthritis mutilans – destructive disease (hands/feet) • Axial involvement – spondylitis • HIV-associated – more severe Psoriatic Arthritis Prevalence of arthritis in psoriasis: 5-20% • Psoriasis usually precedes PSA – 75% • Arthritis precedes psoriasis – 15-20% • Nail changes common – 80% • Usually insidious, but 1/3 have acute onset • Dactylitis • Uveitis Psoriatic plaques • Scalp, extensor surfaces, anal cleft, umbilicus Diagnosis of Psoriatic Arthritis Point system: Need to have established inflammatory articular disease with > 3 points. • Current psoriasis: 2 points • Hxof psoriasis: 1 point • FHxof psoriasis: 1 point • Dactylitis: 1 point • RF negative: 1 point • Nail dystrophy: 1 point • Juxta-articular new bone formation: 1 point Treating Psoriatic Arthritis • Physical therapy: Start early! • Medications: - Biologic DMARDs best for joint disease. - Non-biologics: Methotrexate & Sulfasalazine - NSAIDS Cochrane, 2009 Reactive Arthritis (Reiter’s) • Triggered by an extra-articular infection • Reiter’s triad: 1) Nongonococcal urethritis (Can’t pee) 2) Conjunctivitis (Can’t see) 3) Arthritis (Can’t climb a tree) • Sx usually occur 1-4 weeks after infection • Typically asymmetric, oligoarticular, 2-4 joints Practice Recommendations • DMARDs such as etanercept (Enbrel) and infliximab (Remicade) are effective in treating inflammatory symptoms. (B) • Sulfasalazine (Azulfidine) may be an effective second-line agent that provides short-term relief. (B) • Despite the possibility of a bacterial etiology in reactive arthritis, antibiotic therapy has been ineffective. (B) • Second-line treatment of psoriatic arthritis (after NSAIDs) includes systemic corticosteroids, methotrexate, sulfasalazine, cyclosporine, and tumor necrosis factor-α inhibitors. (B) • Clinical criteria supported by laboratory tests, synovial fluid analysis, and radiographs help to establish the presence of spondyloarthropathies. (C) • Initial management begins with NSAIDs. (C)

Right-handed 80 yo man, previous painter, presents with chronic right, dull hand pain. States his DIPs and PIPs most tender, but not his MCPs. On exam, there is mild swelling in DIP/PIP distribution but no rubor nor calor. Which of the following is NOT consistent with this diagnosis? Osteoarthritis A chronic joint disorder with progressive softening and disintegration of articular cartilage accompanied by osteophytes and capsular fibrosis. Wear and Tear. • Primary or idiopathic • Secondary: Posttraumatic, childhood anatomic abnormality, metabolic, neuropathic, endocrinopathy, Paget’s, gout, septic arthritis Osteoarthritis Symptoms • Absence of systemic findings • Minimal articular inflammation • Distribution: DIP and PIP but NOT MCP • Insidious onset, “aching or burning” • Transient stiffness • More common in hands and large weight-bearing joints Osteoarthritis X-Ray Changes • Joint space narrowing • Subchondral sclerosis • Osteophytes • Cysts Osteoarthritis X-Ray Changes OA Medical Management • NSAIDs – transdermal safest (Diclofenac) • APAP – comparable to placebo for large joints • Walking aids • Weight loss • Physiotherapy – aquatics • Altered activity • Injections: Steroids vs Hyaluronic Acid (weak data) Which non-opioids are are most effective for OA pain? JFP. 2019:68:7:417-18 Recommended OA Treatment • Self-management programs, strengthening, low-impact aerobic exercise, and Tai Chi, neuromuscular education. • Topical NSAIDs may be the most effective. • CBT and balance training appear to improve pain in OA. • Acupuncture and thermal interventions slightly improve pain/function. • In knee and hip OA, intra-articular corticosteroid injections, oral and topical NSAIDs improve pain and function only over the short term. • Consider using Ultrasound guided injections: improved pain scores during and in the weeks following injection. Am Fam Physician.2021;103(2):120-121 NOT Recommended • Immunomodulators • TENS • Glucosamine/chondroitin • APAP comparable to placebo for large joint pain. • Oral NSAIDs and APAP together does not improve overall efficacy. • Biologic injections (Hyaluronic Acid) • Chronic oral NSAIDS (Transdermal safest) Am Fam Physician.2021;103(2):120-121

A 53 yo male with hx of renal stones presents with an exquisitely painful left great toe and heel that started last night. Patient denies fever, rash and other systemic symptoms. Which diagnostic feature is most specific to this inflammatory disorder? Gout: Common Clinical Symptoms • Acute gout often begins at night. • Rapid onset of rubor, calor, swollen, intensely tender. • Rare fever, rash or other systemic signs. • Involvement of 2 or more joints is rare in early flares. • MTP of the great toe (podagra) is the first joint affected in 50% of cases. • 20% of patients have renal stones! Thiazides are bad for gout but helpful for kidney (calcium) stones. Gout: 4 Stages 1. Asymptomatic hyperuricemia 2. Acute flares of crystallization 3. Intervals between flares 4. Advanced gout & complications Gout and Serum Uric Acid • Hyperuricemia alone is insufficient to diagnose gout! • Gout risk increases when serum levels persistently exceed 6.8 mg/dL – at which point extracellular fluids become saturated and hyperuricemia occurs. Microscopy and the Boards • Gout: MSU crystals appear as needle-shaped intracellular and extracellular crystals. When examined with a polarizing filter and red compensator filter, they are negatively birefringent. • Pseudogout: CPP crystals appear shorter than MSU crystals and are often rhomboidal and positively birefringent. Pseudogout • Monoarthritis; clinically indistinguishable from gout. • Often precipitated by illness or surgery. • Pseudogout is most common in the knee (50%) and wrist. • Reported in any joint (including MTP). • CPPD disease may be Asx (deposition of CPP in cartilage). Foods That Increase Gout Risk Foods high in protein: • Beef, pork, lamb (RR 1.41) • Seafood (RR 1.51) High fructose corn syrup • One fructose-sweetened soft drink daily raises risk for gout by 74% (RR 1.74) • 2 drinks per day 139% (RR 2.39) Most antihypertensives worsen Gout After adjusting for multiple confounders: • Losartan RR 0.8 • CCBs RR 0.9 • ACEIs RR 1.3 • NonlosartanARBs RR 1.3 • B-Blockers RR 1.5 • Thiazides RR 2.4 Acute Management Gout Initiate Prophylaxis: - NSAIDs (with PPI) (A) - Low-dose colchicine (0.6 qdor bid) (A) - Low dose corticosteroid (if colchicine or NSAIDs not tolerated) (C) + sxsof Gout Continue anti- Evaluate Gout sxwhile on ULT inflammatory prophylaxis No sxsof Gout Duration of Treatment: - At least 6 months (A) or - 3 months after achieving target UA level if no tophi (B) - 6 months after achieving target UA, if tophi detected on PE (C) Indications for UA lowering agent (UALA) • Tophus or tophi (A) • Frequent attacks (>2 attacks/yr) (A) • CKD stage 2 or worse (C) • Gout with urolithiasis (C) • UA overproduction and urinary overexcretion (>1000 mg daily) (C) Am Fam Physician.2020Nov1;102(9):533-538. American College of Rheumatology Recommendations • Acute tx: NSAIDs, steroids, or colchicine. • Low-dose colchicine should be the first-line therapy for “attack prophylaxis” • Prophylaxis should continue for the greater of: 6 months, 3 months after achieving target UA levels without tophi, or 6 months after achieving target levels with resolution of tophi. • Ok to Start ULT during the gout flare. • Adjuvant tx: topical ice therapy • Medications to avoid: fenofibrates, thiazides, non-losartan ARBs Arthritis Care & Research. 2020 Vol. 72, No. 6, 744–760 Bibliography 1.
