Allopurinol Hypersensitivity • 1% of patients can develop severe allopurinol hypersensitivity syndrome, which carries a mortality of 20-30%. • Severe allopurinol hypersensitivity syndrome is more likely to occur in patients with: • Renal insufficiency, • Those who are taking a thiazide diuretic, and • Those started on allopurinol at a dosage of 300 mg/day. Lyme Arthritis • Erythema migrans 7-10 days after Borrelia burgdorferi tick bite • Early dissemination – Migratory arthralgias, fever, systemic complaints • Late dissemination/chronic disease – Migratory oligoarthritis – Carditis – Neurological
choice_D: Positively birefringent crystals in the tissues

stem: You saw your 72-year-old patient with diabetes and hypertension, 3 months ago, and she was well-controlled on her medications, as she has been for the last 4 years. She is having cataract surgery and comes to you for a preop evaluation. What will you order? CARDIAC RISK 27 million non-cardiac surgeries annually 50,000 postoperative myocardial infarctions Cardiovascular complications responsible for ~ ½ of mortality Assessment of Risk Patient: Operation: RISK Health status Urgency Functional Type status Stress Patient Risk • Overall health status • Class I: Healthy • Class II: Mild systemic disease (well-controlled chronic disease: non-insulin dependent diabetes, asthma) • Class III: Severe systemic disease (insulin-dependent diabetes, COPD, morbid obesity) • Functional status = Metabolic equivalents (METS) • One MET is the energy you spend sitting at rest (basal metabolic rate) • 7-10 METS = good functional capacity (mod-vigorous exercise) • 4-6 METS = moderate functional capacity (climbing a flight of stairs, heavy housework) • < 4 METS = poor functional capacity Risk of Procedure High Risk Moderate Risk Low Risk (> 5% risk of MI) (1-5% risk) (< 1% risk*) Aortic and Head and neck Endoscopic Vascular* Abdominal Cataract*** Peripheral Orthopedic Plastic vascular Prostate Breast Cardiothoracic Emergent Risk factors for MACE • Reduced functional status (< 4 METS) • History of CVA* • Ischemic heart disease* • Chronic pulmonary dysfunction • Heart failure* • Obesity • Anemia • Cardiomyopathy • Moderate/high-risk procedure* • Severe valvular heart disease • Significant arrhythmias RCRI (Revised Cardiac Risk Index) • Chronic renal failure* (Cr > 2 mg/dl) • 1 point each for each starred item • 0-1 = low risk • Insulin dependent diabetes* • ≥ 2 = elevated risk Indications for ECG 1. All patients with signs or symptoms of cardiovascular disease 2. For patients without signs of symptoms: • Not indicated for low-risk procedures • Not indicated for intermediate-risk procedures if no risk factors • Indicated if risk factors are present and surgery is intermediate risk • Indicated for any patient undergoing high-risk surgery

You are seeing a 72-year-old for a pre-operative evaluation for knee replacement surgery. She has well-controlled hypertension and aortic stenosis diagnosed with an echocardiogram 3 years ago but has had no problems other than some mild dyspnea on exertion. What should be done? Cardiac Considerations 1. Hypertension: Grade III ( BP > 180/110) – may delay surgery 2. Acute coronary syndrome • Postpone surgery at least 4-6 weeks after MI: MACE risk 32.8% if < 30 days post-MI • Non-cardiac surgery should be delayed (Levine GN, et al. Circulation2016, Sept 6;134:e123) • 14 days after balloon angioplasty • 30 days after bare metal stents • 6 months after drug-eluting stents 3. Heart failure • 30-day post op mortality rate 9.2%; 13% if exacerbation of HF within 30 days • 3rd heart sound and jugular venous distention—strongest association with MACE* • Optimize fluid status and improve symptoms prior to surgery • Echocardiogram for new dyspnea or recent exacerbation Cardiac Considerations 4. Valvular disease: indications for echocardiogram • Aortic stenosis • Severe mitral stenosis, aortic and mitral regurgitation • New or progressive dyspnea • If stable, echocardiogram needs to have been done in the last year 5. Arrhythmias • Investigate underlying cardiopulmonary disorders • Atrial fibrillation: if clinically stable only requires anticoagulation monitoring/adjustment American College of Surgeons NSQIP MICA (2011) FACS web-based calculator: http://www.riskcalculator.facs.org/ Need CPT code Radiologic and Laboratory Evaluation Chest X-ray • Choosing Wisely: Avoid preoperative chest X-ray for ambulatory patients with unremarkable history and physical (no outcomes evidence for routine CXR) • Indications for CXR: • Cardiopulmonary disease • Risk factors for pulmonary complications: • COPD (Preop O sat ≤ 91%) 2 • Age > 60 years (possibly age 50 in men) • Functional dependence (METS < 4) • Hypoalbuminemia • Heart failure • Emergency or prolonged procedure (> 3 hours significantly increases risk) • Certain surgical sites (thorax, upper abdomen, AAA)—greatest risk for closest to the diaphragm • Procedure-related risk factors are more predictive of pulmonary complications than patient- related factors Obstructive Sleep Apnea • All patients should be screened for OSA (SOR C) (STOP-BANG)* • Snore • Tired during the day • Observed to stop breathing • Pressure (elevated blood pressure) • BMI > 35 • Age > 50 • Neck circumference > 16 inches (40 cm) • Gender male • Patients with OSA who have an oral appliance or CPAP equipment should bring these with them on the day of the surgery [in preop recs] (SOR: A)

Your patient is a 47-year-old female who is undergoing a rotator cuff repair after a fall skiing. She is takes lisinopril/HCTZ, atorvastatin, levothyroxine. She was seen 7 months ago for a Pap smear and complained of more frequent, heavy menses. Her TSH, lipids, and basic metabolic profile (BMP) were all within normal limits 7 months ago. Which labs should you order? Routine Laboratory Testing • Choosing Wisely: Do not obtain baseline laboratory studies in patients without significant systemic disease undergoing low-risk surgery • Not routinely recommended • Electrolytes • Blood glucose • Liver function tests • Urinalysis • PT/PTT (use history-based bleeding assessment—HEMSTOP) • Pulmonary function tests Preoperative exams: COST: > $30 billion annually 60% are unnecessary 30-95% of unexpected lab abnormalities not addressed Laboratory Testing • Urinalysis: only for high-risk urologic or gynecologic procedures • CBC: (Anesthesia society recommends for all major surgery) • At risk for anemia (chronic kidney or liver disease, inflammatory diseases, age > 65) and major surgery, heavy menstrual loss) • Surgery with expected excessive blood loss • Electrolyte and creatinine testing indications: • History of HTN, HF, CKD, complicated DM, liver disease • Medications: diuretics, ACE-I/ARB, NSAIDs, digoxin • Nephrotoxic medication in surgery • A1c: • Patients at very high risk orsigns and symptoms of undiagnosed diabetes Laboratory Testing • Postoperative acute kidney injury (AKI) has a high mortality rate (develops in 1% of surgical patients). Patients with CRF are at increased risk • GFR 10-15 mL/min –55-60% complication rate • Consider preoperative dialysis if GFR < 15 mL/min • Ensure preoperative euvolemia, normal osmolar status, minimize nephrotoxins • Coagulation tests: • Potential bleeding problem by history • Taking anticoagulants • Pregnancy testing in patients of child-bearing age • Sexually active and delayed menses or late in cycle (pregnancy status uncertain) • Concerned about pregnancy Bleeding Assessment (HEMSTOP) 1. Hematoma: Have you ever experiencedbruisesorhematomas> 2 cm without trauma or severebruisingwith minor trauma 2. hEmorrhage: Have you ever had prolonged or unusual bleeding requiring medical evaluation and treatment? 3. Menorrhagia(women): Have you ever had prolonged or heavy menstrual periods requiring medical evaluation and management? 4. Surgery: Have you had excessive bleeding during or after surgery 5. Tooth extraction: Have you ever had prolonged bleeding aftertooth extraction that required medical or dental evaluation? 6. Obstetrics (women): Have you had prolonged or excessive bleeding following a delivery? 7. Parents: Is there anyone in your family with acoagulation disorder (e.g. hemophilia, Von Willebrand disease) Two or more positive answers suggestsBleeding Disorder 1. Test Sensitivity: 89.5% 2. Test Specificity: 98.6% Preoperative Exam in Children • Orthopedic surgery and tonsillectomy/adenoidectomy are most common surgeries • Parental presence during anesthesia should be considered on an individual basis • Offer pregnancy testing for all post-menarchealadolescents • Counseling and cessation therapy for all users of nicotine products • Routine laboratory testing not recommended • Depending on prevalence: SARS-CoV-2 testing • Consider delaying surgery for URI if: history of prematurity, asthma, copious secretion, parent who smokes, surgery involves airway Conditions Requiring Optimization • May require consultation or further testing • Airway anomalies • Infection • Asthma • Seizure disorder • Cystic fibrosis • Malignancy • Sleep Apnea • Sickle cell • Bleeding disorders / Hypercoagulability • Congenital anomalies • Developmental delay • Heart murmur (new/concerning) • Genetic disorders • Diabetes (if not well controlled) • Jehovah’s witness

You are caring for a 5-month-old child who developed intussusception. The mother is still exclusively breast feeding. How long must the child fast prior to surgery ASA Guidelines for Preoperative Fasting Ingested material Minimum hours of fasting Clear liquids 2 Breast milk 4 Formula, milk, light meal 6 Fatty foods / meat 8 ASA (American Society of Anesthesiologists) Preoperative Evaluation in Older Patients • Cognitive status • Patient’s life goals • PHQ-2 (PHQ-9 if positive) • Risk Analysis Index (score of 30 = frail; score of 40 = very frail) • Demographic factors (Age, Sex) • Comorbidities (Cancer, Weight loss, Renal failure, HF, Loss of appetite, Dyspnea at rest, Cognitive decline) • Facility residence • Level of independence (4 ADLs) • Social and living situation Risk Reduction • Pre- and post-operative exercise therapy / Physical therapy • Assess for delayed skin healing and surgical site infection risk: • Smoking, Diabetes, Obesity, Malnutrition, Chronic skin disease • Treat preexisting infections • Provide nutritional supplementation 7-14 days preoperatively • Assess for dental infections • Treat dental abscess prior to surgery • Smoking cessation (Need to quit smoking 8 weeks prior to surgery) • Counseling and cessation therapy should be offered to all patients using nicotine products (including electronic delivery systems) (SOR: C) • Some surgeons will not do elective surgery if the patient smokes Medical Management in the Perioperative Period and Postoperative Management Diabetes in surgery Increased risk of infection and postop CV morbidity and mortality • Stop GLP-1 receptor agonists a week before surgery • Hold oral medications • Insulin management • Poor preoperative control leads to poor outcomes, so control should be addressed prior to surgery • Continue usual diabetes regimen* and minimize fasting • Doses of long-acting insulins should be continued at 50-100% of usual dose • Continue insulin pump basal dose • Hold scheduled short-acting insulins • Utilize short-acting, sliding scale insulin to maintain blood glucose at 140- 180* mg/dL (high quality evidence, strong recommendation)** Statins • Statin benefits • Lipid-lowering • Reduce vascular inflammation • Improve endothelial function • Stabilize atherosclerotic plaques (reduce 30-day MI & death) • Statin therapy (lovastatin and fluvastatin longer acting) • Perioperative initiation is reasonable in patient undergoing vascular surgery • Consider in patients with other indications for statin therapy* (Patients having PCI and CABG have decreased mortality if they take statin on day of surgery—equivalent of 20 mg atorvastatin) • Try to start at least 4 weeks prior to procedure • Should be continued in patients already taking them because risk of CV events sharply increases if stopped -blockers • In patients on beta blockers: continue before, during, and after surgery • Reduce cardiac oxygen demand • If they have been used for at least 4 weeks prior to surgery (SOR: A) • Used for known ischemic heart disease undergoing vascular surgery • If beta blockers have not been used for at least 1 week, initiation may be harmful (SOR: B) • Decreased MI, but increased stroke, hypotension, bradycardia, and death • Still appropriate prior to and after CABG Aspirin • POISE-2 Trial – Stop aspirin 5-7 days prior to surgery when not used for thromboembolism prophylaxis. Safe to continue through surgery if needed • No reduction in death or nonfatal MI in non-cardiac surgery • Whether on aspirin already or started prior to surgery • Bleeding more common with aspirin (hazard ratio = 1.23) • Safety of aspirin withdrawal in patients with prior coronary artery stenting is still questionable, so may continue in these patients • Dual Antiplatelet Therapy (DAPT)—aspirin should be continued when* • < 4-6 weeks after bare metal stent • < 1 year after drug-eluting stent • If stopping clopidogrel or ticagrelor (5 days) or prasugrel (7 days) continue aspirin

Which of the following is true regarding coagulant management prior to a right colectomy of a 65-year-old with hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and stable atrial fibrillation? heparin. Restart apixaban the day following surgery. Bridge with low-molecular-weight heparin. Management of Antithrombotic Medications • High thromboembolic risk • Mechanical heart valve • DVT/PE with hypercoagulable state • History of DVT/PE or stroke < 3 months ago • Severe or multiple thrombophilias(antithrombin deficiency, antiphospholipid Ab syndrome) • Active cancer with high venous thromboembolism risk • CHA DS VASc score ≥ 7 2 2 • Management • Stop oral anticoagulant 1-5 (5 for warfarin) days preop and start LMWH (low-molecular weight heparin) • LMWH usually not needed when DOACs are stopped for 1-2 days • Stop LMWH 12-18 hours preop • Restart LMWH 6 hours postop • Restart anticoagulant when taking PO • Stop LMWH when INR = 2.0 (for warfarin) or after starting DOAC BRIDGE study Medications to Stop Prior to Surgery • ACE inhibitors and ARBs (day of surgery) • Anything unnecessary (herbals, OTCs) • Anticoagulants: • 70% of patients fail to disclose non prescribed medicines • Clopidogrel/prasugrel/ticagrelor: 5-7 days • Herbals alter absorption, distribution, • Apixapan (Eliquis): at least 24 hours metabolism and elimination of • Rivaroxaban (Xarelto): at least 24 hours conventional drugs • Edoxaban (Savaysa): at least 24 hours • Dabigatran (Pradaxa): 3-4 days • NSAIDs: 1-3 days / Cox-2 inhibitors: 2-3 days • Most diabetes medications except long-acting insulin • GLP-1 agonists 7 days before because of safety concerns with vomiting • Osteoporosis agents and hormone therapy • Biologic agents (Humira, Enbrel, infliximab) – hip or knee surgery-– one dosing interval Medications to Give/Continue Perioperatively • Asthma inhalers • Parenteral antibiotics: 30 min prior to surgery • Neuro/psych meds • Discontinue in 24 hours • Anti-Parkinson’s • 48 hours for cardiac procedures • Anti-seizure drugs • Long-acting insulins (usual time) • Benzodiazepines • Steroids (usual daily dose) (to prevent withdrawal) • Anti-arrhythmic agents • Non-biologic DMARDs • Most anti-hypertensives (methotrexate)—hip or knee surgery • Statins • HIV medications • H blockers and PPIs 2 Steroids • Not usually needed for: • Inhaled steroids • Topical steroids • Prednisone < 5 mg/d or < 10 mg every other day • Continue regular larger doses at same dose • If unsure, do AM cortisol • If > 10 mcg/dL then not suppressed • If < 5 mcg/dL then suppressed and will need stress dose • If 5-10 mcg/dL may give steroids or to ACTH suppression test • In adrenal insufficiency (stress dose) – this is seldom done now • Moderate-risk procedures: 50 mg hydrocortisone IV q 8 hours • High-risk procedures: 100 mg hydrocortisone IV q 8 hours Opioid Management • Length of time for use of opioids after surgery • General surgery: 4-9 days • Mastectomy or hysterectomy: 4-13 days • Musculoskeletal: 6-15 days • Serious respiratory depression may occur when opioids (or antidepressants, antihistamines, or anti-anxiety medications) are administered to patients on gabapentin or pregabalin* • More likely in the elderly and patients with COPD Postoperative Pulmonary Complications • Risks • Common complications ‾ Advanced age ‾ Atelectasis ‾ Pneumonia (most common remote) ‾ Functional dependence ‾ Respiratory failure ‾ COPD ‾ Bronchospasm ‾ Heart failure ‾ Exacerbation of underlying disease ‾ Serum albumin < 30 g/L (poor nutrition) • Prevention ‾ High risk surgery (vascular, ‾ Incentive spirometry emergent, AAA, prolonged, ‾ Ambulation neurosurgery, stem: Your remove a 5 mm basal cell cancer from the arm of a 60-year-old and close the wound with 4 sutures. What should you apply to the wound? E. Mupirocin (Bactroban) cream Odds and Ends • White petrolatum ointment is effective for post-procedure care* • Topical antibiotics aggravate wounds, cause contact dermatitis, and promote antibiotic resistance • Decrease pain from infiltration of local anesthetics*: • Warm solution to room temperature; buffer with sodium bicarbonate * • Small needles and insert rapidly with slow infiltration • Inject through edge of wound into subcutaneous tissue • Hyperbaric oxygen: Short list of conditions that benefit* • Decompression sickness • Wounds caused by crush injuries • Short-term for diabetic foot ulcers –maybe • Co-management • Consider agreement with surgeon to order standard tests depending on the surgery (i.e. ultrasound for cholecystectomy or CT for appendicitis) Key Recommendations 1. The decision to perform preoperative testing should be based on the H&P findings, perioperative risk assessment, and clinical judgment (A recommendation) 2. blockers and statins should be continued peri-operatively in patients who are already taking these medications (A recommendation) 3. Perioperative statin therapy is recommended for patients undergoing vascular surgery, regardless of the presence of cardiac risk factors (A recommendation) 4. Initiation of fixed-dose beta blockers immediately before surgery may be harmful and is not advised. If a decision is made to initiate beta-blocker therapy, it should begin several weeks before surgery (B recommendation) 5. Patients in usual state of health do not require preoperative testing for cataract surgery (A recommendation) 6. Most DOACs can be stopped 1-2 days before procedure and started 1-2 days after. Bridging with heparin for patients on warfarin is reserved for those at high risk of thromboembolism. References 1. Arnold MF, Beer J. Preoperative evaluation: a time-saving algorithm. A 52 yo male presents with evolving symptoms of nocturia. He notes increase in dribbling following urination and less force of stream. Which of the following is the most appropriate first line agent of treatment? Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia PREVALENCE Dynamic muscle AGE PERCENT tension 31-40 years 8% 51-60 years 45% BPH 60-80 years >80% Increased • Lifetime risk of surgery = 29% prostate volume Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia SYMPTOMS • force of stream • Incomplete emptying • Hesitancy • Urgency • Terminal dribbling • Nocturia • Frequency COMPLICATIONS • Acute urinary retention • Hydronephrosis • Recurrent UTIs • Renal failure AAFP update: BPH: Rapid Evidence Review: Am Fam Physician.2023;107(6):613-622 Risk Factors • Age: > 30 • Alcohol: Even light drinking. • Smoking • Diabetes: Increasing insulin is associated with increased prostate volume. • HTN • Obesity • Sedentary lifestyle BPH: Rapid Evidence Review: Am Fam Physician.2023;107(6):613-622 BPH Meds Alpha-1 antagonists 5-Alpha reductase inhibitors • 5 approved • 3 approved (finasteride) • Similar efficacy • Similar efficacy • Different side effect profiles • Similar side effect profiles • tamsulosin, terazosin, • 6-12 months Rx for full effect doxazosin with SE • Reduce size • Dynamic component • NNT for hematuria = 2 • NNT to prevent a TURP = 6 BPH Medications • Combination treatment MAY help especially when >30 cc volume. (B) • Saw palmetto is controversial & is no better than placebo. Other BPH Treatments Tadalafil (Cialis) Transurethral microwave thermotherapy (TUMT) • Reason for improvement is unknown Is effective when there is • Level B • No urinary retention • Do not use with alpha- • No previous prostate procedure blockers • Prostate volumes between 30- 100 mL Transurethral resection of the Not as effective as TURP prostate (TURP) Alpha Practice Recommendations Blockers + 5 alpha •Alpha blockers are effective first-line treatments for patients with bothersome, moderate- reductase severe symptoms. (A) inhibitor •Begin Tx with alpha blocker or phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors, which have similar effects, with only slight benefit in combining these medications. (A) •The addition of 5-alpha reductase inhibitor is effective in men with bothersome, moderate to severe BPH symptoms and a documented enlarged prostate when alpha blocker monotherapy is not effective. (A) •5-alpha reductase inhibitors improved LUTS and reduce urinary retention but can take a year to reach their full effect; benefit is likely with combination with alpha blockers. (A) •Transurethral resection of the prostate is the most effective surgical procedure, although other procedures have lower risks of bleeding, incontinence, and sexual adverse effects. (A) •Rec self-management methods, including limited evening fluid intake, reducing caffeine and ETOH intake, toilet and bladder training, sitting to urinate, urethral milking, double voiding, bladder retraining, pelvic floor exercises, and mindfulness techniques to improve sx. (B)

A 70 yo M presents with dysuria, polyuria, urgency, incomplete voiding, and suprapubic pain for several days. On exam, his prostate is tender, enlarged, and boggy. You diagnose him with acute bacterial prostatitis (ABP). All of the following risk factors increase the risk of a poor prognosis with ABP in his age group EXCEPT? Acute Bacterial Prostatitis Incidence Symptoms include: • 20-40 y & >70 • Voiding symptoms: irritative(dysuria) or obstructive(hesitancy, incomplete voiding, weak stream) Dx with exam • May have suprapubic, rectal, or perineal pain • PE: Tender, warm, swollen/boggy, firm & irregular • Painful ejaculation, hematospermia, and painful defecation possible • Systemic symptoms (fever, chills, Diagnostics nausea, emesis, and malaise)  assess for sepsis • UA , C&S Differential Diagnosis of ABP Benign prostatic hypertrophy Obstructive voiding symptoms, enlarged nontender prostate, negative urine culture Chronic bacterial prostatitis Recurring prostatitis symptoms for at least 3 months, positive urine culture with each episode Chronic pelvic pain syndrome Pain attributed to the prostate with no demonstrable evidence of infection Cystitis Irritative voiding symptoms, normal prostate exam Acute Bacterial Prostatitis Rx • Target the organism! Gram-negative enteric bacteria; GC/CT if sexually active • Antibiotic choice (C) - Ceftriaxone/Doxycycline, Quinolones*, Bactrim - Ciprofloxacin 500 mg bid x 2-4 weeks - Increasing gram negative enteric bacteria with quinolone resistance • Duration of therapy: - 10 to 14 days (mild); - 4 weeks (severe) Causes of LUTS other than BPH Anatomic Medications • Fob, strictures • Anticholinergics, Antihistamines, Infections • Beta agonists, • Prostatitis, urethritis, UTI • Opioids, Inflammatory Conditions • Sympathomimetics, • Chronic pelvic pain syndrome • TCA’s • Prostatitis Neurologic Conditions Malignancy • Neurogenic bladder • Bladder and prostate • Overactive bladder

stem: A 46 yo M presents complaining of worsening erectile dysfunction. He denies history of depression, anxiety, CAD or peripheral vascular disease. His BMI is 32. He exercises 3 times weekly. A recent AM testosterone level was 310 ng/dL (280-1,100 ng/dL). Which of the following is the most appropriate therapy? Erectile Dysfunction = Small Vessel Dz ED is a robust predictor of all-cause mortality & CV events in men • Hazard ratio for mortality = 2.04 • Hazard ratio for CV event = 1.62 “Dose-response” increase with ED severity ED Rx begins with PDE5-Inhibitors • Oral phosphodiesterase-5 (PDE5) inhibitors are 1st line. • NNT = 2.1: (A) • Most effective for men with: DM, spinal cord dysfunction, & antidepressants side effects (A) • Helpful in nerve-sparing prostatectomy (B) • Efficacy & SE’s similar among agents. Drop-out rates  for sildenafil (A) Adverse Effects of PDE-5 Inhibitors • Vision disturbances • Priapism (Caution with trazodone!) • Angina • Sudden, permanent sensorineural hearing loss Other ED Treatments Testosterone Trazodone • Helpful if low T (<12 nmol/L) • May worsen (A) • NNT = 2.1: (A) Fibrates & statins Alprostadil • May worsen; (B) • NNT = 3.5; (A) BMI >30  ED risk Yohimbine • Losing weight improves ED (B) • NNT = 6.4: (A) Vacuum devices (B) Practice Recommendations • First line therapy for ED is oral phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitors (A) • PDE type 5 inhibitors are most effective in the treatment of ED associated with DM, spinal cord injury and antidepressant side effects. (A) • Psychosocial therapy and testosterone supplementation in men with hypogonadism are additional ED therapies (B)

stem: A 49 yo presents with recurrent episodes of hematuria and flank pain. Spiral CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis reveals urolithiasis. She eventually passes the stone into a urine strainer. Analysis of the stone reveals calcium oxalate. Dietary recommendations would include: Kidney Stones – Risk Factors RISK FACTORS SYMPTOMS • Male Gender • Abdominal Pain • Age (to 65) Renal colic • Low urine volume Sudden; not relieved • Situational; Geography Radiating pain to back or groin • Heredity • Hematuria • Diet (high Na) • Medications Meds Associated w/ Stone Formation • Triamterene and the sulfonamides (poor solubility) • Calcium and vitamin D supplements (hypercalciuria) • Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (increases urinary pH, thereby increasing calcium phosphate precipitation) • Indinavir, a protease inhibitor, (urinary crystallization) • NOT HCTZ (Increases Calcium reabsorption) Imaging Options Renal Colic Suspected (based on hx/pe) Pregnant, Gallbladder dz or Gyn Hx of radio-opaque Everyone else *A strategy beginning with cause suspected renal calculi ultrasonography (including point-of-care ultrasonography performed by an * emergency physician) can help many patients avoid CT and its associated radiation. (Level of Evidence = 1b–) Could consider bedside US if not obese Ultrasound X-ray IV Non- Pyelogram If Xray normal, but contrast you still have (only if no CT helical CT concerns for stone. available) http://www.aafp.org/afp/2015/0115/p132a.html Management Options <4 mm ~98% pass on their own in 1-2 wks Analgesia 5-10 mm Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin or >10 mm nifedipine) ~53% pass on their own unlikely to have any benefit Indication: for intervention: Strain urine  evidence of persistent UROLOGY obstruction F/U KUB q 1-2 wks Consult  failure of stone progression (SOR C)  persisting colic Urology if not passed in 2-4 wks ESWL or ureteroscopy RTC signs of sepsis Practice Recommendations •Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are the first choice for pain relief in patients with kidney stones (A) •Alpha blockers are the first choice for medical expulsive therapy in patients with kidney stones. (A) •Patients should increase daily fluid intake to 2.5 to 3 L per day to prevent recurrence of kidney stones. (A) •Thiazides, potassium citrate, or allopurinol should be prescribed after recurrence of calcium stones, even in the absence of metabolic abnormalities. (A) Am Fam Physician.2019Apr15;99(8):490-496.

Tumor • Orchitis and others http://www.aafp.org/afp/2014/0501/p723.html Torsion: Clinical Presentation • Abrupt onset of pain – usually testicular, can be lower abdominal, inguinal • Often < 12 hrs duration • May follow exercise or minor trauma • May awaken from sleep • Cremasteric contraction with nocturnal stimulation in REM • Up to 8% report testicular pain in past • May have N&V Diagnosis – “Time is Testicle” • Ideally -- prompt clinical diagnosis • Imaging: 2ndary to clinical exam • Color doppler – decreased intratesticular flow False + in large hydrocele, hematoma Sens 69-100% and Spec 77-100% Lower sensitivity in low-flow pre-pubertal testes • Nuclear technetium-99 radioisotope scan • Show testicular perfusion • 30-min procedure time • Sens and spec 97-100% Management Detorsion within 6hr = 100% viability • Within 12-24 hrs = 20 – 50 % viability • After 24 hrs = 0 - 10% viability Surgical detorsion and orchiopexy if viable • Contralateral exploration and fixation if bell-clapper deformity Orchiectomy if non-viable testicle Never delay surgery on assumption of nonviability, as prolonged symptoms can represent periods of intermittent torsion Epididymitis • Inflammation of epididymis • Subacute onset pain, swelling localized to epididymis, duration of days • With time, swelling and pain less localized • Testis has normal vertical lie • Systemic signs of infection • IncWBC and CRP, fever + in 95% • Cremastericreflex preserved • Urinary complaints: discharge/dysuria PPV 80% Epididymitis Treatment Sexually active treat with ceftriaxone/doxycycline or ofloxacin Pre-pubertal boys • Treat for co-existing UTI if present • Symptomatic Tx with NSAIDs, rest • Referral all to GU for studies to rule out VUR, post urethral valves, duplications Negative culture has 100% NPV for anomaly
